## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the human body temperature regulation system as a closed-loop control system. Identify the reference input, controller, actuator, plant, and sensor in this system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The human body temperature regulation is a **closed-loop negative feedback control system** that maintains core body temperature at approximately **37 °C** (set point) despite variations in ambient temperature.

- **Reference Input:** Hypothalamic set point of 37 °C
- **Controller:** Hypothalamus (preoptic area) acting as the integrating comparator
- **Actuator:** Skeletal muscles (shivering), sweat glands, and skin blood vessels (vasoconstriction/dilation)
- **Plant:** The human body thermal mass (modelled with thermal capacitance and resistance)
- **Sensor:** Thermoreceptors in the skin (peripheral) and hypothalamus (central)
- **Disturbance:** Hot or cold ambient environment, fever, exercise

When core temperature rises, thermoreceptors send signals to the hypothalamus, which initiates sweating and vasodilation, losing heat. When temperature falls, it triggers shivering and vasoconstriction, generating and retaining heat. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Compare the spring-mass-damper mechanical system and the series RLC electrical circuit. Show that their governing differential equations are mathematically analogous, and identify the analogous quantities.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The governing equations are:

| Mechanical (Translational) | Electrical (Series RLC) |
|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| $M \ddot{x} + B \dot{x} + K x = F(t)$ | $L \ddot{q} + R \dot{q} + \dfrac{1}{C} q = v(t)$ |

Both have the form $a\,\ddot{y} + b\,\dot{y} + c\,y = \text{input}(t)$, a second-order linear ODE.

**Analogous quantities (Force-Voltage analogy):**
- Mass $M$ ↔ Inductance $L$
- Damper $B$ ↔ Resistance $R$
- Spring constant $K$ ↔ Inverse Capacitance $1/C$
- Force $F(t)$ ↔ Voltage $v(t)$
- Velocity $\dot{x}$ ↔ Current $i(t)$
- Displacement $x$ ↔ Charge $q(t)$ **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of an armature-controlled DC motor whose armature circuit has resistance $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$ and inductance $L_a = 0.02\ \text{H}$. The motor constants are $K_t = K_b = 0.1\ \text{N·m/A}$ and V·s/rad. The mechanical load has $J = 0.02\ \text{kg·m}^2$ and $B = 0.005\ \text{N·m·s/rad}$. Find the transfer function $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **baroreflex control of blood pressure** in the human body. Identify the equivalent control-system elements and discuss what happens during haemorrhage. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Write the Armature Circuit KVL:**

$$v_a(t) = R_a\,i_a(t) + L_a\,\frac{di_a}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

**Step 2 — Express Back-EMF:**

$$e_b(t) = K_b\,\omega(t) = K_b\,\frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Write the Mechanical Equation:**

$$J\,\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B\,\frac{d\theta}{dt} = T_m(t) = K_t\,i_a(t)$$

**Step 4 — Take Laplace Transforms:**

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a\,s)\,I_a(s) + K_b\,s\,\Theta(s) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$(J\,s + B)\,s\,\Theta(s) = K_t\,I_a(s) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve (ii) for $I_a(s)$:**

$$I_a(s) = \frac{(J\,s + B)\,s\,\Theta(s)}{K_t}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Into (i):**

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a\,s)\,\frac{(J\,s + B)\,s\,\Theta(s)}{K_t} + K_b\,s\,\Theta(s)$$

**Step 7 — Factor $\Theta(s)$:**

$$V_a(s) = s\,\Theta(s)\,\left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a\,s)(J\,s + B) + K_b\,K_t}{K_t} \right]$$

**Step 8 — Plug in Numerical Values:**

Numerator expansion:
$(0.5 + 0.02\,s)(0.02\,s + 0.005) = 0.0025 + 0.01\,s + 0.0001\,s^2 + 0.0004\,s$

$= 0.0001\,s^2 + 0.0104\,s + 0.0025$

Adding $K_b K_t = 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01$:

$= 0.0001\,s^2 + 0.0104\,s + 0.0125$

Dividing by $K_t = 0.1$:

$= 0.001\,s^2 + 0.104\,s + 0.125$

**Step 9 — Final Transfer Function:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{1}{s\,(0.001\,s^{2} + 0.104\,s + 0.125)}}$$

This is a **third-order system** with one pole at the origin (integrator behaviour) and two real or complex poles from the quadratic factor. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.3) — Redrawn in Text Form:**

$$\text{Set BP} \rightarrow \text{Brainstem Comparator} \rightarrow \text{Sympathetic/Vagal Nerves} \rightarrow \text{Heart + Arterioles (Plant)} \rightarrow \text{Actual BP} \rightarrow \text{Baroreceptors (Sensor)} \rightarrow \text{back to Comparator}$$

**Control System Element Mapping:**

| Control System Term | Physiological Counterpart |
|---------------------|----------------------------|
| Reference Input | Desired blood pressure (~120/80 mmHg) set in the brainstem |
| Summing Junction | Nucleus Tractus Solitarius (NTS) in medulla |
| Controller | Cardiovascular centre in medulla oblongata |
| Actuator | Autonomic nervous system (vagus + sympathetic) acting on heart and smooth muscle |
| Plant | Heart (cardiac output) + systemic arterioles (peripheral resistance) |
| Sensor | Baroreceptors in carotid sinus and aortic arch |
| Disturbance | Stress, haemorrhage, postural change, exercise |
| Output | Actual arterial blood pressure |

**Response to Haemorrhage (Disturbance Rejection):**
When blood is lost, arterial pressure falls. The baroreceptors detect this drop and reduce their firing rate. The brainstem responds by:
- **Increasing sympathetic outflow** → heart rate ↑, contractility ↑, vasoconstriction of skin/gut
- **Decreasing vagal (parasympathetic) tone** → heart rate further ↑

This restores blood pressure toward the set point via **negative feedback**. The system fails only if blood loss exceeds the corrective capacity (e.g., > 30% blood volume), leading to hypovolaemic shock. **(7 marks)**

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Restyled for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A thermal system consists of a furnace with thermal capacitance $C = 200\ \text{J/°C}$ connected to the environment through thermal resistance $R = 0.5\ \text{°C/W}$. The ambient temperature is $T_{env} = 25\ \text{°C}$. The heater supplies heat $q(t)$ as the control input. Derive the transfer function $\dfrac{T(s)}{Q(s)}$ for the furnace temperature, and find the steady-state temperature for a constant heat input of $q = 500\ \text{W}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the **insulin–glucagon regulation of blood glucose** as a closed-loop control system. Discuss how a Type-1 diabetic patient differs from this healthy model and what control-engineering interventions are used. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Energy Balance Equation:**

$$C\,\frac{dT}{dt} = q(t) - \frac{T(t) - T_{env}}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$200\,\frac{dT}{dt} = q(t) - \frac{T - 25}{0.5} = q(t) - 2\,(T - 25)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$200\,\frac{dT}{dt} + 2\,T = q(t) + 50$$

**Step 4 — Linearise About Steady State:**
At steady state, $dT/dt = 0$:
$2\,T_{ss} = q_{ss} + 50$, hence $T_{ss} = \dfrac{q_{ss} + 50}{2}$

**Step 5 — Subtract Steady State from Dynamic Equation:**

$$200\,\frac{d\tilde{T}}{dt} + 2\,\tilde{T} = \tilde{q}(t)$$

**Step 6 — Laplace Transform:**

$$(200\,s + 2)\,\tilde{T}(s) = \tilde{Q}(s)$$

**Step 7 — Transfer Function:**

$$\frac{\tilde{T}(s)}{\tilde{Q}(s)} = \frac{1}{200\,s + 2} = \frac{0.5}{100\,s + 1}$$

**Step 8 — Steady-State Temperature for $q = 500\ \text{W}$:**

$$T_{ss} = \frac{500 + 50}{2} = \frac{550}{2} = 275\ \text{°C}$$

**Step 9 — Time Constant:**

$$\tau = RC = 200 \times 0.5 = 100\ \text{seconds} \approx 1.67\ \text{minutes}$$

This means the furnace reaches 63.2% of its final temperature in about 100 seconds. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram in Text Form:**

$$\text{Meal intake} \rightarrow \text{Blood Glucose (Plant)} \rightarrow \text{Glucose Concentration} \rightarrow \text{Pancreatic Sensors} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Insulin/Glucagon} \rightarrow \text{back to Blood Glucose}$$

**Element Mapping:**

| Control Term | Physiological Counterpart |
|--------------|------------------------------|
| Reference Input | $G_{ref} = 90\ \text{mg/dL}$ |
| Plant | Blood glucose concentration |
| Disturbance | Meal (carbohydrate intake), exercise, stress hormones |
| Sensor | Glucose sensors in pancreatic $\beta$-cells |
| Controller | Pancreatic $\alpha$ and $\beta$ cells (secrete glucagon and insulin respectively) |
| Actuator | Liver, muscle, and adipose tissue (cellular glucose uptake) |
| Feedback | Blood glucose level itself (negative feedback) |

**Type-1 Diabetes — Control Engineering Perspective:**

In Type-1 diabetes, the pancreatic $\beta$-cells are destroyed (autoimmune), so the **controller fails to produce insulin**. The closed loop becomes an **open-loop** (or impaired closed loop) and glucose rises dangerously after meals (hyperglycaemia) and may fall too low if insulin is over-administered (hypoglycaemia).

**Control-Engineering Interventions:**

1. **Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM)** — acts as the sensor
2. **Insulin Pump** — acts as the actuator
3. **Control Algorithm (PID / Model Predictive Control)** — runs on a smartphone or pump processor
4. **Artificial Pancreas** — the closed-loop implementation of the above three

This is a **real-world biomedical control system** that has revolutionised diabetes care. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Force-Voltage and Force-Current analogies** — Examiners routinely award 1–2 marks for correctly identifying the analogy type. Mixing them is a fatal mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting the pole at the origin in DC motor transfer function** — A DC motor position transfer function has a $1/s$ factor; velocity does not. Examiners explicitly check for this.
> 3. **In physiological systems, students write "feedback" generically** — Be specific: identify the **sensor type** (baroreceptor, thermoreceptor, chemoreceptor), the **controller** (hypothalamus, brainstem, pancreas), and the **actuator** (muscle, gland, organ). Vague answers get zero.
> 4. **Steady-state calculation in thermal systems** — Students forget to add the ambient term. Always show the linearisation step.
> 5. **Block diagram arrows and signs** — The summing junction is $+$ for reference and $-$ for feedback (negative feedback). Reversing signs gives the wrong closed-loop transfer function.
> 6. **Initial conditions in Laplace transform** — Always state "assuming zero initial conditions" explicitly to earn full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Examples):**

- ☐ **Every example is a closed-loop negative feedback system** — motor, furnace, body, or car.
- ☐ **Five-block skeleton:** Reference → Comparator → Controller → Actuator → Plant → Sensor → back to Comparator.
- ☐ **Mechanical (translational):** $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F(t)$.
- ☐ **Mechanical (rotational):** $J\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + K\theta = T(t)$.
- ☐ **Electrical (series RLC):** $L\ddot{q} + R\dot{q} + q/C = v(t)$.
- ☐ **Thermal:** $C\dot{T} = q - (T - T_{env})/R$.
- ☐ **DC motor position transfer function has a $1/s$ integrator** — three poles in total.
- ☐ **Body temperature:** Hypothalamus = controller, skin/hypothalamic receptors = sensor, muscles/sweat glands = actuator, set point = 37 °C.
- ☐ **Blood glucose:** Pancreas = controller, liver/muscle = actuator, $\beta$-cells = sensor, 90 mg/dL = set point.
- ☐ **Baroreflex:** Carotid/aortic baroreceptors = sensor, brainstem = controller, vagus/sympathetic = actuator, 120/80 mmHg = set point.
- ☐ **Force-Voltage analogy:** $M \leftrightarrow L$, $B \leftrightarrow R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/C$, $F \leftrightarrow V$, $v \leftrightarrow i$, $x \leftrightarrow q$.
- ☐ **Force-Current analogy:** $M \leftrightarrow C$, $B \leftrightarrow 1/R$, $K \leftrightarrow 1/L$, $F \leftrightarrow i$, $v \leftrightarrow v$, $x \leftrightarrow \int v\,dt$.
- ☐ **Thermal-electrical analogy:** Thermal $C$ ↔ electrical $C$, thermal $R$ ↔ electrical $R$, temperature ↔ voltage, heat flow ↔ current.
- ☐ **Physiological systems are non-linear, time-varying, multi-input multi-output (MIMO), and adaptive** — a much richer class than textbook motors.
- ☐ **The human body is a "control engineer's paradise"** — every organ is a controlled subsystem.
- ☐ **Always linearise before applying Laplace transforms** to physiological models.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Cross-domain analogy questions and physiological block-diagram interpretation are asked almost every semester.

---

**End of Module 1 — Examples (Including Physiological Control Systems) Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Loop and Closed Loop Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT521 - CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Loop and Closed Loop Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 : Open Loop and Closed Loop Control Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Control Systems

A **Control System** is a collection of physical components arranged or interconnected in such a way that it commands, directs, or regulates itself or another system to achieve a desired output with a specified performance.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Control Systems Engineering (PEBMT521) under Outcome-Based Education (OBE) lays the foundation for understanding how engineering systems maintain accuracy, stability, and responsiveness in real-time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> The first module introduces the concept of open-loop and closed-loop systems, their block diagram representation, transfer functions, and the fundamental role of feedback in automatic control.

---

### 1.2 Open-Loop Control System (OLCS)

#### Formal Definition
An **Open-Loop Control System** is a type of control system in which the control action is completely independent of the desired output. There is **no feedback path** from the output to the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *"A system in which the output has no effect upon the control signal is called an Open-Loop Control System."*

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **hand dryer** in a public restroom. You press the button for a fixed time (say 30 seconds), and it runs for exactly that duration regardless of whether your hands are still wet or already dry. The system does not "sense" the result of its action — it simply executes the command and stops.

Another classic example is a **toaster**. You set the timer for 2 minutes, and the heating coil runs for the full 2 minutes even if the bread is already burnt. The output (toast color) does not influence the input (timer).

The **driving signal** (input) is the only factor that determines the **controlled variable** (output).

---

### 1.3 Closed-Loop Control System (CLCS)

#### Formal Definition
A **Closed-Loop Control System** is one in which the control action depends on the desired output in some way, i.e., the system has a **feedback mechanism** that continuously monitors the output and compares it with the reference input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *"A system in which the control action is dependent on the output in some way is called a Closed-Loop Control System, also known as a Feedback Control System."*

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Consider a **human driving a car**. The driver sees the road, compares the car's position with the lane (reference), and adjusts the steering wheel. The action depends on the **error** between the desired and actual position. This is a perfect example of a closed-loop biological system.

A more technical example is a **room temperature controller (AC with thermostat)**. The thermostat senses the current room temperature, compares it with the set point, and switches the compressor ON or OFF. The output (room temperature) **directly influences** the input (compressor state).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Feedback:**
> The process by which a portion of the output is returned to the input to modify the system's behavior is called **Feedback**. The most common type is **Negative Feedback**, which reduces the error between desired and actual output.

---

### 1.4 Essential Terminology (KTU Board Definition Style)

- **Plant:** The physical object or system to be controlled (e.g., motor, furnace, antenna).
- **Process:** The operation to be controlled (e.g., chemical, manufacturing).
- **Reference Input (r(t)):** The desired value of the output.
- **Control Signal / Manipulated Variable (u(t)):** The signal applied to the plant to drive it.
- **Controlled Output (y(t) or c(t)):** The actual response of the system.
- **Error Signal (e(t)):** The difference between reference input and feedback signal. Mathematically, $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$, where $b(t)$ is the feedback signal.
- **Controller:** The element that processes the error signal and generates the control action.
- **Feedback Element:** A sensor or transducer that measures the output and produces a feedback signal.
- **Disturbance / Load:** An unwanted external signal that affects the output (denoted as $d(t)$).
- **Actuator:** A device that converts the controller's signal into a physical action on the plant.

---

### 1.5 Classification of Control Systems (For KTU 2-Mark Quick Answers)

> [!NOTE]
> Based on the **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 syllabus**, control systems are classified as:
> 1. **Open-Loop and Closed-Loop** (based on feedback)
> 2. **Continuous-Time and Discrete-Time** (based on signal type)
> 3. **Linear and Non-Linear** (based on mathematical behavior)
> 4. **Time-Invariant and Time-Variant** (based on parameter dependency)
> 5. **Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) and Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO)**
> 6. **Lumped and Distributed Parameter** (based on spatial distribution)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Control System

The fundamental parts of any control system are:

1. **Input (Reference / Desired Value):** The target value at which the system is expected to operate.
2. **Controller / Compensator:** The brain of the system. It accepts the error signal and produces a control signal.
3. **Plant (Process to be controlled):** The actual system being regulated.
4. **Sensor / Feedback Element:** Measures the actual output and converts it to a comparable form.
5. **Comparator (Summing Junction):** Performs the mathematical subtraction between reference and feedback to generate the error signal.
6. **Actuator:** The final control element that physically affects the plant.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Representation

### 2.2.1 Open-Loop Transfer Function

The control action is solely determined by the reference input. The output $C(s)$ depends only on the input $R(s)$ through a forward path transfer function $G(s)$.

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

The overall transfer function (TF) is:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = G(s)$$

### 2.2.2 Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Unity Negative Feedback)

In a closed-loop system, the output is fed back to a summing junction where it is subtracted from the reference input.

Let:
- $R(s)$ = Reference input
- $E(s)$ = Error signal
- $C(s)$ = Output
- $B(s)$ = Feedback signal
- $G(s)$ = Forward path transfer function
- $H(s)$ = Feedback path transfer function

**Step 1 — Error at the summing junction:**

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2 — Feedback signal relation:**

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3 — Output relation:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 3:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange:**

$$C(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

$$C(s) \cdot \left[1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)\right] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 6 — Final Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}}$$

For **Unity Feedback** ($H(s) = 1$):

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The expression $1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)$ is called the Characteristic Equation** when set to zero. It determines the **stability** of the closed-loop system. KTU questions often ask to find the stability condition from this expression.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Comparison: Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop (KTU Board Tabular Answers)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following comparison is a HIGH-YIELD content for KTU 2-mark and 5-mark questions.** It appears in almost every past KTU paper.

| **Property** | **Open-Loop System** | **Closed-Loop System** |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | Absent | Present (negative feedback) |
| **Accuracy** | Poor — depends on calibration | High — error is continuously corrected |
| **Sensitivity to Disturbance** | Very High | Very Low |
| **Stability** | Generally stable (no oscillation issues) | Can become unstable if not designed properly |
| **Bandwidth** | Wide | Reduced due to feedback |
| **Complexity & Cost** | Simple, cheap | Complex, expensive |
| **Response Speed** | Fast (no feedback delay) | Slightly slower (feedback processing) |
| **Reliability** | Low | High |
| **Error Correction** | Cannot self-correct | Self-correcting |
| **Examples** | Toaster, Hand dryer, Traffic light, Washing machine (timer-based) | AC thermostat, Cruise control, DC motor speed control, Autopilot, Human body temperature regulation |
| **Sensitivity to Parameter Variation** | High | Reduced by a factor of $1 + GH$ |
| **Output for Change in Load** | Output changes significantly | Output remains near reference |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Open-Loop TF | $C(s) / R(s) = G(s)$ | Output / Input |
| Closed-Loop TF (Negative Feedback) | $C(s) / R(s) = G(s) / [1 + G(s)H(s)]$ | With feedback $H(s)$ |
| Closed-Loop TF (Positive Feedback) | $C(s) / R(s) = G(s) / [1 - G(s)H(s)]$ | Positive feedback case |
| Error Signal | $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ | Difference at summing junction |
| Feedback Signal | $B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$ | Output fed back via sensor |
| Sensitivity of Closed-Loop TF to $G$ | $S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | KTU frequently tests this |
| Sensitivity of Open-Loop TF to $G$ | $S_G^T = 1$ | For open loop, sensitivity is unity |
| Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(s) \cdot H(s) = 0$ | Determines closed-loop stability |
| Unity Feedback | $H(s) = 1$ | Special case simplification |
| Effect of Feedback on Gain | $G_{\text{CL}} = G / (1+GH)$ | Reduced by factor $(1+GH)$ |
| Effect of Feedback on Disturbance | Reduced by $(1 + G_1 G_2 H)$ | When disturbance enters inside loop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Students:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in place of the pipe symbol `|` inside any cell of a markdown table to avoid breaking the rendering.

---

## 2.5 Why Use Feedback? The Engineering Utility

The introduction of negative feedback to a system, despite reducing the overall gain, brings several **engineering advantages** that make it indispensable in modern control engineering:

1. **Reduction of Sensitivity to Parameter Variations:** Aging, temperature, and manufacturing tolerances cause plant parameters to drift. Feedback reduces this drift's effect on the output.
2. **Improvement of Steady-State Accuracy:** Feedback drives the steady-state error toward zero.
3. **Attenuation of Disturbances:** Internal and external disturbances are reduced by the loop gain.
4. **Control of System Dynamics:** Bandwidth, transient response, and damping can be shaped.
5. **Linearization Effect:** Feedback can make a non-linear system behave more linearly over a wider operating range.

**Real-world engineering fields where this concept is foundational:**
- **Aerospace:** Aircraft autopilot, missile guidance
- **Automotive:** Cruise control, ABS braking, active suspension
- **Robotics:** Joint position control, gripper force control
- **Process Industries:** Temperature, pressure, level, and flow control loops
- **Power Systems:** Voltage and frequency regulation
- **Biomedical:** Drug infusion pumps, artificial pancreas
- **Electronics:** Phase-Locked Loops (PLL) in communication systems

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function with Non-Unity Feedback

The most fundamental derivation in Module 1 is the closed-loop transfer function. We will derive it from first principles using **block diagram algebra**.

### Setup

Consider a standard **single-loop negative feedback system** with:
- Forward path TF: $G(s)$
- Feedback path TF: $H(s)$
- Reference input: $R(s)$
- Output: $C(s)$
- Error signal: $E(s)$
- Feedback signal: $B(s)$

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Error signal at the summing junction:**

The summing junction subtracts the feedback signal from the reference input. The convention is negative feedback.

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2 — Feedback signal generation:**

The feedback element with TF $H(s)$ acts on the output:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3 — Forward path equation:**

The controller and plant in series produce the output from the error:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute this $E(s)$ into Step 3:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 7 — Collect all $C(s)$ terms on the left side:**

$$C(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 8 — Factor out $C(s)$ on the left side:**

$$C(s) \cdot \left[1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)\right] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 9 — Isolate the ratio $C(s) / R(s)$:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

This is the **canonical closed-loop transfer function** for a single-loop negative feedback system.

---

### Special Case 1: Unity Negative Feedback

If $H(s) = 1$ (direct feedback, no sensor dynamics), the formula reduces to:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

### Special Case 2: Positive Feedback

If the summing junction adds the feedback signal instead of subtracting, the denominator changes sign:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A common mistake is to write the positive feedback formula as $G / (1 + GH)$. Always re-check the sign of the summing junction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Feedback on Sensitivity

Sensitivity is a key KTU concept that quantifies how much the closed-loop transfer function $T(s)$ changes when the plant $G(s)$ varies.

### Definition of Sensitivity

$$S_G^T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{\partial T}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{G}{T}$$

This represents the **fractional change in $T$ for a given fractional change in $G$**.

### Derivation for Closed-Loop System

Closed-loop transfer function:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

Compute $\partial T / \partial G$:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1 + GH) \cdot 1 - G \cdot H}{(1 + GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2}$$

Substitute into the sensitivity definition:

$$S_G^T = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2} \cdot \frac{G}{G / (1 + GH)}$$

$$S_G^T = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2} \cdot (1 + GH)$$

$$S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Result:** Sensitivity of a closed-loop system to plant variations is reduced by a factor of $(1 + GH)$ compared to an open-loop system (where $S_G^T = 1$). This is the principal reason feedback is used.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Feedback on Disturbance Rejection

Consider a system where a disturbance $D(s)$ enters at an intermediate point in the forward path. Let the plant be split into $G_1(s)$ (before disturbance) and $G_2(s)$ (after disturbance).

The output in the absence of reference input ($R(s) = 0$) due to disturbance is:

$$C_D(s) = \frac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot H(s)} \cdot D(s)$$

The output due to reference input alone ($D(s) = 0$):

$$C_R(s) = \frac{G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot H(s)} \cdot R(s)$$

By superposition:

$$C(s) = C_R(s) + C_D(s)$$

**Disturbance rejection ratio** (output due to disturbance with feedback divided by output due to disturbance without feedback):

$$\frac{C_D^{\text{closed}}}{C_D^{\text{open}}} = \frac{1}{1 + G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The disturbance effect is reduced by the factor $(1 + G_1 G_2 H)$. Increasing loop gain improves disturbance rejection.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Open vs Closed Loop

| **Application Scenario** | **Preferred System** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| Low-cost, low-accuracy appliances (toaster) | Open-Loop | Cost is critical; no precision required |
| High-precision motor speed control | Closed-Loop | Load variations must not affect speed |
| High-power, fast-acting systems | Open-Loop | Stability of feedback loop is hard to guarantee |
| Process industries (chemical plants) | Closed-Loop | Long-term drift must be auto-corrected |
| Systems with unpredictable disturbances | Closed-Loop | Feedback attenuates unknown disturbances |
| Time-critical simple actuation (doorbell) | Open-Loop | Simplicity and reliability |
| Aerospace guidance | Closed-Loop | Required accuracy and robustness |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Open-Loop Control System

```mermaid
graph LR
  inputNode[Reference Input R s] --> sumNode[Summing Junction]
  sumNode --> controllerNode[Controller Gc s]
  controllerNode --> actuatorNode[Actuator]
  actuatorNode --> plantNode[Plant G s]
  plantNode --> outputNode[Output C s]
  distNode1[Disturbance D s] -.-> plantNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In an open-loop system, the disturbance $D(s)$ directly alters the output $C(s)$ with no way for the system to detect or correct it. The reference input is the **only** signal influencing the controller.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Control System (Negative Feedback)

```mermaid
graph LR
  refNode[Reference Input R s] --> sumNode[Summing Junction]
  fbNode[Feedback Signal B s] --> sumNode
  sumNode --> errNode[Error E s]
  errNode --> ctrlNode[Controller Gc s]
  ctrlNode --> actNode[Actuator]
  actNode --> plantNode[Plant G s]
  plantNode --> outNode[Output C s]
  plantNode --> sensorNode[Feedback Element H s]
  sensorNode --> fbNode
  distNode1[Disturbance D s] -.-> plantNode
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The signal flows in a **loop** (closed path). The summing junction performs subtraction ($R - B$) to produce the error $E(s)$. The feedback path $H(s)$ senses the output and returns a proportionate signal $B(s)$ to the input side.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Detailed Closed-Loop with Disturbance Path

```mermaid
graph LR
  R[Reference Input] --> SUM1((+))
  FB[Feedback B s] --> SUM1
  SUM1 --> ERR[Error E s]
  ERR --> GC[Controller Gc]
  GC --> U[Control Signal U s]
  U --> G1[Plant Part 1 G1 s]
  G1 --> SUM2((+))
  DIST[Disturbance D s] --> SUM2
  SUM2 --> G2[Plant Part 2 G2 s]
  G2 --> C[Output C s]
  C --> H[Feedback Sensor H s]
  H --> FB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The disturbance $D(s)$ enters **inside** the loop (between $G_1$ and $G_2$). Because it is inside the feedback path, the system can sense its effect through the output and correct for it. If the disturbance entered at the input (before $G_1$), the feedback would also try to reject it, but the loop gain would be different.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram Reduction Rules (Quick Reference)

| **Rule** | **Original Form** | **Reduced Form** |
|---|---|---|
| Cascaded blocks | $G_1 \rightarrow G_2$ | $G_1 \cdot G_2$ |
| Parallel blocks (same direction) | $G_1$ and $G_2$ parallel | $G_1 + G_2$ |
| Moving a block past a summing junction | Adds branch in front | Equivalent rearrangement |
| Moving a block past a pickoff point | Adds reciprocal block | Equivalent rearrangement |
| Feedback loop reduction | $G$ in forward, $H$ in feedback | $G / (1 \pm GH)$ |
| Unity feedback | $H = 1$ | $G / (1 \pm G)$ |

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Control Systems with suitable examples.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
| **Criterion** | **Open-Loop** | **Closed-Loop** |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low and depends on calibration | High and self-correcting |
| Disturbance Handling | No compensation | Automatic compensation |
| Example | Electric hand dryer, traffic light | AC thermostat, cruise control |
| Cost & Complexity | Simple and cheap | Complex and costly |
| Stability | Inherently stable | Requires careful design for stability |

**Examples:**
- **Open-Loop:** Toaster with a mechanical timer. The heating element runs for a fixed duration regardless of bread color.
- **Closed-Loop:** Room air conditioner with a thermostat. The temperature sensor continuously measures room temperature and switches the compressor ON/OFF.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "closed-loop is always better." This is **not true** for high-power, fast-acting systems where feedback-induced instability is a serious risk. Mention both advantages **and** limitations to score full marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "feedback" in a control system. Explain the difference between positive and negative feedback.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
**Definition:** Feedback is the process by which a portion of the output signal of a system is returned to the input side to influence the system's subsequent behavior.

**Positive Feedback:** The feedback signal is **added** to the reference input at the summing junction. It **reinforces** the input, increases the loop gain, and can drive the system toward instability or oscillation.

$$E(s) = R(s) + B(s), \quad \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$$

**Negative Feedback:** The feedback signal is **subtracted** from the reference input. It **opposes** the input, reduces error, improves stability, and is the standard configuration in most engineering control systems.

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s), \quad \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Example of Positive Feedback:** Regenerative receiver circuits, oscillator circuits (Barkhausen criterion).
**Example of Negative Feedback:** Operational amplifiers, cruise control in automobiles, body temperature regulation.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Students answer **one full question** from the internal choice pair. The valuation follows strict step-marking.

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a single-loop negative feedback control system with forward path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback path transfer function $H(s)$. Hence, obtain the transfer function for a unity negative feedback system.**

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1 — Define the system signals** (1 mark):
- Reference input $R(s)$, output $C(s)$, error $E(s)$, feedback $B(s)$
- Forward path: $G(s)$, Feedback path: $H(s)$

**Step 2 — Write the summing junction equation** (1 mark):
$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 3 — Write the feedback signal equation** (1 mark):
$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 4 — Write the forward path equation** (1 mark):
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 4 and simplify** (2 marks):
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)\right]$$
$$C(s) \cdot \left[1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)\right] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

**Step 6 — Unity negative feedback** ($H(s) = 1$) (1 mark):
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting the sign in the denominator for positive feedback. The standard KTU convention is **negative feedback** unless otherwise specified. Also, students often skip the algebra expansion step, which costs 2 marks in valuation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a closed-loop system with $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$ and unity negative feedback, determine: (i) the closed-loop transfer function, (ii) the characteristic equation, and (iii) the closed-loop poles.**

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1 — Identify the components** (1 mark):
- $G(s) = 10 / [s(s+2)]$
- $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback)

**Step 2 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function** (2 marks):
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)} = \frac{\frac{10}{s(s+2)}}{1 + \frac{10}{s(s+2)}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(s+2)$:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{10}{s(s+2) + 10} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 2s + 10}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic Equation** (2 marks):
Setting the denominator to zero:
$$s^2 + 2s + 10 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Closed-Loop Poles** (2 marks):
Using the quadratic formula $s = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:
$$s = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 40}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{-36}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm j6}{2}$$

$$s_{1,2} = -1 \pm j3$$

The poles are complex conjugates located in the **left half of the s-plane**, which indicates the system is **stable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Always clearly state the **stability conclusion** after finding the poles. KTU evaluators award an extra mark for explicitly mentioning "left half-plane → stable." Also, never forget to multiply the denominator by $s(s+2)$ to clear the compound fraction.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper] — Internal Alternative

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the effect of negative feedback on the sensitivity, stability, and disturbance rejection of a control system. Derive the sensitivity of a closed-loop transfer function to variations in the plant gain.**

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1 — Effect on Sensitivity** (2 marks):
Sensitivity measures the fractional change in closed-loop TF $T$ to the fractional change in plant $G$. For a closed-loop system:
$$S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

Since $|1 + GH| > 1$ for typical systems, the sensitivity is **less than unity**. This means closed-loop systems are less sensitive to plant variations than open-loop systems (where $S_G^T = 1$).

**Step 2 — Effect on Stability** (2 marks):
Feedback can either improve or degrade stability depending on the loop gain and phase. Properly designed negative feedback improves stability margins, but excessive loop gain can drive poles into the right half-plane, causing instability. The characteristic equation $1 + GH = 0$ must be analyzed (Routh-Hurwitz, Root Locus) to ensure all closed-loop poles have negative real parts.

**Step 3 — Effect on Disturbance Rejection** (1 mark):
A disturbance entering inside the loop is attenuated by the factor $(1 + G_1 G_2 H)$ in the output. Feedback acts as an automatic corrector.

**Step 4 — Derivation of Sensitivity** (2 marks):
$$S_G^T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{G}{T} \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial G}$$

With $T = G / (1 + GH)$:
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2}$$

$$S_G^T = \frac{G}{G / (1+GH)} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} = (1+GH) \cdot \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} = \frac{1}{1 + GH}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked about "sensitivity," students often confuse it with the system's frequency response or noise. Stay strictly on the formal definition: **ratio of fractional change in TF to fractional change in plant.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Control Systems under the following heads: (i) accuracy, (ii) sensitivity to parameter variation, (iii) bandwidth, (iv) stability, (v) cost, (vi) examples. State two cases where open-loop systems are preferred over closed-loop.**

#### Model Answer:

**(i) Accuracy:** (1 mark)
- Open-Loop: Poor accuracy, depends on calibration of components.
- Closed-Loop: High accuracy due to continuous error correction.

**(ii) Sensitivity to Parameter Variation:** (1 mark)
- Open-Loop: $S_G^T = 1$, output changes proportionally with plant variation.
- Closed-Loop: $S_G^T = 1 / (1 + GH)$, much lower sensitivity.

**(iii) Bandwidth:** (1 mark)
- Open-Loop: Wide bandwidth (no feedback-induced limitations).
- Closed-Loop: Reduced bandwidth due to feedback dynamics.

**(iv) Stability:** (1 mark)
- Open-Loop: Inherently stable (no feedback loop to oscillate).
- Closed-Loop: Requires careful design; can become unstable if not properly compensated.

**(v) Cost and Complexity:** (1 mark)
- Open-Loop: Low cost, simple construction, fewer components.
- Closed-Loop: Expensive due to sensors, controllers, and feedback path.

**(vi) Examples:** (1 mark)
- Open-Loop: Toaster, hand dryer, traffic signal, washing machine (timer).
- Closed-Loop: AC thermostat, voltage regulator, cruise control, autopilot.

**Cases where Open-Loop is preferred:** (1 mark)
1. **High-power, fast-acting systems:** Where the response time of feedback would be too slow (e.g., missile launchers, blast furnace control). Feedback-induced instability risk is also a concern at very high power levels.
2. **Simple, low-cost applications:** Where accuracy is not critical and adding sensors/feedback would inflate cost without meaningful performance gain (e.g., household toasters, fan regulators, simple timers).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only disadvantages of open-loop systems. KTU expects a **balanced answer** with clear cases where open-loop is the right engineering choice. Failing to give examples loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Open & Closed-Loop Systems**

- **Open-Loop:** No feedback. $C(s)/R(s) = G(s)$. Cheap, fast, but inaccurate and disturbance-prone. Examples: toaster, dryer, timer-based machines.

- **Closed-Loop:** Has negative feedback. $C(s)/R(s) = G(s) / [1 + G(s)H(s)]$. Accurate, robust, but more complex and potentially unstable. Examples: AC, cruise control, voltage regulator.

- **Negative Feedback** is the standard in control engineering because it:
  - Reduces sensitivity by factor $(1 + GH)$.
  - Attenuates disturbance by factor $(1 + G_1 G_2 H)$.
  - Improves steady-state accuracy.
  - Can linearize non-linear systems.

- **Positive Feedback** is rare in control (it causes oscillation), but used in oscillators.

- **Characteristic Equation** of a closed-loop system: $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. Roots determine stability.

- **Summing Junction** convention: Negative feedback uses $E = R - B$. Positive feedback uses $E = R + B$.

- **Unity Feedback** ($H(s) = 1$) simplification: $C(s)/R(s) = G/(1+G)$.

- **Sensitivity** of closed-loop system = $1/(1+GH)$; of open-loop = $1$.

- **Bandwidth** of closed-loop system is reduced compared to open-loop.

- **Stability Criterion** (preliminary, expanded in later modules): All closed-loop poles must lie in the **left half of the s-plane** (negative real parts) for BIBO stability.

- **Block Diagram Reduction:** Remember the canonical forms for cascaded, parallel, and feedback loops.

- **Mistakes to Avoid in KTU Exam:**
  1. Wrong sign in denominator (positive vs negative feedback).
  2. Skipping the algebra expansion steps in derivation.
  3. Forgetting to multiply through to clear compound fractions.
  4. Not stating stability conclusion explicitly after finding poles.
  5. Confusing sensitivity with frequency response or noise.
  6. Writing only one side of the comparison table (must give both).

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Open Loop and Closed Loop Control Systems.**
> Continue to Module 2 for **Mathematical Modeling of Physical Systems** and **Transfer Function derivation** of mechanical, electrical, and electromechanical systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Control System Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Control System Design Process** is the structured, iterative engineering methodology by which a control engineer transforms a set of **performance specifications** (time-domain and frequency-domain requirements) of a given plant (process/system to be controlled) into a **compensator/controller** (typically transfer function $G_c(s)$ or controller structure such as P, PI, PD, PID, lead, lag, or lead–lag) such that the resulting **closed-loop system** satisfies all the prescribed specifications of stability, transient response, steady-state accuracy, disturbance rejection, and robustness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Examiner's Wording):**
> *Control system design is the art and science of selecting controller structure and parameters so that the closed-loop transfer function $T(s)$ meets the desired specifications on steady-state error $e_{ss}$, peak overshoot $M_p$, settling time $t_s$, rise time $t_r$, gain margin (GM), phase margin (PM), and bandwidth $\omega_b$.*

The complete design cycle (as per KTU Module-1 syllabus) consists of the following six canonical stages:

| Stage | Name | Key Activity |
|:-----:|:-----|:-------------|
| 1 | **Plant Modelling** | Derive mathematical model $G_p(s)$ of the physical system |
| 2 | **Specification Translation** | Convert $\%M_p$, $t_s$, $e_{ss}$, etc. into pole/zero locations or $\omega_n$, $\zeta$ |
| 3 | **Open-Loop Analysis** | Examine uncompensated response (root locus, Bode, Nyquist) |
| 4 | **Compensator Selection** | Choose type (P/PI/PD/PID/Lead/Lag) and topology (cascade/feedback) |
| 5 | **Parameter Tuning** | Use Ziegler–Nichols, root-locus, Bode, or analytical methods |
| 6 | **Validation & Iteration** | Simulate, verify against specs, iterate until convergence |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Driving a Car on a Hilly Road"

Imagine you are driving a car and must maintain a **cruise speed of 60 km/h** (the *desired output* $r(t)$) on a road that goes up and down (the *plant $G_p(s)$* with unknown disturbances).

- **Plant** = The car itself (engine, brakes, mass, drag).
- **Sensor** = The speedometer (feedback element $H(s)$).
- **Controller** = YOU, the driver, with your eyes and brain acting as the compensator $G_c(s)$.
- **Reference Input** = The 60 km/h set on the cruise control.
- **Error** = Difference between 60 km/h and actual speed.
- **Actuator** = Your foot on the accelerator/brake.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** When the road inclines, the car slows down → error increases → you press more accelerator (proportional action). If the error persists, you press harder (integral action eliminates steady-state error). If you overshoot, you release the accelerator quickly (derivative action damps oscillation). The *design process* is exactly this — translating "smooth, fast, stable driving" into mathematical controller equations.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The KTU 2024 examiner **frequently tests** the following performance indices (these are the *yardsticks* by which your design is judged):

- **Damping ratio $\zeta$** — dimensionless, $0 < \zeta < 1$ for underdamped response.
- **Natural frequency $\omega_n$** — in **rad/s**.
- **Steady-state error $e_{ss}$** — dimensionless (or in same units as output).
- **Phase Margin (PM)** — typically **$45°$ to $60°$** for good design.
- **Gain Margin (GM)** — typically **$\geq 6$ dB** (i.e., $\geq 2$).
- **Peak overshoot $M_p$** — typically **$\leq 5\%$ to $20\%$** depending on application.
- **Bandwidth $\omega_b$** — frequency (rad/s) at which $\vert T(j\omega)\vert = -3$ dB.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Step-by-Step Design Philosophy

The control system design process is **not a single equation** — it is a *chain of logical decisions*. Below is the deep theoretical breakdown of every stage.

### Step 1 — Plant Modelling
- Obtain the **transfer function** $G_p(s)$ of the uncontrolled plant.
- Sources: physical laws (Newton, Kirchhoff), system identification, or manufacturer datasheet.
- **Why?** You cannot compensate what you do not understand. The model is the *language* in which the rest of the design is spoken.

### Step 2 — Identify Performance Specifications
Translate real-world expectations into quantitative KTU-friendly metrics:

$$\text{Time Domain: } \{t_r,\, t_s,\, M_p,\, e_{ss}\}$$
$$\text{Frequency Domain: } \{\text{PM},\, \text{GM},\, \omega_b,\, M_r\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Mapping (Very High-Yield for KTU):**
> 
> $$\zeta = \frac{-\ln(M_p/100)}{\sqrt{\pi^2 + \ln^2(M_p/100)}}$$
> 
> $$\omega_n = \frac{4}{\zeta \cdot t_s} \quad \text{(for 2\% settling time)}$$
> 
> $$\omega_n = \frac{1.8}{t_r} \quad \text{(for rise time approximation)}$$

### Step 3 — Decide the Desired Closed-Loop Pole Location
The **dominant poles** of the closed-loop system must lie in a region of the $s$-plane:
- **Real part** $<\sigma = -\frac{4}{t_s}$ (left of the settling-time line).
- **Imaginary part** $\pm j\omega_d = \pm j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ (height determined by $\omega_n$).
- **Angle** $\theta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$ from the negative real axis.

### Step 4 — Open-Loop Compensation
Two KTU-approved approaches:

**(A) Root-Locus Method** (Classical):
- Add poles/zeros of $G_c(s)$ so the root locus **passes through the desired pole** $s_d$.
- Satisfies **angle condition**: $\angle G_c(s_d)G_p(s_d) = -180°$ (mod $360°$).
- Satisfies **magnitude condition**: $\vert G_c(s_d)G_p(s_d)\vert = 1$ (for unity feedback).

**(B) Frequency-Response Method** (Bode/Nyquist):
- Add lead network to **boost PM**, lag network to **reduce steady-state error**.
- Cascade compensation via $G_c(s)$.

### Step 5 — Realize the Compensator
- Convert algebraic compensator $G_c(s)$ into **operational-amplifier circuits** (electronic realization) or **software code** (microcontroller/DSP).
- Active realizations: inverting amplifier, integrator, differentiator using op-amps.

### Step 6 — Validate and Iterate
- Use **MATLAB/Simulink** (or KTU-approved tools) to simulate step response and Bode plot.
- If $M_p > 20\%$ or PM $< 45°$, **return to Step 4** and add more compensation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most important high-yield summary** for the Module-1 question on Design Process. Memorize the mappings below.

| # | Performance Spec | Formula | Standard Target | Units |
|:-:|:-----------------|:--------|:----------------|:------|
| 1 | Damping ratio from $M_p$ | $\zeta = \dfrac{-\ln(M_p/100)}{\sqrt{\pi^2 + \ln^2(M_p/100)}}$ | $0.4$ to $0.8$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Settling time (2% band) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | Depends on app | seconds (s) |
| 3 | Settling time (5% band) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{3}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | — | s |
| 4 | Rise time | $t_r \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | — | s |
| 5 | Peak time | $t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_d} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | — | s |
| 6 | Steady-state error (step) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$, $K_p = \lim_{s\to 0}G_c(s)G_p(s)$ | $< 0.05$ (5%) | — |
| 7 | Steady-state error (ramp) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$, $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0}sG_c(s)G_p(s)$ | App dependent | — |
| 8 | Steady-state error (parabola) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a}$, $K_a = \lim_{s\to 0}s^2 G_c(s)G_p(s)$ | — | — |
| 9 | Phase margin (approx) | $\text{PM} \approx \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta}{\sqrt{\sqrt{1+4\zeta^4}-2\zeta^2}}\right)$ | $45°$ to $60°$ | degrees |
| 10 | Resonant peak $M_r$ | $M_r = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ for $0 \le \zeta \le 0.707$ | $\leq 1.5$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Resonant frequency | $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ | — | rad/s |
| 12 | Bandwidth | $\omega_b \approx \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2+\sqrt{4\zeta^4-4\zeta^2+2}}$ | — | rad/s |
| 13 | Lead compensator zero | $z_c = -\dfrac{1}{T}$ | — | rad/s |
| 14 | Lead compensator pole | $p_c = -\dfrac{1}{\alpha T}$, $\alpha < 1$ | — | rad/s |
| 15 | Lag compensator zero | $z_c = -\dfrac{1}{T}$ | — | rad/s |
| 16 | Lag compensator pole | $p_c = -\dfrac{1}{\beta T}$, $\beta > 1$ | — | rad/s |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The control system design process is the **backbone of every modern automation industry**:

- **Aerospace:** Autopilot design (Boeing 777 uses $H_\infty$ design; classical lead-lag still used in launch vehicles).
- **Automotive:** Cruise control, anti-lock braking (ABS), electronic stability program (ESP).
- **Robotics:** Joint position/torque control of robotic arms (PI control in industrial robots).
- **Process Industry:** PID controllers in chemical plants, refineries, paper mills — over **95% of all industrial controllers are PID** (per a Honeywell survey).
- **Power Systems:** Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR), Load Frequency Control (LFC).
- **Biomedical:** Insulin pumps, artificial hearts, prosthetics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this process iterative?** Because no analytical formula can simultaneously satisfy *all* specifications (e.g., high bandwidth conflicts with noise rejection). The engineer *trades off* one specification against another — this is the essence of the **design process**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Damping Ratio from Peak Overshoot

The **step response** of a standard second-order system (the *universal design template* for KTU) is given by:

$$Y(s) = \frac{\omega_n^2}{s(s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)}$$

The **inverse Laplace transform** yields the time-domain output for $0 < \zeta < 1$:

$$y(t) = 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\, \sin\!\left(\omega_d t + \phi\right)$$

where

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}, \qquad \phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$$

The **peak overshoot** $M_p$ occurs at the **peak time** $t_p$, where the derivative $\frac{dy}{dt} = 0$. Differentiating $y(t)$ and setting the result to zero:

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{\omega_n}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[\zeta\sin(\omega_d t + \phi) - \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}\cos(\omega_d t + \phi)\right] = 0$$

The bracketed term must vanish:

$$\zeta\sin(\omega_d t_p + \phi) = \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}\cos(\omega_d t_p + \phi)$$

Using the identity $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$ with $A = \omega_d t_p$ and $B = \phi$:

$$\tan(\omega_d t_p + \phi) = \frac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}{\zeta}$$

Since $\tan\phi = \frac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}{\zeta}$, we conclude $\omega_d t_p + \phi = \pi$ (the **first positive extremum**), giving:

$$t_p = \frac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_d} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

Substituting $t = t_p$ into $y(t)$ and simplifying (after algebraic reduction using $\sin\pi = 0$ and $\cos\pi = -1$):

$$y(t_p) = 1 + e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_p}$$

The **peak overshoot** is therefore:

$$M_p = y(t_p) - 1 = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_p} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\zeta\pi}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right)$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln M_p = -\frac{\zeta\pi}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\ln^2 M_p = \frac{\zeta^2 \pi^2}{1-\zeta^2}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\ln^2 M_p \cdot (1-\zeta^2) = \zeta^2 \pi^2$$

$$\ln^2 M_p - \zeta^2 \ln^2 M_p = \zeta^2 \pi^2$$

$$\ln^2 M_p = \zeta^2 (\pi^2 + \ln^2 M_p)$$

Dividing:

$$\zeta^2 = \frac{\ln^2 M_p}{\pi^2 + \ln^2 M_p}$$

Taking the positive square root (since $\zeta > 0$):

$$\boxed{\;\zeta = \frac{\vert \ln M_p \vert}{\sqrt{\pi^2 + \ln^2 M_p}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic of every step:**
> 1. We started with the canonical second-order step response in Laplace domain.
> 2. Differentiated to find the first peak (extremum condition).
> 3. Used trigonometric identities to find $t_p$.
> 4. Substituted $t_p$ back into $y(t)$ to find $y_{\max}$.
> 5. Solved for $\zeta$ algebraically — this gives us the **key KTU formula** that converts the *specification* $M_p$ into the *design parameter* $\zeta$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Settling Time Relation

The **settling time** $t_s$ is defined as the time after which the response stays within a $\pm\Delta$ band of the final value (typically $\Delta = 0.02$ for **2% criterion** or $\Delta = 0.05$ for **5% criterion**).

The **envelope** of the underdamped response decays exponentially as $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$. The peak deviation from unity is:

$$\vert y(t) - 1 \vert \le \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$$

For small $\zeta$ ($\zeta < 0.3$), $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1$, so the bound simplifies to $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$.

Setting this equal to the tolerance $\Delta$:

$$e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_s} = \Delta$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\zeta\omega_n t_s = \ln \Delta$$

$$t_s = -\frac{\ln \Delta}{\zeta \omega_n}$$

For $\Delta = 0.02$ (2%):

$$t_s = -\frac{\ln(0.02)}{\zeta \omega_n} = -\frac{-3.912}{\zeta \omega_n} \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$$

For $\Delta = 0.05$ (5%):

$$t_s = -\frac{\ln(0.05)}{\zeta \omega_n} = -\frac{-2.996}{\zeta \omega_n} \approx \frac{3}{\zeta \omega_n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $t_s$ is *inversely proportional* to $\zeta \omega_n$ — the **real part of the dominant pole**. Hence, to make the system faster (smaller $t_s$), we must **push the closed-loop poles further to the left** in the $s$-plane. This is the geometric meaning of the design process.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Lead Compensator Transfer Function

A **lead compensator** is realized by the following electrical network: a resistor $R_1$ in series with a parallel combination of $R_2$ and capacitor $C$, with the output taken across the parallel combination.

The **transfer function** of this network is derived using the voltage-divider rule in the $s$-domain:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot \frac{1 + R_2 C s}{1 + \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} C s}$$

Let

$$K_c = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}, \qquad T = R_2 C, \qquad \alpha = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Substituting:

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + T s}{1 + \alpha T s}$$

Since $0 < \alpha < 1$ (because $R_1, R_2 > 0$), the **zero** is at $s = -1/T$ (further left on the real axis), and the **pole** is at $s = -1/(\alpha T)$ (closer to the imaginary axis, since $\alpha < 1$).

Rewriting in the **standard pole-zero form**:

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{s + 1/T}{s + 1/(\alpha T)}$$

The **DC gain** is:

$$G_c(0) = K_c = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The **high-frequency gain** is:

$$\lim_{s \to \infty} G_c(s) = \frac{K_c}{\alpha} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

Hence, the **maximum phase lead** occurs at the **geometric mean** of the pole and zero frequencies:

$$\omega_m = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}$$

The **maximum phase contribution** is:

$$\phi_{\max} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1 - \alpha}{1 + \alpha}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why lead compensation?** A lead network adds *positive phase* at the gain-crossover frequency, thereby **increasing the phase margin** (PM). The design process selects $\alpha$ and $T$ to:
> 1. Place a new zero/pole such that the **angle condition** is satisfied at the desired closed-loop pole $s_d$.
> 2. The **magnitude condition** then gives the required gain $K_c$.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Complete Closed-Loop Design Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **architectural flow** of a classical control system design — including the plant, controller, feedback, disturbance, and sensor noise paths. (This is the *unified picture* the KTU examiner expects you to draw.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Reference Input R of s"]
    SUM1["Summing Junction"]
    EC["Error Signal E of s"]
    GC["Compensator Gc of s"]
    GP["Plant Gp of s"]
    OUT["Output Y of s"]
    FBK["Feedback H of s"]
    SUM2["Disturbance Injection Point"]
    DSB["Disturbance D of s"]
    NOI["Sensor Noise N of s"]
    SUM3["Summing Junction for Noise"]

    R1 --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> EC
    EC --> GC
    GC --> GP
    GP --> SUM2
    DSB --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> OUT
    OUT --> SUM3
    NOI --> SUM3
    SUM3 --> FBK
    FBK --> SUM1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - Forward path: $R(s) \rightarrow E(s) \rightarrow G_c(s) \rightarrow G_p(s) \rightarrow Y(s)$.
> - Feedback path: $Y(s) \rightarrow H(s) \rightarrow$ returns to summing junction.
> - **Disturbance $D(s)$** enters at the plant output (load disturbance).
> - **Noise $N(s)$** enters at the sensor (measurement noise).
> - The **closed-loop transfer function** is $T(s) = \frac{G_c(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) H(s)}$.

## 4.2 Process Flow — The Six-Stage Iterative Design Cycle

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the **iterative nature** of the design process — note the feedback loop from validation back to specification adjustment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STG1["Stage 1: Plant Modelling — Derive Gp of s"]
    STG2["Stage 2: Identify Specs — Mp, ts, ess, PM, GM"]
    STG3["Stage 3: Open Loop Analysis — Root Locus or Bode"]
    DEC1["Decide Compensation Type — Lead, Lag, PID, Lead Lag"]
    STG5["Stage 5: Parameter Tuning — Solve Angle and Magnitude Conditions"]
    STG6["Stage 6: Validate via Simulation — Compare with Specs"]
    DEC2{"All Specs Satisfied?"}
    STG7["Stage 7: Realize Compensator — Op amp circuit or code"]
    STG8["Stage 8: Document and Deploy"]

    STG1 --> STG2
    STG2 --> STG3
    STG3 --> DEC1
    DEC1 --> STG5
    STG5 --> STG6
    STG6 --> DEC2
    DEC2 -- YES --> STG7
    DEC2 -- NO --> STG2
    STG7 --> STG8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the loop back to Stage 2?** KTU examiners *love* testing whether students understand that the design process is **iterative**, not one-shot. If the simulation shows $M_p = 35\%$ but the spec says $\le 20\%$, you go back and **re-select** $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of a Typical PID-Based Industrial Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["Setpoint SP"]
    PV["Process Variable PV"]
    ERR["Error e of t"]
    PID["PID Controller — Kp plus Ki over s plus Kd s"]
    ACT["Actuator — Valve or Motor"]
    PLT["Plant — Tank or Motor"]
    SEN["Sensor — Transmitter"]
    MEA["Measured Value"]

    SP --> ERR
    PV --> ERR
    ERR --> PID
    PID --> ACT
    ACT --> PLT
    PLT --> PV
    PV --> SEN
    SEN --> MEA
    MEA --> PV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **PID Structure in Continuous Time:**
> 
> $$u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
> 
> The corresponding transfer function in the $s$-domain is:
> 
> $$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$
> 
> The **design process** here is to select $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ (three knobs) so that the closed-loop response meets all specifications.
