# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** State the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. What is the necessary condition for stability based on the coefficients of the characteristic equation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion is a mathematical method used to determine the absolute stability of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system by analyzing the coefficients of its characteristic equation. It states that **a system is stable if and only if all elements in the first column of the Routh array have the same sign** (typically all positive), and **the number of sign changes in the first column equals the number of roots of the characteristic equation lying in the right-half of the s-plane**.
>
> **Necessary Condition:** For the characteristic polynomial $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \ldots + a_n = 0$, all coefficients must be **real, positive, and none should be missing**. If even one coefficient is negative or zero, the system is definitely unstable.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2.** What is the role of the auxiliary polynomial in the Routh array? When is it used?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The auxiliary polynomial is used in a **special case of the Routh array construction** when an **entire row of zeros** appears. This situation arises when the characteristic equation has **symmetrical roots about the origin** (e.g., pairs like $\pm j\omega$, $\pm \sigma$, or $\pm \alpha \pm j\beta$).
>
> The auxiliary polynomial $A(s)$ is formed using the coefficients of the **row immediately above the row of zeros**. Its derivative $\frac{dA(s)}{ds}$ is then computed, and the coefficients of this derivative **replace the zero row**, allowing the array to be completed. The auxiliary polynomial is also set to zero to find the **frequency of marginal oscillation**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Model Question

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q(A).** The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback control system is:
>
> $$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$
>
> **(a)** Determine the characteristic equation of the closed-loop system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, find the range of $K$ for which the system is stable. Also determine the value of $K$ for marginal stability and the corresponding frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Characteristic Equation (7 marks)

The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{\frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}}{1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}}$$

The characteristic equation is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$:

$$1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0$$

Multiplying through by $s(s+2)(s+4)$:

$$s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0$$

Expanding the polynomial $s(s+2)(s+4)$:

$$(s+2)(s+4) = s^2 + 6s + 8$$

Multiplying by $s$:

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s$$

Therefore, the characteristic equation is:

$$\boxed{s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0}$$

**Necessary condition check:** All coefficients of $s^3, s^2, s$ are positive. The constant term $K$ must be positive for the necessary condition.

#### Part (b) — Routh-Hurwitz Analysis (7 marks)

Constructing the Routh array for $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$:

**Row $s^3$:** Coefficients of $s^3$ and $s^1$: $1, 8$

**Row $s^2$:** Coefficients of $s^2$ and $s^0$: $6, K$

**Row $s^1$:** Compute using the determinant formula.

$$b_1 = -\frac{1}{6}\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 8 \\ 6 & K \end{vmatrix} = -\frac{1}{6}(1 \cdot K - 8 \cdot 6) = -\frac{1}{6}(K - 48) = \frac{48 - K}{6}$$

**Row $s^0$:** Compute using the $s^1$ and $s^2$ rows.

$$c_1 = -\frac{1}{b_1}\begin{vmatrix} 6 & K \\ b_1 & 0 \end{vmatrix} = -\frac{1}{b_1}(0 - K \cdot b_1) = K$$

**Final Routh Array:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $8$ |
| $s^2$ | $6$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{48 - K}{6}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | $0$ |

**Stability Conditions (all first-column elements positive):**

$$1 > 0 \quad \text{(satisfied)}$$

$$6 > 0 \quad \text{(satisfied)}$$

$$\frac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \implies K < 48$$

$$K > 0$$

**Range of K for stability:**

$$\boxed{0 < K < 48}$$

**Marginal Stability:** At $K = 48$, the $s^1$ row becomes zero, indicating marginal stability. The auxiliary polynomial is formed from the $s^2$ row:

$$A(s) = 6s^2 + K = 6s^2 + 48 = 0$$

Solving for $s$:

$$6s^2 + 48 = 0 \implies s^2 = -8 \implies s = \pm j\sqrt{8} = \pm j2\sqrt{2}$$

**Frequency of oscillation:**

$$\omega = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \text{ rad/sec}$$

At $K = 48$, the system will exhibit **sustained oscillations** at this frequency. For $K > 48$, the system becomes unstable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — KTU Model Question (Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q(B).** A unity feedback system has an open-loop transfer function:
>
> $$G(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s-1)(s+3)}$$
>
> **(a)** Find the condition on $K$ for the system to be stable. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Determine the number of poles in the right-half s-plane when $K = 5$ and when $K = 50$. Comment on the stability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Stability Range (7 marks)

The characteristic equation is $1 + G(s) = 0$:

$$1 + \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s-1)(s+3)} = 0$$

Multiplying through by $s(s-1)(s+3)$:

$$s(s-1)(s+3) + K(s+2) = 0$$

Expanding $s(s-1)(s+3)$:

$$(s-1)(s+3) = s^2 + 2s - 3$$

Multiplying by $s$:

$$s^3 + 2s^2 - 3s$$

Adding $K(s+2) = Ks + 2K$:

$$s^3 + 2s^2 + (-3 + K)s + 2K = 0$$

$$\boxed{s^3 + 2s^2 + (K-3)s + 2K = 0}$$

**Necessary condition check:** Coefficient of $s^2$ is positive ($2$). For $K > 3$, the coefficient of $s$ is positive. For $K > 0$, the constant term is positive.

**Constructing the Routh Array:**

**Row $s^3$:** Coefficients of $s^3$ and $s^1$: $1, (K-3)$

**Row $s^2$:** Coefficients of $s^2$ and $s^0$: $2, 2K$

**Row $s^1$:** Compute.

$$b_1 = -\frac{1}{2}\begin{vmatrix} 1 & (K-3) \\ 2 & 2K \end{vmatrix} = -\frac{1}{2}(1 \cdot 2K - (K-3) \cdot 2) = -\frac{1}{2}(2K - 2K + 6) = -\frac{1}{2}(6) = -3$$

**Row $s^0$:** Compute.

$$c_1 = -\frac{1}{-3}\begin{vmatrix} 2 & 2K \\ -3 & 0 \end{vmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}(0 - (-3)(2K)) = \frac{1}{3}(6K) = 2K$$

**Final Routh Array:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $K - 3$ |
| $s^2$ | $2$ | $2K$ |
| $s^1$ | $-3$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $2K$ | $0$ |

**Stability Conditions:**

- $1 > 0$: Always satisfied.
- $2 > 0$: Always satisfied.
- $-3 > 0$: **Impossible!** This element is always negative.

**Conclusion:** **No value of $K$ can make the system stable**. The system is **unstable for all $K > 0$**.

#### Part (b) — Counting RHP Poles (7 marks)

Sign changes in the first column: $+1 \to +2 \to -3 \to +2K$ (for $K > 0$).

- Sign change 1: $+2 \to -3$ (positive to negative)
- Sign change 2: $-3 \to +2K$ (negative to positive, for $K > 0$)

**Number of sign changes = 2**, regardless of the value of $K > 0$.

**Case 1: $K = 5$**
- First column: $1, 2, -3, 10$
- Sign changes: $2$
- **2 roots in RHP** → System is **unstable**.

**Case 2: $K = 50$**
- First column: $1, 2, -3, 100$
- Sign changes: $2$
- **2 roots in RHP** → System is **unstable**.

**Stability Comment:** In both cases, the system is unstable with 2 poles in the right-half s-plane. The presence of the term $(s-1)$ in the open-loop transfer function (indicating an unstable plant with a pole at $s = 1$ in the RHP) makes the closed-loop system inherently difficult to stabilize for any positive $K$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign of Coefficient Mistake (1-2 marks lost):** Students often forget to apply the negative sign from the Routh formula correctly. The formula is $R_{i,j} = -\frac{(a)(d) - (b)(c)}{c}$ where the denominator is the first element of the row *above*. Write it down explicitly to avoid sign errors.
> 2. **Necessary Condition Skip (1 mark lost):** Always check that all coefficients are positive and no terms are missing **before** constructing the Routh array. This is a quick preliminary check that many KTU students omit.
> 3. **Special Case Panic (2-3 marks lost):** When a zero appears in the first column or a row of zeros occurs, students often freeze. Remember: (a) for a single zero, use the $\varepsilon$ method; (b) for a row of zeros, form the auxiliary polynomial from the row above and take its derivative.
> 4. **Auxiliary Polynomial Confusion (2 marks lost):** The auxiliary polynomial is formed from the row *above* the zero row, NOT the zero row itself. Setting $A(s) = 0$ gives the location of symmetrical roots on the $j\omega$-axis.
> 5. **Final Conclusion Phrasing:** Always end with a clear statement: *"The system is stable / unstable / marginally stable with N RHP poles."* Partial credit is often awarded for correct procedure even if a computation error occurs, but the **final conclusion must be unambiguous**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ The **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** determines the **absolute stability** of an LTI system from its characteristic equation without solving for the roots.
- ✅ The characteristic equation is obtained from $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ for a closed-loop system.
- ✅ **Necessary condition**: All coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be **positive, real, and no term should be missing** (zero coefficient is a missing term).
- ✅ The **Routh array** has $n+1$ rows for an $n$-th order polynomial.
- ✅ The **first two rows** contain the coefficients of even and odd powers of $s$ respectively.
- ✅ Each subsequent element is computed as: $R_{i,j} = \dfrac{R_{i-2,0} \cdot R_{i-1,j+1} - R_{i-1,0} \cdot R_{i-2,j+1}}{R_{i-1,0}}$
- ✅ **Stability condition**: All elements in the **first column must be positive** (assuming positive coefficients).
- ✅ **Number of sign changes in the first column = Number of roots in the RHP**.
- ✅ **Special Case 1**: Zero in the first column → Replace with small $\varepsilon > 0$, take limit.
- ✅ **Special Case 2**: Row of zeros → Form auxiliary polynomial from the row above, differentiate, replace the zero row with the derivative's coefficients.
- ✅ **Marginal stability** occurs when an auxiliary polynomial has purely imaginary roots; the frequency of oscillation is found by solving $A(s) = 0$ for $s = j\omega$.
- ✅ For **range of $K$ problems**: Set up the Routh array symbolically, impose all first-column elements $> 0$, and solve the resulting inequalities.
- ✅ The criterion is **purely analytical** — it tells you *whether* the system is stable and *how many* unstable poles exist, but not the exact pole locations.
- ✅ Routh-Hurwitz is **limited to polynomials with real coefficients**; it cannot directly handle time-delayed or distributed-parameter systems.
- ✅ Common KTU marks distribution: 3-mark questions test definitions and small computations; 14-mark questions test full array construction and parameter range analysis.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for stability**: "**A**ll **C**oefficients **P**ositive" (ACP) is necessary; "**A**ll **F**irst-**C**olumn **P**ositive" (AFCP) is necessary and sufficient.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Root Locus: Construction of Root Locus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT521 - CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Root Locus: Construction of Root Locus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Locus Definition (W. R. Evans, 1948):**
> The **root locus** of a closed-loop system is the graphical locus (path) in the complex $s$-plane traced by the closed-loop poles as a specific system parameter (most commonly the open-loop gain $K$) is varied from **zero to infinity**, while all other system parameters are held constant.

Mathematically, if the open-loop transfer function is

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K \cdot N(s)}{D(s)}$$

then the **characteristic equation** governing closed-loop behaviour is

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad D(s) + K \cdot N(s) = 0$$

The root locus is the set of all points $s = \sigma + j\omega$ in the $s$-plane that satisfy this equation for $0 \le K \le \infty$.

## 1.2 Magnitude and Angle Criteria (The Two Pillars)

Every point $s_1$ on the root locus must satisfy **both** of the following conditions simultaneously:

**Magnitude Criterion:**
$$\left\vert G(s_1)H(s_1) \right\vert = 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad K = \frac{1}{\left\vert G_0(s_1)H_0(s_1) \right\vert}$$

where $G_0 H_0$ is the open-loop transfer function with $K$ factored out (i.e., $G(s)H(s) = K \cdot G_0(s)H_0(s)$).

**Angle Criterion:**
$$\angle G(s_1)H(s_1) = \pm 180^\circ (2q+1), \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Only the **angle criterion** is used to determine *where* the locus lies. The magnitude criterion is used *afterwards* to find the value of $K$ at any chosen point on the locus.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Lantern on a Long Rope

Imagine you are standing on a perfectly dark night on a straight road. You hold a lantern that is **tied to a rope of variable length** ($K$ plays the role of the rope length). As you let the rope unwind:

- At **rope length = 0**, the lantern sits exactly on your hand — these are the **open-loop poles** (starting points).
- As the rope lengthens, the lantern swings outward, tracing a **path in the sand** — this path is the **root locus**.
- If there is a **tree or a pole on the road** that the rope can wrap around (an **open-loop zero**), the lantern will eventually settle there when the rope is fully extended — these are the **ending points** for some branches.
- If there is no such anchor, the lantern simply **walks off into the darkness along a straight direction** (an **asymptote**) — the remaining branches go to **infinity**.

The "swinging path" tells you the **stability of the system** at every gain value. If the path ever crosses into the **right half of the plane (RHP)**, the system becomes **unstable** at that gain — exactly when the lantern's shadow falls on the wrong side of the road.

## 1.4 Why Root Locus is the Workhorse of Control Design

| Aspect | Significance in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Visualisation** | Converts an abstract polynomial equation into a tangible 2-D plot |
| **Stability analysis** | Instantly shows range of $K$ for stability (where locus is in LHP) |
| **Transient response design** | Damping ratio ($\zeta$) and natural frequency ($\omega_n$) read directly from pole locations |
| **Compensator design** | Gain ($K$) can be tuned to place poles at desired locations |

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, root locus problems are typically **14-mark analytical** questions (full construction + stability range) or **3-mark short questions** (rules or specific calculations).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 11 Golden Rules of Root Locus Construction (Evans' Rules)

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step procedure** that KTU examiners expect every student to follow in order. Memorise the numbering — it is the de-facto answer template for any "construct the root locus" question worth 7 or 14 marks.

### Rule 1 — Stage the Poles and Zeros
Write $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K(s+z_1)(s+z_2)\dots(s+z_m)}{(s+p_1)(s+p_2)\dots(s+p_n)}$ and mark all **$n$ poles** ($\times$) and **$m$ zeros** ($\circ$) on the $s$-plane.

### Rule 2 — Number of Branches
$$\text{Number of branches} = n$$
(since the characteristic equation is of degree $n$, it has $n$ roots for every $K$).

### Rule 3 — Starting and Ending Points
- **Starting points** ($K = 0$): the **$n$ open-loop poles**.
- **Ending points** ($K = \infty$): the **$m$ open-loop zeros** (if any) plus the **$n-m$ points at infinity** (along the asymptotes).

### Rule 4 — Symmetry
The root locus is **always symmetric about the real axis** because complex roots of real-coefficient polynomials occur in conjugate pairs.

### Rule 5 — Real-Axis Segments
> A point on the real axis belongs to the root locus **if and only if the total number of real poles AND real zeros to its right is odd.**

**Why it works:** Each pole/zeros to the right contributes a $\pm 180^\circ$ angle. An odd count gives a net angle of $\pm 180^\circ$, satisfying the angle criterion.

### Rule 6 — Asymptotes (Behaviour at Infinity)
Number of asymptotes: $n - m$. Their angles and intersection point are:

$$\boxed{\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ}{n-m}, \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \dots, (n-m-1)}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_A = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\text{Real part of }p_i) - \sum_{j=1}^{m}(\text{Real part of }z_j)}{n-m}}$$

### Rule 7 — Breakaway and Break-in Points
These are points on the real axis where **two branches of the locus meet and depart** (breakaway) or **arrive from complex space** (break-in).

**Method:** From $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, isolate $K$ as a function of $s$:
$$K = -\frac{1}{G_0(s)H_0(s)}$$

Then solve:

$$\boxed{\frac{dK}{ds} = 0}$$

**Validity check:** A valid breakaway/break-in point must lie **on the root locus** (i.e., on a real-axis segment satisfying Rule 5) **and** correspond to a **positive** value of $K$.

### Rule 8 — Angle of Departure (from a Complex Pole)
For a complex pole $p_k$:

$$\boxed{\phi_d = 180^\circ + \sum_{j=1}^{m}\angle(p_k - z_j) - \sum_{\substack{i=1 \\ i \neq k}}^{n}\angle(p_k - p_i)}$$

All angles are measured **from the respective pole/zero to $p_k$**.

### Rule 9 — Angle of Arrival (at a Complex Zero)
For a complex zero $z_k$:

$$\boxed{\phi_a = 180^\circ - \sum_{j \neq k}\angle(z_k - z_j) + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\angle(z_k - p_i)}$$

### Rule 10 — Intersection with the Imaginary Axis (Marginal Stability Point)
Two standard methods:
- **Routh-Hurwitz:** Construct the Routh array; the auxiliary equation (row of zeros) gives the $j\omega$-crossing frequency, and the corresponding row gives the **critical gain** $K_{max}$.
- **Substitution:** Put $s = j\omega$ into the characteristic equation, equate real and imaginary parts to zero, solve simultaneously.

### Rule 11 — Gain Calculation at Any Point $s_1$
$$\boxed{K = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert s_1 - p_i \right\vert}{\prod_{j=1}^{m} \left\vert s_1 - z_j \right\vert}}$$

(Product of distances from $s_1$ to all poles) divided by (product of distances from $s_1$ to all zeros).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Quick Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Characteristic equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Foundation of everything |
| 2 | Angle criterion | $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180^\circ (2q+1)$ | Used to **test** a point |
| 3 | Magnitude criterion | $K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G_0(s_1)H_0(s_1)\vert}$ | Used to **find** $K$ |
| 4 | Asymptote angle | $\phi_A = \dfrac{(2q+1)180^\circ}{n-m}$ | $q = 0,1,\dots,n-m-1$ |
| 5 | Centroid (intersection) | $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum \text{Re}(p_i) - \sum \text{Re}(z_j)}{n-m}$ | Real axis intercept of asymptotes |
| 6 | Breakaway condition | $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$ | Check $K > 0$ |
| 7 | Angle of departure | $\phi_d = 180^\circ + \sum \angle \text{(zeros)} - \sum \angle \text{(poles)}$ | Exclude the pole itself |
| 8 | Angle of arrival | $\phi_a = 180^\circ - \sum \angle \text{(zeros)} + \sum \angle \text{(poles)}$ | Exclude the zero itself |
| 9 | $j\omega$ crossing | Routh array auxiliary row $= 0$ | $K_{max}$ found here |
| 10 | Gain at any point | $K = \dfrac{\prod \vert s - p_i\vert}{\prod \vert s - z_j\vert}$ | Apply to a known $s$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units in this topic are dimensionless** — all quantities are pure complex-plane geometry, so no physical units apply. The "magnitude" is unitless because $K$ is unitless in the canonical form.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How Root Locus Helps |
|---|---|
| **Servo-motor position control** | Designer picks $K$ so that closed-loop poles have $\zeta = 0.7$ for fast, well-damped response |
| **Aircraft autopilot gain tuning** | Root locus reveals the **exact gain at which the plane oscillates** (marginal stability) — a safety-critical limit |
| **DC motor speed regulator** | Locus on real axis ⇒ non-oscillatory response (good for steady loads) |
| **Op-amp compensator design** | Adding a pole/zero (lead/lag compensator) reshapes the locus to pass through the **desired dominant-pole location** |
| **Process control (chemical plants)** | The locus tells the operator at what controller gain the loop will go unstable — a direct PID tuning tool |

## 2.4 A Note on Negative $K$ (Complementary Root Locus)

When $K$ varies from $0$ to $-\infty$, we get the **complementary root locus** satisfying
$$1 - G(s)H(s) = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \angle G(s)H(s) = 0^\circ, \pm 360^\circ, \dots$$
KTU rarely tests this, but the angle rule changes from "$180^\circ(2q+1)$" to "$360^\circ q$".

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Full Root Locus Construction (14-Mark Standard)

### Problem Statement
> Consider a unity-feedback control system with
> $$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$
> Sketch the root locus and find the range of $K$ for stability.

### Step 1 — Stage the Open-Loop Poles and Zeros

**Action:** Factor the denominator to obtain pole locations.

The denominator expands to:
$$s(s+2)(s+5) = s(s^2 + 7s + 10) = s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s$$

So the open-loop transfer function is

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s}$$

**Poles** (set denominator $= 0$): $s = 0, \; s = -2, \; s = -5$  $\Longrightarrow$  $n = 3$

**Zeros**: None (numerator is constant)  $\Longrightarrow$  $m = 0$

**Stageing result:**

```
        jω
         |
         |
    ×    |         ×          ×
   -5    |        -2         0
---------+--------------------→ σ
```

> Three poles, no zeros, so $n - m = 3$ asymptotes, three branches starting at the three poles.

### Step 2 — Number of Branches and Symmetry

Number of branches $= n = 3$.

By Rule 4, the locus is **symmetric about the real axis**. Since all three poles are real, we will get a **pair of complex branches** breaking away into the upper and lower half-planes and a **single real-axis branch** that may break away and re-enter.

### Step 3 — Real-Axis Segments (Rule 5)

Test points on the real axis by counting poles and zeros to the **right**:

| Test Point | Poles to right | Zeros to right | Total | On Locus? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $s = 1$ (right of 0) | 0, –2, –5 (all 3) | 0 | **3 (odd)** | **Yes** |
| $s = -1$ (between 0 and –2) | –2, –5 (2) | 0 | **2 (even)** | No |
| $s = -3$ (between –2 and –5) | –5 (1) | 0 | **1 (odd)** | **Yes** |
| $s = -6$ (left of –5) | 0 | 0 | **0 (even)** | No |

**Real-axis root-locus segments:** $[0, +\infty)$ wait — actually we follow the **odd-rule** strictly:

- The point at $s = 1$ has three poles to the right $\Rightarrow$ odd $\Rightarrow$ **on locus**.
- Therefore the segment from $s = 0$ extending **rightward to $+\infty$** is part of the locus.

> Correction: In standard root-locus convention, the locus to the right of pole at $0$ is on the locus. We then check between the poles:
- Between $-2$ and $0$: 1 pole ($-2$) to the right of a test point at $-1$ $\Rightarrow$ **odd $\Rightarrow$ on locus**.
- Between $-5$ and $-2$: 1 pole ($-5$) to the right of a test point at $-3$ $\Rightarrow$ **odd $\Rightarrow$ on locus**.

So the **real-axis locus** lies on the segments $[0, +\infty)$ … actually let me apply the rule carefully:

For a test point at $s = -1$ (between $0$ and $-2$):
- Poles to the right: $0$ (yes, $+0 > -1$), $-2$ (no, $-2 < -1$), $-5$ (no).
- So **1 pole to the right** $\Rightarrow$ odd $\Rightarrow$ **on locus**.

For a test point at $s = -3$ (between $-2$ and $-5$):
- Poles to the right: $-5$ (yes, $-5 > -3$).
- So **1 pole to the right** $\Rightarrow$ odd $\Rightarrow$ **on locus**.

For a test point at $s = 1$ (right of $0$):
- Poles to the right of $s=1$: **none** (all poles are $\le 0$).
- So **0 poles/zeros to the right** $\Rightarrow$ even $\Rightarrow$ **NOT on locus**.

For a test point at $s = -6$ (left of $-5$):
- Poles to the right: 0, $-2$, $-5$ = **3 poles**.
- Odd $\Rightarrow$ **on locus**.

**Final real-axis segments:** $[-2, 0]$ and $[-5, -2]$ and $(-\infty, -5]$.

In summary, **almost the entire negative real axis** is on the locus, **except** the segment to the **right of $s = 0$**.

```
        jω
         |
    ×    |         ×          ×
   -5----+---- -2 ---- 0
---------+--------------------→ σ
         |    (real-axis locus on these segments)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-axis locus segments** (corrected and final):
> - $(-\infty, -5]$: on locus (3 poles to the right of any interior test point $\Rightarrow$ odd).
> - $[-5, -2]$: on locus (1 pole to the right $\Rightarrow$ odd).
> - $[-2, 0]$: on locus (1 pole to the right $\Rightarrow$ odd).
> - $[0, +\infty)$: NOT on locus (0 poles to the right $\Rightarrow$ even).

### Step 4 — Asymptotes (Rule 6)

Number of asymptotes $= n - m = 3 - 0 = 3$.

**Angles:**

$$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ}{3}, \quad q = 0, 1, 2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
q=0: \quad \phi_A &= \frac{180^\circ}{3} = 60^\circ \\
q=1: \quad \phi_A &= \frac{3 \cdot 180^\circ}{3} = 180^\circ \\
q=2: \quad \phi_A &= \frac{5 \cdot 180^\circ}{3} = 300^\circ \; (\text{or } -60^\circ)
\end{aligned}$$

**Centroid (intersection on real axis):**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{\sum \text{Re}(p_i) - \sum \text{Re}(z_j)}{n - m} = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-5) - 0}{3} = \frac{-7}{3} = -2.33$$

So the **three asymptotes are straight lines radiating from $\sigma_A = -2.33$ at angles $60^\circ$, $180^\circ$, and $300^\circ$**.

### Step 5 — Breakaway Point (Rule 7)

The characteristic equation is

$$s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + K = 0$$

Solve for $K$:

$$K = -(s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s)$$

Differentiate with respect to $s$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -(3s^2 + 14s + 10) = 0$$

$$3s^2 + 14s + 10 = 0$$

Apply the quadratic formula:

$$s = \frac{-14 \pm \sqrt{14^2 - 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 10}}{2 \cdot 3} = \frac{-14 \pm \sqrt{196 - 120}}{6} = \frac{-14 \pm \sqrt{76}}{6}$$

$$s = \frac{-14 \pm 8.72}{6}$$

Two solutions:
- $s_1 = \dfrac{-14 + 8.72}{6} = \dfrac{-5.28}{6} = -0.88$
- $s_2 = \dfrac{-14 - 8.72}{6} = \dfrac{-22.72}{6} = -3.79$

**Validity Check:**
- $s_1 = -0.88$ lies between $-2$ and $0$ — this is **on the real-axis locus** (Rule 5). Compute $K$:
  $$K = -((-0.88)^3 + 7(-0.88)^2 + 10(-0.88)) = -(-0.681 + 5.421 - 8.8) = -(-4.06) = 4.06 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$
  **Valid breakaway point at $s \approx -0.88$** with $K \approx 4.06$.
- $s_2 = -3.79$ lies between $-5$ and $-2$ — this is also **on the real-axis locus**. Compute $K$:
  $$K = -((-3.79)^3 + 7(-3.79)^2 + 10(-3.79)) = -(-54.45 + 100.55 - 37.9) = -8.2$$
  $K = -8.2 < 0$ — **Invalid** (reject, $K$ must be non-negative for standard root locus).

So the **only valid breakaway point** is $\boxed{s_b = -0.88, \; K \approx 4.06}$.

### Step 6 — Intersection with Imaginary Axis (Rule 10)

Apply the **Routh-Hurwitz** criterion. The characteristic equation is

$$s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + K = 0$$

**Routh Array:**

$$\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 10 \\
s^2 & 7 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{70 - K}{7} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & 0
\end{array}$$

**Stability condition:** All first-column elements must be **strictly positive**.

$$7 > 0 \; \checkmark \quad \text{(always)}$$

$$\frac{70 - K}{7} > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K < 70$$

$$K > 0$$

Therefore, the system is **stable for** $\boxed{0 < K < 70}$.

**Auxiliary equation** (when $K = 70$, the $s^1$ row is zero):
$$7s^2 + K = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 7s^2 + 70 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s^2 = -10 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s = \pm j\sqrt{10}$$

**Marginal stability point:** $s = \pm j 3.16$ at $K = 70$.

### Step 7 — Summary Plot

```
                  jω
                   |
         (locus)   |  / 60° asymptote
              \    | / 
               \   |/         ×  (pole at 0)
   j3.16 -----  \--X--        |
                /  | \         |
               /   |  \  (locus in upper half)
              /    |   \   /
   ×(pole-2) ×   |    \ /
   ----------●----|-----●------→ σ
   -5       -2.33 |    -0.88
              ↑   |   ↑
           centroid breakaway
                \  |  /
                 \ | /
                  300° asymptote
                   |
```

### Step 8 — Step-by-Step Routh Calculation Re-Derived for Clarity

The first column of the Routh array is computed as follows:

**$s^3$ row:** coefficients of odd powers: $1, 10$.

**$s^2$ row:** coefficients of even powers: $7, K$.

**$s^1$ row:** entries computed as

$$b_1 = \frac{7 \cdot 10 - 1 \cdot K}{7} = \frac{70 - K}{7}, \quad b_2 = \frac{7 \cdot 0 - 1 \cdot 0}{7} = 0$$

**$s^0$ row:** entry is just $K$ (the constant term of the characteristic polynomial).

**Auxiliary equation** is formed from the row *above* the row of zeros. When $K = 70$, the $s^1$ row becomes zero, so we use the $s^2$ row:

$$A(s) = 7s^2 + K = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \pm j\sqrt{K/7} = \pm j\sqrt{10}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — "Given a Transfer Function, How Do I Start?"

| Step | Question to Ask Yourself | Rule Number | Deliverable |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Where are the poles? Where are the zeros? | Rule 1 | Pole-zero map |
| 2 | How many branches? | Rule 2 | $n$ |
| 3 | Where do they start and end? | Rule 3 | $K=0$ at poles, $K=\infty$ at zeros/infinity |
| 4 | Is it symmetric? | Rule 4 | Yes, always — about real axis |
| 5 | Which real-axis segments? | Rule 5 | Count to the right — must be odd |
| 6 | How do the branches fly to infinity? | Rule 6 | $(n-m)$ asymptotes from $\sigma_A$ at $\phi_A$ |
| 7 | Where does the real-axis locus peel off? | Rule 7 | Solve $dK/ds = 0$ |
| 8 | At what complex pole does the locus leave at an unusual angle? | Rule 8 | Angle of departure $\phi_d$ |
| 9 | At what complex zero does the locus arrive? | Rule 9 | Angle of arrival $\phi_a$ |
| 10 | When does the locus cross into RHP (instability)? | Rule 10 | Routh-Hurwitz or $s = j\omega$ |
| 11 | What is $K$ at a desired point? | Rule 11 | Product of distances formula |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Root Locus Construction Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid block visualises the **complete decision flow** that a student should follow when answering a 14-mark "construct the root locus" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read GsH s and factor it] --> B[Identify poles p_i and zeros z_j]
    B --> C[Count n poles and m zeros]
    C --> D{n greater than m?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Compute asymptote angles phi_A using 2q+1*180 divided by n-m]
    D -- No --> F[Most branches end at finite zeros]
    E --> G[Compute centroid sigma_A on real axis]
    G --> H[Mark real-axis locus segments odd-count rule]
    H --> I[Find breakaway points via dK by ds = 0]
    I --> J{Any complex poles or zeros?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Compute angle of departure or arrival]
    J -- No --> L[Skip to Routh analysis]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Apply Routh-Hurwitz to find j omega crossing]
    M --> N[Determine K max for marginal stability]
    N --> O[Compute K at any test point using magnitude criterion]
    O --> P[Sketch the complete root locus with all features labelled]
    F --> H
```

## 4.2 Mapping the 11 Rules to a Single Block Diagram

The following block diagram shows how each rule **contributes a specific piece of information** to the final root locus plot.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Open-Loop Transfer Function] --> R1[Rule 1: Pole-Zero Map]
    R1 --> R2[Rule 2: Number of Branches equals n]
    R2 --> R3[Rule 3: Start and End Points]
    R3 --> R4[Rule 4: Symmetry about Real Axis]
    R4 --> R5[Rule 5: Real-Axis Segments Odd Test]
    R5 --> R6[Rule 6: Asymptotes Angles and Centroid]
    R6 --> R7[Rule 7: Breakaway and Break-in Points]
    R7 --> R8[Rule 8: Angle of Departure from Complex Poles]
    R8 --> R9[Rule 9: Angle of Arrival at Complex Zeros]
    R9 --> R10[Rule 10: Imaginary Axis Crossing via Routh]
    R10 --> R11[Rule 11: K value at any test point]
    R11 --> OUT[Final Root Locus Plot with all 11 features]
```

## 4.3 Generic Root Locus Topology (Block Architecture)

For a third-order system with **three real poles and no zeros** (e.g., $\frac{K}{s(s+a)(s+b)}$), the locus has a characteristic "Y-shape with the tail going to the right" topology. The block architecture below shows the **functional regions** of such a plot.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Real_Axis_Region["Real-Axis Region"]
        A1[Branch starts at pole s equals 0] --> A2[Branch moves left along negative real axis]
        A2 --> A3[Branch meets branch from s equals minus a at breakaway]
        A3 --> A4[Two complex branches depart into upper and lower half-planes]
        A4 --> A5[Complex branches curve back and cross imaginary axis at s equals j omega at K equals Kmax]
    end
    subgraph Asymptote_Region["Asymptote Region"]
        B1[Centroid sigma_A on real axis] --> B2[Asymptote at 60 degrees]
        B1 --> B3[Asymptote at 180 degrees]
        B1 --> B4[Asymptote at 300 degrees]
    end
    A5 --> B2
    A5 --> B4
    A3 --> B3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this architecture matters in KTU exams:** Examiners award **partial credit** for each correctly computed feature. A student who draws *only* the asymptotes and centroid but forgets the breakaway point still earns 5–6 marks. This is why committing the **11 rules in order** is the safest strategy.
