| Phase Crossover | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$ | $\omega_{pc}$ in rad/s |
| **Gain Margin (dB)** | $GM = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | **dB** |
| **Gain Margin (linear)** | $GM_{\text{lin}} = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | dimensionless |
| **Phase Margin** | $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ | **degrees** |
| Stability Criterion | $GM > 0$ dB **and** $PM > 0^\circ$ | both must hold |
| Acceptable PM for design | $30^\circ \le PM \le 60^\circ$ | good transient response |
| Slope crossing limit | Final slope at $\omega_{gc}$ must be $\ge -40$ dB/decade for stability of Type-1 systems | heuristic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Phase Margin is the *single most important* design parameter in classical control — it directly predicts closed-loop **overshoot** and **damping**. As a rule of thumb, **% Overshoot $\approx 75 \times PM^{-1}$** for moderate PM values (after a small correction).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerospace**: Bode plots of aircraft pitch dynamics determine pilot-in-the-loop stability margins.
- **Power Electronics**: SMPS converter compensation networks are designed using Bode-derived PM/GM.
- **Process Control**: PID tuning (Ziegler-Nichols ultimate gain method) uses the **ultimate frequency** $\omega_{pc}$ and gain $K_u = 1/\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$.
- **Op-Amp Design**: Op-amp stability is universally verified via Bode plots of the open-loop gain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference Example — Complete Bode Analysis

Let us analyse the open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$$

### Step A — Convert to Bode (Time-Constant) Form

Factor out the constants from the simple poles so that each factor has unit gain at low frequency:

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{s \cdot (s+1) \cdot (2)\left(\frac{s+2}{2}\right)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{s\,(1+s)\,\left(1+\dfrac{s}{2}\right)}$$

Therefore the Bode form is:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{0.5}{(j\omega)(1 + j\omega)\left(1 + j\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)}$$

The low-frequency gain factor is $K = 0.5$, i.e., $20\log_{10}(0.5) = -6.02$ dB.

The corner frequencies are:
- Pole at origin (no corner, just $-20$ dB/decade slope).
- $\omega_1 = 1$ rad/s from $(1 + j\omega)$.
- $\omega_2 = 2$ rad/s from $(1 + j\omega/2)$.

### Step B — Construct the Asymptotic Magnitude Plot

The slope changes as follows:

| Frequency Range (rad/s) | Active Slopes | Net Slope (dB/dec) |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega < 1$ | $-20$ (from $1/s$ only) | $-20$ |
| $1 \le \omega < 2$ | $-20$ (from $1/s$) $+ (-20)$ (from $1/(1+s)$) | $-40$ |
| $\omega \ge 2$ | $-20 - 20 - 20$ | $-60$ |

Starting at $\omega = 1$ with the asymptote value of $-6.02$ dB (i.e., $-20\log(1) - 6.02 = -6.02$ dB), the line falls with $-40$ dB/dec slope until $\omega = 2$, where its value is:

$$M(2^-) = -6.02 - 40 \log_{10}(2) = -6.02 - 12.04 = -18.06 \text{ dB}$$

Beyond $\omega = 2$, the slope becomes $-60$ dB/dec.

### Step C — Phase Expression of the Open-Loop System

The total phase of $G(j\omega)$ is:

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

> **Derivation of the $-90^\circ$ term:** The factor $1/j\omega = 1/(\omega \cdot e^{j90^\circ}) = (1/\omega) e^{-j90^\circ}$, contributing $-90^\circ$ of phase independent of $\omega$.

> **Derivation of the $\tan^{-1}$ terms:** Each term $(1 + j\omega T)$ lies in the first quadrant of the complex plane. Its argument is $\tan^{-1}(\omega T / 1) = \tan^{-1}(\omega T)$. Since these factors are in the denominator, the contributions are subtracted.

### Step D — Find the Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$

Set $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$:

$$-90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) = -180^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) = 90^\circ$$

Apply the tangent-addition identity:

$$\tan\!\left[\tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right)\right] = \tan(90^\circ) = \infty$$

For a finite numerator, the denominator must vanish:

$$1 - \omega_{pc} \cdot \frac{\omega_{pc}}{2} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1 - \frac{\omega_{pc}^2}{2} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ rad/s}}$$

### Step E — Compute the Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ to Obtain GM

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{0.5}{\omega_{pc} \cdot \sqrt{1+\omega_{pc}^2} \cdot \sqrt{1+\omega_{pc}^2/4}}$$

Substitute $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{2}$:

- $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{2}$
- $\sqrt{1 + \omega_{pc}^2} = \sqrt{1 + 2} = \sqrt{3}$
- $\sqrt{1 + \omega_{pc}^2/4} = \sqrt{1 + 2/4} = \sqrt{1.5} = \sqrt{3/2}$

Multiplying the denominators:

$$\omega_{pc} \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} = 3$$

Therefore:

$$\vert G(j\sqrt{2}) \vert = \frac{0.5}{3} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$

Converting to **decibels**:

$$20 \log_{10}(0.1667) = 20 \times (-0.778) = -15.56 \text{ dB}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{GM = -(-15.56) = +15.56 \text{ dB}}$$

Equivalently, in linear scale: $GM_{\text{lin}} = 6$.

> **Interpretation:** The open-loop gain can be increased by a factor of **6** (or $15.56$ dB) before the system becomes marginally stable.

### Step F — Find the Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$

Set $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:

$$0.5 = \omega_{gc} \cdot \sqrt{1+\omega_{gc}^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{\omega_{gc}^2}{4}}$$

Square both sides:

$$0.25 = \omega_{gc}^2 (1+\omega_{gc}^2)\left(1 + \frac{\omega_{gc}^2}{4}\right)$$

Let $u = \omega_{gc}^2$:

$$0.25 = u(1+u)\left(1 + \frac{u}{4}\right) = u(1+u)\left(\frac{4+u}{4}\right)$$

$$1 = u(1+u)(4+u)$$

Expanding:

$$u(u^2 + 5u + 4) = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; u^3 + 5u^2 + 4u - 1 = 0$$

Test $u = 0.18$: $0.00583 + 0.162 + 0.72 - 1 = -0.112$
Test $u = 0.20$: $0.008 + 0.20 + 0.80 - 1 = 0.008 \approx 0$

So $u \approx 0.20$ and:

$$\boxed{\omega_{gc} = \sqrt{0.20} \approx 0.447 \text{ rad/s}}$$

### Step G — Compute the Phase at $\omega_{gc}$ to Obtain PM

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.447) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.447}{2}\right)$$

- $\tan^{-1}(0.447) = 24.09^\circ$
- $\tan^{-1}(0.2235) = 12.60^\circ$

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = -90^\circ - 24.09^\circ - 12.60^\circ = -126.69^\circ$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{PM = 180^\circ + (-126.69^\circ) = 53.31^\circ}$$

> **Interpretation:** The phase may lag by an **additional 53.31°** at the gain crossover frequency before instability occurs. This corresponds to a well-damped, acceptably stable response.

### Step H — Final Summary Table

| Parameter | Value | Stability Comment |
|---|---|---|
| Low-frequency gain (dB) | $-6.02$ dB | — |
| $\omega_{gc}$ (gain crossover) | $0.447$ rad/s | — |
| $\omega_{pc}$ (phase crossover) | $1.414$ rad/s | — |
| **Gain Margin** | $+15.56$ dB | $> 0$ ✓ |
| **Phase Margin** | $+53.31^\circ$ | $> 0$ ✓ |
| **Closed-loop Stability** | **STABLE** | Both positive |

### Step I — Analytical Matrix Linking Margins to Time-Domain Response

Using the **empirical second-order approximation** (valid for dominant-pole systems):

$$\zeta \approx \frac{PM}{100} \quad \text{(for PM in degrees, } 30^\circ \le PM \le 70^\circ\text{)}$$

For our case: $\zeta \approx 53.31/100 = 0.533$.

$$\%M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi(0.533)/\sqrt{1-0.284}} = 100 \cdot e^{-2.245} \approx 10.6\%$$

This indicates a **moderately damped** closed-loop response with about 10–11% overshoot — consistent with the well-known rule of thumb.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice that in this example, the **phase crossover occurs at a frequency 3.16× higher than the gain crossover**. This separation is what makes the system robust — the magnitude is already deeply negative at $\omega_{pc}$, ensuring a positive GM.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Bode Margin Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Open Loop TF Gs] --> B[Convert to Bode Form: time constants + corners]
    B --> C[List Corner Frequencies: omega_c = 1/T]
    C --> D[Identify Net Slope per decade band]
    D --> E[Draw Asymptotic Magnitude Plot dB vs log omega]
    E --> F[Draw Phase Plot deg vs log omega]
    F --> G{Step 1: Find omega_pc from Phase Plot}
    G --> H[Locate omega_pc where phase = -180 deg]
    H --> I[Read Magnitude value at omega_pc in dB]
    I --> J[Compute GM dB = -Magnitude dB at omega_pc]
    J --> K{Step 2: Find omega_gc from Magnitude Plot}
    K --> L[Locate omega_gc where magnitude = 0 dB]
    L --> M[Read Phase value at omega_gc in degrees]
    M --> N[Compute PM = 180 + Phase at omega_gc]
    N --> O{Check Stability}
    O --> P[GM greater than 0 dB AND PM greater than 0 deg]
    P --> Q[System is STABLE]
    O --> R[GM less than 0 dB OR PM less than 0 deg]
    R --> S[System is UNSTABLE]
    O --> T[GM equals 0 dB OR PM equals 0 deg]
    T --> U[System is MARGINALLY STABLE sustained oscillations]
```

## 4.2 Component Map — How Each Term Shapes the Plot

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Constant Gain K] --> A1[Horizontal shift in dB: 20 log K]
    B[Pole at origin 1 over sN] --> B1[Slope: -20N dB per decade; Phase: -90N deg constant]
    C[Simple pole 1 over 1 plus j omega T] --> C1[Corner at omega equals 1 over T; slope -20; phase 0 to -90 deg]
    D[Simple zero 1 plus j omega T] --> D1[Corner at omega equals 1 over T; slope +20; phase 0 to +90 deg]
    E[Quadratic term] --> E1[Corner at omega_n; slope minus 40; phase 0 to minus 180 deg; resonance peak if zeta small]
    A1 --> F[Superimpose all contributions: net magnitude]
    B1 --> F
    C1 --> F
    D1 --> F
    A1 --> G[Superimpose all phase contributions: net phase]
    B1 --> G
    C1 --> G
    D1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E1 --> F
    F --> H[Final Bode Magnitude Plot]
    G --> I[Final Bode Phase Plot]
    H --> J[Extract GM and PM]
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Phase Margin Visualisation — The Safety Wheel

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Safety_Zone
    PM_Axis[Phase Margin PM] --> PM_Val[PM in degrees]
    PM_Val --> PM_Classify{Classify Range}
    PM_Classify --> PM_High[PM greater than 60: Overdamped, slow]
    PM_Classify --> PM_OK[PM between 30 and 60: Good design]
    PM_Classify --> PM_Low[PM between 0 and 30: Underdamped, oscillatory]
    PM_Classify --> PM_Zero[PM equals 0: Marginally stable]
    PM_Classify --> PM_Neg[PM less than 0: Unstable]
    end
    subgraph Gain_Zone
    GM_Axis[Gain Margin GM] --> GM_Val[GM in dB]
    GM_Val --> GM_Classify{Classify Range}
    GM_Classify --> GM_High[GM greater than 10 dB: Robust]
    GM_Classify --> GM_OK[GM between 6 and 10 dB: Acceptable]
    GM_Classify --> GM_Low[GM between 0 and 6 dB: Marginal]
    GM_Classify --> GM_Zero[GM equals 0 dB: Marginally stable]
    GM_Classify --> GM_Neg[GM less than 0 dB: Unstable]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block describes one computational or classification step. In an actual KTU exam, students are expected to *draw* the asymptotic Bode plot by hand, identify $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$ from the curves, and then compute the margins.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Gain Margin and Phase Margin. State the conditions for stability of a closed-loop system using the Bode plot.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Gain Margin (GM):** It is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased before the closed-loop system becomes unstable. Mathematically,
$$GM = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \text{ dB}$$
where $\omega_{pc}$ is the phase crossover frequency at which $\angle G(j\omega) = -180^\circ$.

- **Phase Margin (PM):** It is the additional phase lag that can be tolerated at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ (where $\vert G(j\omega) \vert = 0$ dB) before instability.
$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$$

- **Stability Condition:** The closed-loop system is **stable** if and only if $GM > 0$ dB **and** $PM > 0^\circ$. It is marginally stable if either margin is zero, and unstable if either margin is negative. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by a "corner frequency" in a Bode plot? List the slope and phase changes contributed by a simple pole of the form $\frac{1}{1 + j\omega T}$ at its corner frequency.**

**Model Answer:**

A **corner frequency** $\omega_c$ is the angular frequency at which the asymptotic magnitude plot of a Bode plot changes its slope. For a term $\frac{1}{1 + j\omega T}$, the corner frequency is $\omega_c = 1/T$ rad/s.

- **Slope change:** $-20$ dB/decade (the slope changes by $-20$ dB/dec after $\omega_c$).
- **Phase change:** The phase contribution transitions from $0^\circ$ to $-90^\circ$, with a transition rate of $-45^\circ$/decade centred at $\omega_c$ (i.e., the phase begins changing one decade below $\omega_c$ and completes one decade above).
- **Magnitude correction at $\omega_c$:** The actual magnitude is $-3$ dB relative to the asymptote intersection. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper]

For the open-loop transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{20}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the asymptotic Bode magnitude and phase plots. Clearly label all corner frequencies and slopes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the Gain Margin and Phase Margin. Comment on the closed-loop stability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Bode Form:** Factor each pole to have unit low-frequency gain:

$$G(s) = \frac{20}{2 \cdot 5 \cdot s\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{2}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{5}\right)} = \frac{2}{s\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{2}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{5}\right)}$$

Low-frequency gain: $20\log_{10}(2) = 6.02$ dB.

Corner frequencies: $\omega_1 = 2$ rad/s and $\omega_2 = 5$ rad/s.

**Step 2 — Slope Schedule:**

| Frequency Range (rad/s) | Net Slope (dB/decade) |
|---|---|
| $\omega < 2$ | $-20$ |
| $2 \le \omega < 5$ | $-40$ |
| $\omega \ge 5$ | $-60$ |

**Step 3 — Asymptote Values:**

- At $\omega = 1$: $M \approx 6.02 - 20\log_{10}(1) = 6.02$ dB
- At $\omega = 2$: $M \approx 6.02 - 20\log_{10}(2) = 0$ dB
- At $\omega = 5$: $M \approx 0 - 40\log_{10}(2.5) = -15.96$ dB
- At $\omega = 10$: $M \approx -15.96 - 60\log_{10}(2) = -33.98$ dB

**Step 4 — Phase Plot:**

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{5}\right)$$

- At $\omega = 0.1$: $\approx -90^\circ - 2.86^\circ - 1.15^\circ = -94^\circ$
- At $\omega = 1$: $\approx -90^\circ - 26.57^\circ - 11.31^\circ = -127.88^\circ$
- At $\omega = 5$: $\approx -90^\circ - 68.20^\circ - 45^\circ = -203.20^\circ$
- At $\omega = 10$: $\approx -90^\circ - 78.69^\circ - 63.43^\circ = -232.12^\circ$

The phase crosses $-180^\circ$ somewhere between $\omega = 1$ and $\omega = 5$. **(7 marks — plot sketch required)**

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Find $\omega_{pc}$** by solving:

$$-90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = -180^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = 90^\circ$$

Denominator of tangent-addition formula equals zero:

$$1 - \frac{\omega_{pc}}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_{pc}}{5} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 - \frac{\omega_{pc}^2}{10} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:**

$$\vert G(j\sqrt{10}) \vert = \frac{2}{\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 10/4} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 10/25}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{3.5} \cdot \sqrt{1.4}}$$

$$= \frac{2}{3.162 \times 1.871 \times 1.183} = \frac{2}{7.00} \approx 0.286$$

**Step 3 — Gain Margin:**

$$GM = -20 \log_{10}(0.286) = 20 \times 0.544 = +10.87 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Find $\omega_{gc}$** by solving $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:

$$\frac{2}{\omega_{gc} \sqrt{1+\omega_{gc}^2/4}\sqrt{1+\omega_{gc}^2/25}} = 1$$

By iteration: try $\omega_{gc} = 1.5$:
$$\frac{2}{1.5 \cdot \sqrt{1.5625} \cdot \sqrt{1.09}} = \frac{2}{1.5 \times 1.25 \times 1.044} = \frac{2}{1.957} = 1.022 \approx 1$$

So $\omega_{gc} \approx 1.5$ rad/s.

**Step 5 — Phase at $\omega_{gc}$:**

$$\angle G(j1.5) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.75) - \tan^{-1}(0.3) = -90^\circ - 36.87^\circ - 16.70^\circ = -143.57^\circ$$

**Step 6 — Phase Margin:**

$$PM = 180^\circ - 143.57^\circ = +36.43^\circ$$

**Step 7 — Stability Comment:**

Both $GM = +10.87$ dB $> 0$ and $PM = +36.43^\circ > 0$. The closed-loop system is **stable** with a moderate phase margin indicating a reasonably well-damped response. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

For the open-loop transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{K(1 + 0.5s)}{s(1 + s)(1 + 0.1s)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the asymptotic Bode magnitude and phase plots for $K = 1$. Indicate all slopes and corner frequencies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the value of $K$ at which the system becomes marginally stable. Hence determine the gain margin for $K = 1$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Corner Frequencies:**

- Zero at $\omega_z = 1/0.5 = 2$ rad/s
- Pole at $\omega_{p1} = 1$ rad/s
- Pole at $\omega_{p2} = 1/0.1 = 10$ rad/s
- Pole at origin ($-20$ dB/decade)

**Step 2 — Slope Schedule:**

| Frequency Range (rad/s) | Slope Change | Net Slope (dB/decade) |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega < 1$ | $0$ (initial) | $-20$ |
| $1 \le \omega < 2$ | $-20$ from pole at 1 | $-40$ |
| $2 \le \omega < 10$ | $+20$ from zero at 2 | $-20$ |
| $\omega \ge 10$ | $-20$ from pole at 10 | $-40$ |

**Step 3 — Phase Expression:**

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega)$$

The phase plot starts at $-90^\circ$ and ends at $-90^\circ - 90^\circ + 90^\circ - 90^\circ = -180^\circ$ asymptotically.

The phase approaches $-180^\circ$ only as $\omega \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Marginal Stability Condition:**

For marginal stability, the phase must reach $-180^\circ$ at a finite frequency. Examine the phase expression as $\omega \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{\omega \to \infty} \angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - 90^\circ + 90^\circ - 90^\circ = -180^\circ$$

This limit is reached asymptotically. For the phase to be exactly $-180^\circ$ at some finite $\omega_{pc}$, set:

$$-90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$

$$\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{pc}) = \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) - 90^\circ$$

Taking tangent of both sides:

$$\frac{0.5\omega_{pc}}{1} = \frac{\omega_{pc} + 0.1\omega_{pc}^2}{1 - 0.1\omega_{pc}^2} - \infty$$

As $\omega_{pc}$ increases, $\tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) \to 90^\circ$ when $1 - 0.1\omega_{pc}^2 = 0$, i.e., $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$ rad/s. At this point, RHS becomes undefined, matching the asymptote of $\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega_{pc})$ approach from below.

Therefore $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10}$ rad/s is the phase crossover for any $K$.

**Step 2 — Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ for $K = 1$:**

$$\vert G(j\sqrt{10}) \vert = \frac{1 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.25 \cdot 10}}{\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 10} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.01 \cdot 10}} = \frac{\sqrt{3.5}}{\sqrt{10} \cdot \sqrt{11} \cdot \sqrt{1.1}}$$

$$= \frac{1.871}{3.162 \times 3.317 \times 1.049} = \frac{1.871}{10.998} \approx 0.170$$

**Step 3 — Gain Margin for $K = 1$:**

$$GM = -20 \log_{10}(0.170) = 20 \times 0.7696 \approx +15.39 \text{ dB}$$

In linear scale: $GM_{\text{lin}} = 1/0.170 = 5.88$.

**Step 4 — Critical Gain for Marginal Stability:**

The system becomes marginally stable when the gain is multiplied by $5.88$:

$$K_{\text{critical}} = 5.88$$

Alternatively, the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ must equal 1, so:

$$K \cdot \vert G_0(j\sqrt{10}) \vert = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; K = \frac{1}{0.170} = 5.88$$

**Conclusion:** For $K = 1$, the system has $GM \approx 15.39$ dB and is **stable**. Marginal stability occurs at $K \approx 5.88$. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Bode Plot Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to Bode (time-constant) form** — If poles are not in $(1 + j\omega T)$ form, the corner frequencies will be wrongly identified, costing **2–3 marks** directly.
> 2. **Phase sign error in zeros vs. poles** — Zeros add positive phase, poles add negative phase. Sign mix-up leads to wrong $\omega_{pc}$, cascading into wrong GM. **Lose 3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Reading $\omega_{gc}$ from the asymptotic plot** — Many students use the asymptotic intersection instead of the *actual* curve. KTU expects the asymptotic value (which is acceptable) but mark deductions apply if the $-3$ dB corner correction is ignored at $\omega_{gc}$ when specified. **Lose 1 mark**.
> 4. **Unit confusion in $GM$** — Always quote GM in dB **and** mention the linear value when needed. **Lose 1 mark** if only one form is given.
> 5. **Stability comment missing** — A full 14-mark answer MUST end with an explicit stability statement. **Lose 1–2 marks** otherwise.
> 6. **Not labelling axes** — Magnitude axis in dB, phase axis in degrees, frequency axis in rad/s. **Lose 1 mark** for missing labels.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bode plots are semi-log plots**: $\log_{10}\omega$ on the horizontal axis, magnitude in dB / phase in degrees on the vertical axes.
- **Always convert** to time-constant form $G(s) = K \cdot \prod (1 + sT) / \prod (1 + sT_p) \cdot s^N$ before plotting.
- **Corner frequencies** $\omega_c = 1/T$ for simple poles/zeros; $\omega_n$ for quadratic terms.
- **Slope changes** at corners: $\pm 20$ dB/dec per simple factor, $\pm 40$ dB/dec per quadratic.
- **Phase contributions**:
  - Pole at origin: $-90^\circ \times N$ (constant)
  - Simple pole: $0^\circ$ to $-90^\circ$, slope $-45^\circ$/decade across corner
  - Simple zero: $0^\circ$ to $+90^\circ$, slope $+45^\circ$/decade
  - Quadratic: $0^\circ$ to $\mp 180^\circ$
- **Gain Margin (dB)** = $-20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ where $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$
- **Phase Margin** = $180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ where $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 0$ dB
- **Stability rule**: $GM > 0$ dB **AND** $PM > 0^\circ$ ⇒ Closed-loop stable.
- **Design targets**: $PM \approx 45^\circ$–$60^\circ$ and $GM \ge 6$ dB for robust performance.
- **Second-order approximation**: $\zeta \approx PM/100$ (rough), $\%M_p \approx 100 \cdot e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
- **Marginal stability** corresponds to $GM = 0$ dB or $PM = 0^\circ$ (sustained oscillation).
- **Final slope rule**: For Type-1 systems to be stable, the final slope at $\omega_{gc}$ should be $\ge -40$ dB/decade (i.e., $-20$ dB/dec with a $-20$ dB/dec change at most).
- **Ziegler-Nichols ultimate gain** $K_u = 1/\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ and ultimate period $T_u = 2\pi/\omega_{pc}$ are direct Bode-plot outputs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Phase and Non-Minimum Phase Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT521 - CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Minimum Phase and Non-Minimum Phase Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In control systems engineering, transfer functions are classified based on the location of their zeros (and poles) in the complex s-plane. A **Minimum Phase System** is one in which **all the zeros of the transfer function lie in the left-half of the s-plane (LHP)** (including the jω-axis, but excluding infinity), and the system has no poles in the right-half of the s-plane (RHP). For such systems, the magnitude and phase responses are uniquely and minimally linked by the **Bode gain-phase relationship**.

Conversely, a **Non-Minimum Phase System** is one in which **at least one zero (or pole) of the transfer function lies in the right-half of the s-plane (RHP)**. These systems exhibit a larger phase lag (or lead) for a given magnitude response compared to a minimum phase system with identical magnitude characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A system with **all poles in the LHP and all zeros in the RHP** is a **Stable Non-Minimum Phase System**. A system with **all poles in the RHP** is an **Unstable Minimum Phase System** (an unusual KTU exam favorite).
> 
> Standard benchmark: $G_1(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$ (Minimum Phase) and $G_2(s) = \dfrac{1}{1-s} = \dfrac{-1}{s-1}$ (Non-Minimum Phase — zero in RHP).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two friends running on a straight track toward a finish line, both taking exactly 5 seconds to finish:
- **Friend A (Minimum Phase):** Runs smoothly along the shortest path, without backtracking. His motion is direct and clean.
- **Friend B (Non-Minimum Phase):** Reaches the finish line in the same 5 seconds, but along the way takes a long detour backward (negative direction) before sprinting forward to compensate. Although he covers the same total distance in the same total time (same **magnitude** response), he wastes energy and causes extra phase shifts (he is "out of phase" with Friend A at almost every instant).

In Bode plot terms: both have the same $|G(j\omega)|$ curve, but the non-minimum phase system carries a **larger phase lag** at every frequency because of that RHP zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** A non-minimum phase plant is much harder to control (e.g., a missile whose tail-fin deflection initially causes the body to move the wrong way before correcting). The Nyquist and Bode plots of such systems violate the standard "intuitive" phase-magnitude correspondence.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Minimum Phase | Non-Minimum Phase |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero Location** | LHP ($\text{Re}(z) < 0$) | At least one zero in RHP ($\text{Re}(z) > 0$) |
| **Pole Location (Stability)** | LHP for stability | LHP for stability possible |
| **Phase at $\omega \to \infty$** | $-90°(n-m)$ (minimum) | Greater (more negative) lag |
| **DC Gain (s=0) Behavior** | Standard | Sign may invert (e.g., negative) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All-pass systems** are a special subset of non-minimum phase systems. They have the property $|G(j\omega)| = 1$ for all $\omega$ (magnitude is constant), but phase varies. Example: $G(s) = \dfrac{s-1}{s+1}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Distinction Exists

The concept arises from the **Bode gain-phase theorem** (also called the Bode integral relation), which states:

> For a **minimum phase** system, the **phase angle at any frequency is uniquely determined by the slope of the magnitude (in dB) versus log-frequency plot**, and vice-versa.

Mathematically, if we define the slope $S$ of the asymptotic magnitude plot in dB/decade at frequency $\omega_0$:

$$\phi(\omega_0) \approx \frac{\pi}{180°} \times \text{(average slope of magnitude curve around } \omega_0)$$

For a non-minimum phase system, **this correspondence breaks down**, and a single magnitude curve can correspond to multiple different phase plots, depending on zero locations.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Pole-Zero Inventory**
Catalog all poles $p_i$ and zeros $z_j$ of $G(s)$.

**Step 2 — LHP vs RHP Test**
- If every $\text{Re}(z_j) < 0$ and every $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ → **Minimum Phase**.
- If any $\text{Re}(z_j) > 0$ → **Non-Minimum Phase**.

**Step 3 — Magnitude Equivalence**
Two transfer functions with the **same magnitude response** $|G(j\omega)|$ can be constructed. One minimum phase, one non-minimum phase. The non-minimum phase version has extra phase lag.

**Step 4 — All-Pass Decomposition Theorem (KTU Favorite)**
Any non-minimum phase transfer function $G(s)$ can be factored as:

$$G(s) = G_{\text{mp}}(s) \cdot G_{\text{ap}}(s)$$

where $G_{\text{mp}}(s)$ is the **minimum phase equivalent** and $G_{\text{ap}}(s)$ is an **all-pass system** of the form $\dfrac{s-a}{s+a}$ (with $a>0$).

**Step 5 — Bode Plot Signature**
- Minimum phase: phase and magnitude "agree" (gentle slope → gentle phase).
- Non-minimum phase: phase "overshoots" negatively at the RHP-zero corner frequency $\omega = a$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General TF | $G(s) = K \dfrac{\prod_{j=1}^{m}(s-z_j)}{\prod_{i=1}^{n}(s-p_i)}$ | Standard form |
| 2 | Frequency Response | $G(j\omega) = K \dfrac{\prod (j\omega - z_j)}{\prod (j\omega - p_i)}$ | Substitute $s=j\omega$ |
| 3 | Magnitude (dB) | $\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega) \vert$ | dB |
| 4 | Phase | $\angle G(j\omega) = \sum \angle(j\omega - z_j) - \sum \angle(j\omega - p_i)$ | Degrees or radians |
| 5 | RHP Zero Phase Contribution | $\angle(j\omega - a) = 180° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)$, $a>0$ | Lags more than LHP zero |
| 6 | LHP Zero Phase Contribution | $\angle(j\omega + a) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)$ | Standard lead |
| 7 | All-Pass Form | $G_{\text{ap}}(s) = \dfrac{s-a}{s+a}$, $a>0$ | $\vert G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) \vert = 1$ for all $\omega$ |
| 8 | Decomposition | $G(s) = G_{\text{mp}}(s) \cdot G_{\text{ap}}(s)$ | All-pass factor $\dfrac{s-a}{s+a}$ |
| 9 | Minimum Phase Criterion | All $\text{Re}(z_j) \le 0$ & all $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ | Definition |
| 10 | Bode Gain-Phase Relation | $\phi(\omega_0) \approx \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \dfrac{dA/d\nu}{1+(\nu - \nu_0)^2}\,d\nu$ | Holds only for min-phase |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Do NOT confuse "minimum phase" with "minimum phase margin." They are entirely different concepts. Minimum phase refers to **zero location in s-plane**, while phase margin is a relative-stability measure on the Nyquist diagram.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft pitch dynamics can be non-minimum phase (e.g., the AH-64 helicopter, certain high-performance fighter aircraft) because elevator deflection first causes the body to pitch *opposite* to the commanded direction.
- **Chemical Process Control:** Distillation columns and certain heat-exchanger configurations exhibit inverse-response (non-minimum phase) behavior.
- **Robotics:** Flexible-link manipulators display non-minimum phase zeros due to vibrational modes.
- **Network Synthesis:** All-pass filters in telecommunications deliberately use non-minimum phase to achieve pure phase-shift without amplitude distortion (used in phase equalizers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Phase Contribution of an RHP Zero vs LHP Zero

**Problem Setup:** Compare the phase contribution of an LHP zero at $s = -a$ and an RHP zero at $s = +a$ (both with $a > 0$).

**Case 1: LHP Zero** — Term is $(s+a)$. Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$j\omega + a = a + j\omega$$

- **Magnitude:** $\sqrt{a^2 + \omega^2}$
- **Phase:** $\angle(a + j\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)$  → ranges from $0°$ (at $\omega=0$) to $+90°$ (at $\omega=\infty$).

**Case 2: RHP Zero** — Term is $(s - a)$. Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$j\omega - a = -a + j\omega$$

- **Magnitude:** $\sqrt{a^2 + \omega^2}$  (identical to Case 1!)
- **Phase:** $\angle(-a + j\omega) = 180° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)$  → ranges from $180°$ (at $\omega=0$) to $+90°$ (at $\omega=\infty$).

**Conclusion:** The magnitudes are identical at every frequency, but the RHP zero has an extra phase shift of $180° - 2\tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$, which approaches $180°$ at low frequencies and $0°$ at high frequencies. The RHP zero is "phase-laggier" in the mid-frequency range.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — All-Pass Magnitude Verification

**Goal:** Prove that $G_{\text{ap}}(s) = \dfrac{s-a}{s+a}$ has $\vert G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) \vert = 1$ for all $\omega > 0$.

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega - a}{j\omega + a}$$

**Step 1:** Compute magnitude of numerator.

$$\vert j\omega - a \vert = \sqrt{\omega^2 + a^2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute magnitude of denominator.

$$\vert j\omega + a \vert = \sqrt{\omega^2 + a^2}$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio.

$$\vert G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{\sqrt{\omega^2 + a^2}}{\sqrt{\omega^2 + a^2}} = 1 \quad \forall \omega$$

This confirms the **all-pass** property — magnitude is unity, only phase changes.

**Step 4:** Compute the phase.

$$\angle G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) = \angle(j\omega - a) - \angle(j\omega + a)$$

$$\angle G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) = \left[180° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)\right] - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\angle G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) = 180° - 2\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)}$$

- At $\omega = 0$: $\angle = 180° - 0 = 180°$
- At $\omega = a$: $\angle = 180° - 2(45°) = 90°$
- At $\omega \to \infty$: $\angle = 180° - 180° = 0°$

So the all-pass network gives pure phase roll-off, perfect for delay equalization.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Decomposing a Non-Minimum Phase System

**Problem:** Given a non-minimum phase transfer function with an RHP zero at $s = a$:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{K(s-a)}{(s+p_1)(s+p_2)} \quad \text{with } a, p_1, p_2 > 0$$

Show how to extract the minimum phase equivalent and the all-pass factor.

**Step 1:** Multiply and divide by $(s+a)$ to introduce a matched pair:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{K(s-a)}{(s+a)} \cdot \dfrac{(s+a)}{(s+p_1)(s+p_2)}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the two parts.

$$\underbrace{G_{\text{ap}}(s) = \dfrac{s-a}{s+a}}_{\text{All-pass factor (non-min phase)}} \quad \text{and} \quad \underbrace{G_{\text{mp}}(s) = \dfrac{K(s+a)}{(s+p_1)(s+p_2)}}_{\text{Minimum phase equivalent}}$$

**Step 3:** Verification.
- $G_{\text{mp}}(s)$ has all zeros in LHP ($s = -a$) and all poles in LHP ($s = -p_1, -p_2$) → minimum phase ✓
- $G_{\text{ap}}(s)$ is in standard all-pass form ✓
- $G(s) = G_{\text{mp}}(s) \cdot G_{\text{ap}}(s)$ ✓

**Step 4:** Magnitude check.

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \vert G_{\text{mp}}(j\omega) \vert \cdot \vert G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) \vert = \vert G_{\text{mp}}(j\omega) \vert \cdot 1 = \vert G_{\text{mp}}(j\omega) \vert$$

The minimum-phase equivalent alone determines the magnitude. The all-pass factor only contributes to the phase.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Table of Bode Behavior

For the canonical case $a = 1$, $K = 1$:

| Frequency $\omega$ | LHP Zero $\dfrac{1}{s+1}$ Phase | RHP Zero $\dfrac{-1}{s-1}$ Phase | Phase Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0.01$ | $+0.57°$ | $180° - 0.57° = 179.43°$ | $\approx 180°$ |
| $0.1$ | $+5.71°$ | $180° - 5.71° = 174.29°$ | $\approx 180°$ |
| $1$ | $+45°$ | $180° - 45° = 135°$ | $90°$ |
| $10$ | $+84.3°$ | $180° - 84.3° = 95.7°$ | $\approx 12°$ |
| $100$ | $+89.4°$ | $180° - 89.4° = 90.6°$ | $\approx 1.2°$ |

> The RHP zero has **almost the same magnitude** as the LHP zero but carries a **dramatically larger phase** at low frequencies — exactly the KTU "minimum phase" diagnostic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Transfer Function G s"] --> B["Inspect Zeros z sub j"]
    B --> C{"All zeros in LHP Re z less than 0?"}
    C -- Yes --> D{"All poles in LHP Re p less than 0?"}
    C -- No --> E["At least one zero in RHP"]
    D -- Yes --> F["MINIMUM PHASE STABLE"]
    D -- No --> G["MINIMUM PHASE UNSTABLE"]
    E --> H["NON MINIMUM PHASE"]
    H --> I{"Same magnitude as a min phase system?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["NON MINIMUM PHASE EQUIVALENT"]
    I -- No --> K["GENERIC NON MINIMUM PHASE"]
    J --> L["Can be written as G sub mp s times G sub ap s"]
    L --> M["G sub ap s is ALL PASS factor s minus a over s plus a"]
    F --> N["Bode phase and magnitude are uniquely linked"]
    H --> O["Extra phase lag exists beyond min phase"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — All-Pass Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input["Input R s"] --> Cascade1["G sub ap s equals s minus a over s plus a"]
    Cascade1 --> Cascade2["G sub mp s equals K s plus a over s plus p1 times s plus p2"]
    Cascade2 --> Output["Output Y s"]
    Cascade1 -. "Magnitude equals 1, phase varies 180 to 0 deg" .- Note1["All Pass Filter Block"]
    Cascade2 -. "Same magnitude as G s, only phase differs" .- Note2["Minimum Phase Equivalent Block"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Phase Response Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Frequency omega increases from 0 to infinity"] --> LHP["LHP zero: phase goes from 0 deg to plus 90 deg monotonically"]
    Start --> RHP["RHP zero: phase goes from 180 deg to plus 90 deg monotonically"]
    LHP --> Compare{"Compare at omega equals a"}
    RHP --> Compare
    Compare --> Result1["At omega equals a: LHP gives plus 45 deg, RHP gives plus 135 deg"]
    Result1 --> Result2["Phase difference equals 90 deg at corner frequency"]
    Result2 --> Result3["At high omega: both approach plus 90 deg difference shrinks to 0"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — Engineering Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Min vs Non Min Phase System"] --> B{"Application Domain"}
    B -- Aerospace --> C["Aircraft pitch dynamics: elevator deflection may cause initial opposite motion"]
    B -- Process Control --> D["Distillation columns and heat exchangers: inverse response behavior"]
    B -- Robotics --> E["Flexible link manipulators: vibrational RHP zeros"]
    B -- Communication --> F["Phase equalizers: all pass networks for group delay compensation"]
    C --> G["Use robust control methods: H infinity, sliding mode"]
    D --> H["Use Smith predictor or IMC for inverse response compensation"]
    E --> I["Use input shaping or feedback linearization"]
    F --> J["Use cascaded all pass sections for desired phase profile"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define a **Minimum Phase System**. State any two conditions that must be satisfied for a transfer function to qualify as a minimum phase system.

**Model Answer:**

A minimum phase system is a linear time-invariant (LTI) system in which **all zeros of the transfer function lie in the left-half of the s-plane (LHP)** (or on the jω-axis), and the system has **no poles in the right-half of the s-plane (RHP)** for stability.

**Two conditions:**
1. All poles of $G(s)$ must lie in the LHP, i.e., $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ for stability.
2. All zeros of $G(s)$ must lie in the LHP or on the jω-axis, i.e., $\text{Re}(z_j) \le 0$.

A unique correspondence exists between the magnitude response $|G(j\omega)|$ and the phase response $\angle G(j\omega)$ for such systems (Bode's gain-phase relationship).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

What is an **all-pass system**? Give one example and state one important property.

**Model Answer:**

An all-pass system is a special type of non-minimum phase transfer function whose **magnitude response is unity at every frequency**, i.e., $|G(j\omega)| = 1$ for all $\omega \ge 0$, but whose phase varies with frequency.

**Example:** 
$$G_{\text{ap}}(s) = \dfrac{s - 2}{s + 2}$$

**Important property:** 
- It passes all frequency components with **equal amplitude (magnitude = 1)**, but with different phase shifts.
- It is used in **phase equalization** and **group delay compensation** in communication and control systems.
- Its phase response is: $\angle G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) = 180° - 2\tan^{-1}(\omega/2)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q-A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Consider the transfer function:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{10(1 - 0.5s)}{(s+1)(s+2)}$$

**(i)** Identify whether the system is minimum phase or non-minimum phase. Justify your answer.
**(ii)** Decompose $G(s)$ into the product of a minimum phase transfer function $G_{\text{mp}}(s)$ and an all-pass transfer function $G_{\text{ap}}(s)$.
**(iii)** Determine $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ and $\angle G(j\omega)$ at $\omega = 0$, $\omega = 1$ rad/s, and $\omega = \infty$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback system has open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K(s+1)}{s^2(s+10)}$. For this system:
**(i)** State whether the system is minimum phase or non-minimum phase.
**(ii)** Sketch the Bode magnitude plot (asymptotic) and identify the type and order.
**(iii)** Comment on the stability of the closed-loop system.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a)(i) Identification:**

Rewrite: $G(s) = \dfrac{10(1 - 0.5s)}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{10 \cdot (-0.5)(s - 2)}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{-5(s - 2)}{(s+1)(s+2)}$

- Poles: $s = -1$, $s = -2$ → both in LHP ✓
- Zero: $s = +2$ → **RHP** ✗

Therefore, $G(s)$ is a **Non-Minimum Phase System** (it has a zero in the RHP at $s = 2$).

**(a)(ii) Decomposition into $G_{\text{mp}} \cdot G_{\text{ap}}$:**

We have the RHP zero at $s = 2$. The all-pass factor must pair this with an LHP zero at $s = -2$:

$$G_{\text{ap}}(s) = \dfrac{s - 2}{s + 2}$$

Now write:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{10(1 - 0.5s)}{(s+1)(s+2)}$$

Factor out and rearrange:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{(s-2)}{(s+2)} \cdot \dfrac{10(1 - 0.5s)(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+2)(s-2)}$$

Wait, let us do it cleanly. Start with $G(s) = \dfrac{-5(s-2)}{(s+1)(s+2)}$. Multiply and divide by $(s+2)$:

$$G(s) = \underbrace{\dfrac{s - 2}{s + 2}}_{G_{\text{ap}}(s)} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{-5(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+2)}}_{...}$$

But this introduces a pole-zero cancellation at $s = -2$. Cleaner: multiply numerator and denominator of original $G(s)$ by $(s+2)$? No — we need an $(s+2)$ in numerator.

Take $G(s) = \dfrac{10(1 - 0.5s)}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{-5(s - 2)}{(s+1)(s+2)}$.

Multiply and divide by $(s+2)$ — but $(s+2)$ is already in denominator. Insert $(s+2)$ in numerator:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{-5(s-2)}{(s+1)(s+2)} \cdot \dfrac{(s+2)}{(s+2)} = \dfrac{-5(s-2)(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+2)^2}$$

Then:

$$G(s) = \underbrace{\dfrac{s-2}{s+2}}_{G_{\text{ap}}(s)} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{-5(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+2)}}_{G_{\text{mp}}(s)} = \dfrac{s-2}{s+2} \cdot \dfrac{-5}{s+1}$$

Hmm — but $G_{\text{mp}}(s) = \dfrac{-5}{s+1}$ has no zero. That is fine; the original numerator's RHP zero got moved into the all-pass factor. The LHP zero that we added and cancelled is now in $G_{\text{mp}}$ as the constant $-5$ vs the previous RHP zero.

A cleaner KTU-style decomposition:

$$G(s) = \dfrac{s-2}{s+2} \cdot \dfrac{-5(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{s-2}{s+2} \cdot \dfrac{-5}{s+1}$$

$$\boxed{G_{\text{ap}}(s) = \dfrac{s-2}{s+2}, \qquad G_{\text{mp}}(s) = \dfrac{-5}{s+1}}$$

**Verification:** $G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega-2}{j\omega+2}$, $\vert G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{\sqrt{\omega^2+4}}{\sqrt{\omega^2+4}} = 1$ ✓

**Magnitude check:** $\vert G(j\omega) \vert = 1 \cdot \left\vert\dfrac{-5}{j\omega+1}\right\vert = \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{\omega^2+1}}$. From original: $\left\vert \dfrac{-5(j\omega-2)}{(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)} \right\vert = \dfrac{5\sqrt{\omega^2+4}}{\sqrt{\omega^2+1}\sqrt{\omega^2+4}} = \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{\omega^2+1}}$ ✓

**(a)(iii) Frequency Response Values:**

Using $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{-5(j\omega - 2)}{(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)}$.

| $\omega$ | $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ | $\angle G(j\omega)$ |
|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $\dfrac{5 \cdot 2}{1 \cdot 2} = 5$ | $180° - 0° - 0° = 180°$ |
| $1$ | $\dfrac{5\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{2}\sqrt{10}} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{20}} = 2.5$ | $180° - \tan^{-1}(0.5) - \tan^{-1}(1) - \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 180° - 26.57° - 45° - 26.57° = 81.86°$ |
| $\infty$ | $\dfrac{5\omega}{\omega \cdot \omega} = \dfrac{5}{\omega} \to 0$ | $180° - 90° - 90° - 90° = -90°$ |

**(b)(i) Identification of $G(s) = \dfrac{K(s+1)}{s^2(s+10)}$:**

- Poles: $s = 0$ (double), $s = -10$ → all in LHP or on jω-axis.
- Zero: $s = -1$ → in LHP.

**All zeros in LHP, all poles in non-RHP** → **Minimum Phase System**.

**(b)(ii) Bode Magnitude Sketch (Asymptotic):**

Rewrite in time-constant form: $G(s) = \dfrac{K(s+1)}{s^2(s+10)} = \dfrac{K}{10} \cdot \dfrac{(1+s)}{s^2(1+0.1s)}$

- **DC gain factor:** $K/10$
- **Type:** 2 (two poles at origin)
- **Corner frequencies:** $\omega = 1$ rad/s (zero), $\omega = 10$ rad/s (pole)
- **Initial slope (low $\omega$):** $-40$ dB/decade (due to $s^2$)
- **At $\omega = 1$:** slope changes by $+20$ dB/dec (zero) → becomes $-20$ dB/dec
- **At $\omega = 10$:** slope changes by $-20$ dB/dec (pole) → becomes $-40$ dB/dec again

**Asymptotic magnitude:**

| $\omega$ range | Slope (dB/dec) |
|---|---|
| $\omega < 1$ | $-40$ |
| $1 < \omega < 10$ | $-20$ |
| $\omega > 10$ | $-40$ |

**Bode magnitude plot (text representation):**

```
|G(jω)| dB
  |
  |\
  | \  slope -40 dB/dec
  |  \
  |   \____ slope -20 dB/dec (between ω=1 and ω=10)
  |        \   slope -40 dB/dec
  |         \________
  |___________________________ ω (log scale)
  0.1   1    10   100
```

**(b)(iii) Stability Comment:**

For a Type-2 system, the **closed-loop system is always unstable** for any $K > 0$ because the phase starts at $-180°$ (due to the two integrators) and the phase margin is zero or negative. To verify, the characteristic equation is:

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \Rightarrow s^2(s+10) + K(s+1) = 0$$

$$s^3 + 10s^2 + Ks + K = 0$$

Routh array:

| $s^3$ | $1$ | $K$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s^2$ | $10$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{10K - K}{10} = \dfrac{9K}{10}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | |

All first-column entries are positive for $K > 0$ — by Routh-Hurwitz, the system **is closed-loop stable** for $K > 0$. (My apologies — the conclusion is that the system is stable, but the **transient response will be sluggish** and require careful design.)

**Final Comment:** Although open-loop stable and minimum phase, this Type-2 plant has a slow response and may exhibit high overshoot. Derivative feedback or a lead compensator is typically added to improve phase margin.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q-B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** For the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{5(1-s)}{(s+2)(s+3)}$:
**(i)** Determine if the system is minimum phase or non-minimum phase.
**(ii)** Compute $|G(j\omega)|$ and $\angle G(j\omega)$ for $\omega = 0, 1, 2$ rad/s, and $\omega \to \infty$. Tabulate the results.
**(iii)** Rewrite $G(s)$ as a product of an all-pass and a minimum phase transfer function. Verify that the magnitude responses of $G(s)$ and $G_{\text{mp}}(s)$ are identical.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the phase response of a minimum phase system $G_1(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$ and a non-minimum phase system $G_2(s) = \dfrac{-1}{s-1}$:
**(i)** Plot the magnitude response $|G(j\omega)|$ for both and show they are equal in magnitude.
**(ii)** Compute the phase difference $\phi_2(\omega) - \phi_1(\omega)$ and identify at which frequency this difference is maximum.
**(iii)** Discuss the practical implications of non-minimum phase behavior in control system design.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a)(i) Identification:**

$G(s) = \dfrac{5(1-s)}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \dfrac{-5(s-1)}{(s+2)(s+3)}$

- Poles: $s = -2$, $s = -3$ → both in LHP ✓
- Zero: $s = 1$ → in RHP ✗

**Non-Minimum Phase System.**

**(a)(ii) Frequency Response Table:**

$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{5(1 - j\omega)}{(j\omega+2)(j\omega+3)}$

For each $\omega$, compute magnitude and phase:

- **$\omega = 0$:**  
  $G(j0) = \dfrac{5(1)}{2 \cdot 3} = \dfrac{5}{6} \approx 0.833$  
  $\angle G(j0) = \angle 1 - \angle 2 - \angle 3 = 0° - 0° - 0° = 0°$  
  (Wait, but the numerator is $5(1 - 0) = 5$, real positive. So phase = 0°.)

- **$\omega = 1$:**  
  $G(j1) = \dfrac{5(1 - j1)}{(2 + j1)(3 + j1)}$  
  Numerator: $\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2}$, $\angle = -45°$  
  $(2+j1)$: $\sqrt{5}$, $\angle = 26.57°$  
  $(3+j1)$: $\sqrt{10}$, $\angle = 18.43°$  
  Magnitude: $\dfrac{5\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}\sqrt{10}} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{50}} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{2}}{5\sqrt{2}} = 1$  
  Phase: $-45° - 26.57° - 18.43° = -90°$

- **$\omega = 2$:**  
  $G(j2) = \dfrac{5(1 - j2)}{(2 + j2)(3 + j2)}$  
  Numerator: $\sqrt{1+4} = \sqrt{5}$, $\angle = \tan^{-1}(-2/1) = -63.43°$  
  $(2+j2)$: $\sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2}$, $\angle = 45°$  
  $(3+j2)$: $\sqrt{13}$, $\angle = 33.69°$  
  Magnitude: $\dfrac{5\sqrt{5}}{2\sqrt{2}\sqrt{13}} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{5}}{2\sqrt{26}} = \dfrac{5 \times 2.236}{2 \times 5.099} = \dfrac{11.18}{10.198} \approx 1.096$  
  Phase: $-63.43° - 45° - 33.69° = -142.12°$

- **$\omega \to \infty$:**  
  Magnitude: $\dfrac{5\omega}{\omega \cdot \omega} = \dfrac{5}{\omega} \to 0$  
  Phase: $\angle(j\omega) - 2\angle(j\omega) = -\angle(j\omega) = -90°$ (numerator phase: $-90°$, each denominator: $90°$)

| $\omega$ (rad/s) | $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ | $\angle G(j\omega)$ |
|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $0.833$ | $0°$ |
| $1$ | $1.000$ | $-90°$ |
| $2$ | $1.096$ | $-142.12°$ |
| $\infty$ | $0$ | $-90°$ |

**(a)(iii) All-Pass Decomposition:**

RHP zero is at $s = 1$. Pair with LHP zero at $s = -1$:

$$G_{\text{ap}}(s) = \dfrac{s-1}{s+1}$$

Now, $G(s) = \dfrac{-5(s-1)}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \dfrac{s-1}{s+1} \cdot \dfrac{-5(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+3)}$

$$\boxed{G_{\text{ap}}(s) = \dfrac{s-1}{s+1}, \qquad G_{\text{mp}}(s) = \dfrac{-5(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+3)}}$$

**Magnitude Verification:**

- $|G(j\omega)| = \dfrac{5\sqrt{1+\omega^2}}{\sqrt{4+\omega^2}\sqrt{9+\omega^2}}$
- $|G_{\text{mp}}(j\omega)| = \dfrac{5\sqrt{1+\omega^2}}{\sqrt{4+\omega^2}\sqrt{9+\omega^2}}$ ✓ (identical)
- $|G_{\text{ap}}(j\omega)| = 1$ for all $\omega$ ✓

**(b)(i) Magnitude Comparison:**

- $G_1(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{j\omega+1}$: $|G_1| = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\omega^2+1}}$
- $G_2(j\omega) = \dfrac{-1}{j\omega-1}$: $|G_2| = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\omega^2+1}}$

**Equal magnitudes at all frequencies** — both systems are first-order with the same corner frequency $\omega = 1$ rad/s.

**(b)(ii) Phase Difference:**

- $\angle G_1(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega)$
- $\angle G_2(j\omega) = 180° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - 180° = -\tan^{-1}(\omega)$ — wait, let me recompute.

$G_2(j\omega) = \dfrac{-1}{j\omega-1}$. Numerator $-1 = e^{j180°}$. Denominator $j\omega-1$ has phase $\angle(-1 + j\omega) = 180° - \tan^{-1}(\omega)$ (in second quadrant).

So $\angle G_2(j\omega) = 180° - [180° - \tan^{-1}(\omega)] = \tan^{-1}(\omega)$.

Hmm, let me redo: $\angle(-1) - \angle(j\omega - 1) = 180° - (180° - \tan^{-1}(\omega/1)) = \tan^{-1}(\omega)$.

Therefore:

- $\angle G_1 = -\tan^{-1}(\omega)$ (lag, ranges $0°$ to $-90°$)
- $\angle G_2 = +\tan^{-1}(\omega)$ (lead, ranges $0°$ to $+90°$)

**Phase Difference:** $\Delta\phi = \angle G_2 - \angle G_1 = 2\tan^{-1}(\omega)$

| $\omega$ | $\angle G_1$ | $\angle G_2$ | $\Delta\phi$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $0°$ | $0°$ | $0°$ |
| $1$ | $-45°$ | $+45°$ | $90°$ (max change rate) |
| $10$ | $-84.3°$ | $+84.3°$ | $168.6°$ |
| $\infty$ | $-90°$ | $+90°$ | $180°$ (asymptote) |

**Maximum phase difference** occurs as $\omega \to \infty$, where $\Delta\phi \to 180°$. The most rapid change in $\Delta\phi$ occurs near $\omega = 1$ rad/s (the corner frequency).

**(b)(iii) Practical Implications of Non-Minimum Phase Behavior:**

1. **Difficult Control Design:** Non-minimum phase systems exhibit **inverse response** — initial response goes in the opposite direction of the commanded setpoint. Classical PID control cannot remove this fundamental lag without sacrificing stability.

2. **Reduced Phase Margin:** The extra phase lag makes it harder to achieve desired phase margin, requiring **lead compensators** or **higher-gain controllers** which may cause instability.

3. **Bandwidth Limitation:** To avoid exciting the inverse-response dynamics, controllers must operate at **lower bandwidth**, reducing the speed of response.

4. **Industrial Examples:**  
   - Boiler steam drums (shrink-and-swell effect)  
   - Distillation column temperature control  
   - High-speed aircraft pitch dynamics  
   - Flexible robotic arms

5. **Recommended Solutions:** Use **Smith Predictor**, **Internal Model Control (IMC)**, or **$H_\infty$ robust control** to handle the inverse-response zeros explicitly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing *minimum phase* with *minimum phase margin* — these are **completely different** concepts. (Penalty: 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to check **poles** along with zeros. A system can be minimum phase yet unstable if poles are in the RHP.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Incorrectly computing the phase of an RHP zero as $\tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$. The correct expression is $180° - \tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$ (because the complex number $-a + j\omega$ lies in the 2nd quadrant).
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** When decomposing $G(s)$ into $G_{\text{mp}} \cdot G_{\text{ap}}$, students often forget to **verify the magnitude** matches the original — always cross-check $|G_{\text{mp}}(j\omega)| = |G(j\omega)|$ at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \infty$.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** In the Bode phase plot, the contribution of an RHP zero is **lag** (not lead) at low frequencies, but rises to $90°$ at high frequencies — students often draw it incorrectly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **Minimum Phase = All zeros in LHP** (or on jω-axis) and all poles in LHP for stability.
- ☐ **Non-Minimum Phase = At least one zero (or pole) in RHP.**
- ☐ **All-Pass System:** $|G(j\omega)| = 1$ for all $\omega$. Canonical form: $G_{\text{ap}}(s) = \dfrac{s-a}{s+a}$, $a > 0$.
- ☐ **Decomposition Theorem:** Any $G(s) = G_{\text{mp}}(s) \cdot G_{\text{ap}}(s)$ — magnitude comes from $G_{\text{mp}}$, extra phase from $G_{\text{ap}}$.
- ☐ **RHP Zero Phase:** $\angle(j\omega - a) = 180° - \tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$.
- ☐ **LHP Zero Phase:** $\angle(j\omega + a) = +\tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$.
- ☐ **Bode Gain-Phase Relation:** Holds **only for minimum phase systems**.
- ☐ **Phase Difference Maximized:** $\Delta\phi \to 180°$ as $\omega \to \infty$ between LHP and RHP zeros at same frequency.
- ☐ **Inverse Response:** Real-world signature of non-minimum phase (initial motion in wrong direction).
- ☐ **Industrial Examples:** Aircraft pitch, distillation columns, helicopter dynamics, flexible manipulators, all-pass equalizers in telecom.
- ☐ **Counter-intuitive Stability:** A system with all RHP poles is **unstable** but can still be classified as minimum phase if zeros are in LHP — but this is rare in practice.
- ☐ **DC Gain Sign Trick:** A single RHP zero causes the **DC phase to be 180°**, flipping the sign of the low-frequency response.
- ☐ **Always Verify:** After all-pass decomposition, check $|G_{\text{mp}}(j0)| = |G(j0)|$ and $|G_{\text{mp}}(j\infty)| = |G(j\infty)|$.
- ☐ **Common Exam Trap:** "Minimum phase system = minimum energy system" — wrong; minimum phase refers to phase angle, not energy.
- ☐ **Mnemonic:** **"MP = Magnitude Predicts Phase"** — only true for minimum phase systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Space Modelling: Concepts of State
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT521 - CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** State Space Modelling: Concepts of State

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of State, State Variables, and State Space

In the context of **Control Systems Engineering**, the classical (transfer function) approach describes a system only in terms of its input-output behavior. The **State Space approach** is a modern, time-domain technique that provides a complete internal description of a system by tracking a minimum set of internal variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State of a System (KTU 2024 Official Definition):**
> The **state of a system** is the smallest set of variables (called *state variables*) such that the knowledge of these variables at an initial time $t = t_0$, together with the knowledge of the input for $t \geq t_0$, completely determines the behaviour of the system for any time $t \geq t_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **State Variable (Strict KTU Terminology):**
> A **state variable** is any variable in a set of variables that fully describes the dynamic state of the system. The number of state variables required equals the **order of the system** (highest power of the differential operator $s$ in the transfer function, excluding any pole-zero cancellations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Vector and State Space:**
> - **State Vector $\vec{x}(t)$:** The column vector formed by arranging all the $n$ independent state variables of an $n^{th}$ order system.
> - **State Space:** The $n$-dimensional space whose coordinate axes are the state variables $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$. Any point in this space represents a particular *state* of the system.

For an $n^{th}$ order system, we have $n$ state variables. For an $r$-input, $m$-output system, the standard state space form is written as:

$$
\vec{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \\ \vdots \\ x_n(t) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}, \quad \vec{u}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} u_1(t) \\ \vdots \\ u_r(t) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{r}, \quad \vec{y}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} y_1(t) \\ \vdots \\ y_m(t) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}
$$

The two governing matrix equations that mathematically encapsulate the entire system are:

$$
\dot{\vec{x}}(t) = A\,\vec{x}(t) + B\,\vec{u}(t) \quad \text{(State Equation)}
$$

$$
\vec{y}(t) = C\,\vec{x}(t) + D\,\vec{u}(t) \quad \text{(Output Equation)}
$$

where:
- $A$ is the **$n \times n$ System Matrix** (dynamics matrix)
- $B$ is the **$n \times r$ Input Matrix** (control matrix)
- $C$ is the **$m \times n$ Output Matrix** (sensor matrix)
- $D$ is the **$m \times r$ Feedforward (Direct Transmission) Matrix**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "GPS Navigation" Picture

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Driving a Car on Kerala's Hill Highway**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car from **Thiruvananthapuram to Kasaragod**. Your friend (a passenger) is blindfolded and asks, *"Where are we right now?"*
>
> - If you only say *"We have been driving for 6 hours"*, that is the **input-output (classical) description** — it gives one piece of information.
> - However, to **completely determine your future position** (even if you fall asleep or get a flat tyre), your friend needs to know:
>   1. Your **current Latitude coordinate** $x_1(t)$ — *where you are*
>   2. Your **current Longitude coordinate** $x_2(t)$ — *where you are*
>   3. Your **current speed (magnitude of velocity)** $x_3(t)$ — *how fast*
>   4. Your **current heading (direction)** $x_4(t)$ — *which way*
>
> These 4 numbers form your **state vector** $\vec{x}(t) = [x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4]^T$. With these and the **steering/accelerator/brake inputs** $u(t)$, anyone can **predict your future position for all $t > t_0$**.
>
> The *state space* is simply the 4-dimensional mathematical map where every possible combination of (latitude, longitude, speed, heading) is a point — your car traces a path through this 4-D space as you drive.

## 1.3 Why State Space? Advantages over Classical Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Advantages of State Space Modelling (KTU 2024 High-Yield Point):**
>
> 1. **Handles Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO)** systems naturally (classical methods struggle).
> 2. **Time-domain based** — no need for Laplace transforms initially.
> 3. **Provides internal description** — initial conditions are explicitly part of the model.
> 4. Suitable for **non-linear, time-varying, and digital (sampled-data)** systems.
> 5. Foundation for **modern optimal control, Kalman filtering, and adaptive control**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Choices for State Variables

In engineering practice, we normally choose state variables as elements that store energy. These are:
- **Inductance $L$** in electrical circuits $\Rightarrow$ current $i_L$ stores magnetic energy $\frac{1}{2}Li_L^2$
- **Capacitance $C$** in electrical circuits $\Rightarrow$ voltage $v_C$ stores electric energy $\frac{1}{2}Cv_C^2$
- **Mass $M$** in mechanical systems $\Rightarrow$ velocity $v$ stores kinetic energy $\frac{1}{2}Mv^2$
- **Spring $K$** in mechanical systems $\Rightarrow$ displacement $x$ stores potential energy $\frac{1}{2}Kx^2$

> [!NOTE]
> The total number of **independent energy-storage elements** equals the **order of the system** $n$ and therefore equals the **number of state variables** required.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Analysis of the State Space Model

The complete state-space description requires four matrices, often called the **ABCD-quadruple** $(A, B, C, D)$ or in some references the **$\Sigma$ (Sigma) system description**. Let us dissect the role of each matrix:

| Matrix | Dimension | Name | Physical Interpretation | Effect on System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A$ | $n \times n$ | System / State Matrix | Internal coupling between state derivatives and states | Determines **stability, natural modes, eigenvalues** of the system |
| $B$ | $n \times r$ | Input / Control Matrix | How the external inputs $u(t)$ influence each state derivative | Determines the **controllability reach** |
| $C$ | $m \times n$ | Output / Sensor Matrix | How the states combine to form measured outputs | Determines the **observability** of internal states |
| $D$ | $m \times r$ | Feedforward Matrix | Direct input-to-output coupling (bypassing dynamics) | Usually $D = 0$ for strictly proper systems (most physical systems) |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Identifying State Variables

> [!TIP]
> **The KTU Board Examiner's "Energy-Element" Method:**
>
> For **any** physical system (mechanical, electrical, electromechanical, thermal), follow this strict protocol:
>
> 1. **Step 1:** Identify every independent energy-storage element. (Inductors, capacitors, masses, springs.)
> 2. **Step 2:** Assign one state variable per element — typically the **across variable** for the element:
>    - Inductor $\rightarrow$ current through it, $i_L(t)$
>    - Capacitor $\rightarrow$ voltage across it, $v_C(t)$
>    - Mass $\rightarrow$ velocity, $v(t) = \dot{x}(t)$
>    - Spring $\rightarrow$ displacement, $x(t)$
> 3. **Step 3:** Verify that the system order $n$ (highest derivative in governing ODE, or highest power of $s$ in denominator of $G(s)$) matches the number of energy-storage elements. If not, there are dependent elements.
> 4. **Step 4:** Write the state equation $\dot{\vec{x}} = A\vec{x} + B\vec{u}$ by applying **KVL/KCL** (for electrical) or **Newton's Laws** (for mechanical).
> 5. **Step 5:** Write the output equation $\vec{y} = C\vec{x} + D\vec{u}$ by identifying which combinations of states (and possibly inputs) are measured.

## 2.3 The Notion of "Minimal" State Representation

A system may have multiple valid state-space representations (an infinite family, in fact, related by similarity transformations). The representation with the **fewest state variables** equal to the order $n$ is called the **minimal realization**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:**
> Choosing a state variable that is *not independent*. For example, in a series RLC circuit, the loop equation couples $i_L$ and $v_C$ — you must still choose BOTH as states because the system order is 2. Choosing only the current is a *reduced* (lower-order) approximation, not the true state.

## 2.4 The State Space as a Mathematical Manifold

> [!NOTE]
> **State Trajectory:**
> As time progresses, the state vector $\vec{x}(t)$ moves through the $n$-dimensional state space, tracing a curve called the **state trajectory** or **state-space trajectory**. Plotting $x_1(t)$ vs $x_2(t)$ for a 2nd order system is called a **phase portrait** — a powerful visualization tool for understanding system behaviour without explicitly plotting time response.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | State Equation | $\dot{\vec{x}}(t) = A\,\vec{x}(t) + B\,\vec{u}(t)$ | First-order vector ODE; $A$ is $n \times n$, $B$ is $n \times r$ |
| 2 | Output Equation | $\vec{y}(t) = C\,\vec{x}(t) + D\,\vec{u}(t)$ | $C$ is $m \times n$, $D$ is $m \times r$ |
| 3 | State Vector | $\vec{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ | $n$ elements for an $n^{th}$ order system |
| 4 | Initial Condition | $\vec{x}(t_0) = \vec{x}_0$ | Classical methods ignore this; state space uses it explicitly |
| 5 | Number of State Variables | $n$ = order of system = # of independent energy-storage elements | $n$ is fixed; choice of which physical quantities = flexible |
| 6 | Single-Input Case (SISO) | $B$ is $n \times 1$, $C$ is $1 \times n$, $D$ is $1 \times 1$ scalar | Most undergraduate problems are SISO |
| 7 | Strictly Proper System | $D = 0$ | Almost all physical systems with no direct algebraic path |
| 8 | Block Diagram of State Model | $\dot{\vec{x}} \rightarrow [sI-A]^{-1} \rightarrow \vec{x}$ | The state evolves via integrating matrix $[sI-A]^{-1}$ |
| 9 | Transfer Function from SS | $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$ | Converts state space $\rightarrow$ classical transfer function |
| 10 | Eigenvalues of A | $\det(sI - A) = 0$ | Roots equal poles of $G(s)$; govern natural response |
| 11 | Phase Variable Form | Companion matrix $A$ has sub-diagonal of 1s, last row = $-a_n, \ldots, -a_0$ | Standard form from ODE $\dddot{y}+a_2\ddot{y}+a_1\dot{y}+a_0 y = b_0 u$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU BOARD NOTE (Frequently Tested):**
> The state equation and output equation are **simultaneous**. You cannot write a state equation alone or output equation alone and call it a "state space model". Both are required, plus the initial condition $\vec{x}(0)$, to completely specify a system.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

State space modelling underpins:
- **Aerospace:** Designing autopilot systems for aircraft (NASA, ISRO) using full 6-DOF rigid body dynamics $\Rightarrow$ $\vec{x}$ contains 12 states (position + velocity in 3 axes + 3 Euler angles + 3 angular rates).
- **Robotics:** Manipulator arm control with multiple joint angles and velocities.
- **Power Systems:** Multi-machine grid modelling for stability analysis (PMU data).
- **Automotive:** Electronic Stability Control (ESC) — yaw rate, lateral velocity, steering angle as states.
- **Process Control:** Multivariable distillation columns with coupled temperatures and compositions.
- **Econometrics:** Dynamic models of national economies (state = capital stock, employment, etc.).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From a Single-Input Linear ODE to State Space (Phase Variable Form)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Board Exams and appears almost every year. We will exhaustively derive it.

**Given:** A general $n^{th}$ order linear, time-invariant (LTI) differential equation with constant coefficients:

$$
\frac{d^{n}y(t)}{dt^{n}} + a_{n-1}\frac{d^{n-1}y(t)}{dt^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_{1}\frac{dy(t)}{dt} + a_{0}\,y(t) = b_0\,u(t)
$$

We are told the input $u(t)$ does not contain any derivatives of $u$ (the single-input case, no input derivatives — to keep the derivation clean). Let us work this out for $n = 3$ first, then generalize.

### Sub-case: Third-Order System ($n = 3$)

**Given ODE:**

$$
\dddot{y}(t) + a_2\ddot{y}(t) + a_1\dot{y}(t) + a_0 y(t) = b_0 u(t)
$$

**Step 1 — Define the state variables (Phase Variable Convention).**

The **phase variable form** assigns the state variables in cascading order starting from the highest-order derivative's "integrator chain":

$$
x_1(t) = y(t)
$$

$$
x_2(t) = \dot{y}(t) = \dot{x}_1(t)
$$

$$
x_3(t) = \ddot{y}(t) = \dot{x}_2(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate each state variable to obtain $\dot{x}_i$.**

From the definitions:

$$
\dot{x}_1(t) = x_2(t)
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2(t) = x_3(t)
$$

$$
\dot{x}_3(t) = \dddot{y}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Express the highest derivative in terms of states and input using the original ODE.**

Solving the original ODE for $\dddot{y}(t)$:

$$
\dddot{y}(t) = -a_2\ddot{y}(t) - a_1\dot{y}(t) - a_0 y(t) + b_0 u(t)
$$

Substituting $x_1 = y$, $x_2 = \dot{y}$, $x_3 = \ddot{y}$:

$$
\dot{x}_3(t) = -a_0\,x_1(t) - a_1\,x_2(t) - a_2\,x_3(t) + b_0\,u(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the State Vector and write matrix form.**

$$
\vec{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \\ x_3(t) \end{bmatrix} \quad ; \quad \dot{\vec{x}}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1(t) \\ \dot{x}_2(t) \\ \dot{x}_3(t) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\dot{\vec{x}}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ b_0 \end{bmatrix} u(t)
$$

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ b_0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the Output Equation.**

If the output is exactly $y(t)$, then $y = x_1$:

$$
\vec{y}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + 0 \cdot u(t)
$$

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = [0]
$$

This is the **Phase Variable Canonical Form** (also called the **Observable Canonical Form** when output equation is $y = x_1$).

### Generalization to $n^{th}$ Order

For an $n^{th}$ order ODE with no input derivatives:

$$
\dddot{y}+\cdots+a_1\dot{y}+a_0 y = b_0 u
$$

The matrices take the standard companion structure:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}_{n \times n}
$$

$$
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ b_0 \end{bmatrix}_{n \times 1}
$$

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}_{1 \times n}
$$

$$
D = [0]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** The matrix $A$ has a super-diagonal of 1s (the "integrator chain") and a **last row of $-a_0, -a_1, \ldots, -a_{n-1}$** (in increasing index order, right to left).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Second-Order RLC Series Circuit

> [!TIP]
> **Example (Frequently Asked in KTU):** A series RLC circuit has input voltage $v_i(t)$, output voltage across the capacitor $v_C(t)$. The loop equation gives:
>
> $$L\frac{di(t)}{dt} + Ri(t) + v_C(t) = v_i(t), \quad i(t) = C\frac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$$
>
> Derive the state space model.

**Step 1 — Choose state variables based on energy storage.**

- Inductor stores energy in current $\Rightarrow$ state variable $x_1 = i_L(t)$
- Capacitor stores energy in voltage $\Rightarrow$ state variable $x_2 = v_C(t)$

**Step 2 — Apply KVL to obtain $\dot{x}_1$.**

From the loop equation, $L\dot{i}_L = v_i - Ri_L - v_C$:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = \dot{i}_L = -\frac{R}{L}\,x_1 - \frac{1}{L}\,x_2 + \frac{1}{L}\,v_i
$$

**Step 3 — Use the capacitor relation to obtain $\dot{x}_2$.**

From $i_L = C\dot{v}_C$:

$$
\dot{x}_2 = \dot{v}_C = \frac{1}{C}\,i_L = \frac{1}{C}\,x_1
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble matrix form.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -R/L & -1/L \\ 1/C & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1/L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} v_i
$$

**Step 5 — Write the output equation. Output $y = v_C = x_2$:**

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + [0]\,v_i
$$

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} -R/L & -1/L \\ 1/C & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1/L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = [0]
$$

**Verification via transfer function approach (sanity check):**

The transfer function $G(s) = V_C(s)/V_i(s)$ for series RLC is classically known as:

$$
G(s) = \frac{1/(LC)}{s^2 + (R/L)s + 1/(LC)}
$$

Now compute using the state-space formula $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$:

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s + R/L & 1/L \\ -1/C & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\det(sI - A) = s\left(s + \frac{R}{L}\right) - \left(\frac{1}{L}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{C}\right) = s^2 + \frac{R}{L}s + \frac{1}{LC}
$$

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det} \begin{bmatrix} s & -1/L \\ 1/C & s + R/L \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
C(sI - A)^{-1}B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\det} \begin{bmatrix} s & -1/L \\ 1/C & s + R/L \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1/L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\det} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s/L \\ 1/(LC) \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1/(LC)}{s^2 + (R/L)s + 1/(LC)}
$$

This matches the classical result exactly. **Verification successful.**

## 3.3 The Significance of $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ — Eigenstructure

The state matrix $A$ alone governs the **natural (unforced, $u=0$) response** of the system. The general solution to $\dot{\vec{x}} = A\vec{x}$ with initial condition $\vec{x}(0) = \vec{x}_0$ is:

$$
\vec{x}(t) = e^{A t}\,\vec{x}_0 = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{(sI - A)^{-1}\right\}\vec{x}_0
$$

The **characteristic equation** of the system is:

$$
\det(sI - A) = 0
$$

The roots $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n$ are the **eigenvalues of $A$** and are identical to the **poles of the transfer function** $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$ for minimal realizations. This connection is what links classical control and modern control theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Quick-Check (Board Favourite):**
> The system is **asymptotically stable** if and only if **all eigenvalues of $A$** have **strictly negative real parts** $\Rightarrow$ $\text{Re}(s_i) < 0$ for all $i = 1, \ldots, n$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the State Space Model

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **complete functional architecture** of a state space system, showing how the matrices $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ interact with the state vector $\vec{x}$, input $\vec{u}$, and output $\vec{y}$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputVec["Input Vector u(t)"]:::inputStyle
    bMatrix["Input Matrix B (n x r)"]:::matrixStyle
    aMatrix["State Matrix A (n x n)"]:::matrixStyle
    integrator["Integrator Chain sI-A"]:::dynStyle
    stateVec["State Vector x(t)"]:::stateStyle
    cMatrix["Output Matrix C (m x n)"]:::matrixStyle
    dMatrix["Feedforward Matrix D (m x r)"]:::matrixStyle
    outputVec["Output Vector y(t)"]:::outputStyle
    summer1["Summing Junction +"]:::sumStyle
    summer2["Summing Junction +"]:::sumStyle
    derivative["Derivative x'(t)"]:::dynStyle

    inputVec --> bMatrix
    bMatrix --> summer1
    stateVec --> aMatrix
    aMatrix --> summer1
    summer1 --> derivative
    derivative --> integrator
    integrator --> stateVec
    stateVec --> cMatrix
    inputVec --> dMatrix
    dMatrix --> summer2
    cMatrix --> summer2
    summer2 --> outputVec

    classDef inputStyle fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef matrixStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stateStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sumStyle fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dynStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **blue** blocks $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ are matrices that "shape" the signals.
- The **green** state vector $\vec{x}$ is the central information carrier.
- The **purple** integrator block $sI-A$ and the derivative block $\dot{\vec{x}}$ together implement the differential equation.
- The **yellow** summing junctions add the contributions from the state path and input path.

## 4.2 Phase Portrait Sketch for a 2nd-Order Undamped System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHASE_PORTRAIT["Phase Portrait: 2nd Order Undamped (eigenvalues on imaginary axis)"]
        start1["x1 = 1, x2 = 0"] --> p1["(1, 0)"]
        p1 --> p2["(0, 1)"]
        p2 --> p3["(-1, 0)"]
        p3 --> p4["(0, -1)"]
        p4 --> start1
    end
    classDef nodeStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class start1,p1,p2,p3,p4 nodeStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> The phase portrait shows **closed orbits (ellipses/circles)** for an undamped system — neither converging to nor diverging from the origin. This is the hallmark of **marginally stable** behaviour.

## 4.3 Energy-Storage-to-State-Variable Mapping (Systematic Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Physical System Given"]:::initStyle
    identify["Identify all energy-storage elements (L, C, M, K)"]:::stepStyle
    count["Count independent elements = n (system order)"]:::stepStyle
    assign["Assign one state variable per element: x1 = iL, x2 = vC, x3 = v, x4 = x"]:::stepStyle
    applyKVL["Apply KVL / KCL / Newton / Lagrange to get differential equations"]:::stepStyle
    solveHighest["Solve for the highest derivative: d^n x_i / dt^n = f(x1,...,xn, u)"]:::stepStyle
    assemble["Assemble matrices A, B, C, D"]:::stepStyle
    verify["Verify: Compute G(s) = C(sI-A)⁻¹B + D matches known TF"]:::stepStyle
    endNode["Final State Space Model Complete"]:::finalStyle

    start --> identify --> count --> assign --> applyKVL --> solveHighest --> assemble --> verify --> endNode
    classDef initStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef stepStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef finalStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

This flowchart is the **algorithmic recipe** a student must follow during the KTU exam to derive state space models from any physical system description.

## 4.4 State Space vs Transfer Function: Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ode["n-th order ODE in y(t)"]:::physStyle
    diffEq["Differential Equation in Time Domain"]:::physStyle
    laplaceTF["Apply Laplace Transform"]:::physStyle
    tfForm["Transfer Function G(s)"]:::tfStyle
    stateSpace["State Space: x' = Ax + Bu, y = Cx + Du"]:::ssStyle
    canonical["Phase Variable / Controllable / Diagonal / Observer Forms"]:::canonStyle

    ode --> diffEq --> laplaceTF --> tfForm
    ode --> diffEq --> stateSpace
    tfForm -.->|"Conversion via C(sI-A)⁻¹B+D"| stateSpace
    stateSpace --> canonical
    tfForm -.->|"Multiple Realizations"| canonical
    classDef physStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tfStyle fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ssStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef canonStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board Hint:** The dotted arrows indicate that the transformation is *not unique* — there are multiple state space realizations for a single transfer function, just as there are multiple block diagram realizations of the same TF.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are short-answer conceptual questions typical of KTU's 3-mark Part A. They test definitional clarity and conceptual understanding (Bloom's Level: Remember + Understand).

### Question 1: State Variable Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A]`
