```mermaid
    stateX2 -- "x2" --> intA
    intA --> stateX1
    stateX1 -- "x1" --> intA
    stateX1 --> yOut

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sumNode fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef integNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stateNode fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gainNode fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each integrator box represents one state variable. The signal flowing right-to-left along the bottom is the "state vector" itself — each integrator output becomes the next state. The leftmost summing junction collects the gains $-\frac{a_i}{a_0}$ and $\frac{1}{a_0}$ to form $\dot{x}_n$, which feeds back into the topmost integrator.

---

### 4.2 Conversion Process Flowchart (From ODE to State-Space)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: nth Order Linear ODE"]:::startNode
    B["Identify coefficients a0, a1, ..., an"]:::procNode
    C{"Is a0 = 1?"}:::decisionNode
    D["Divide entire ODE by a0 to normalize"]:::procNode
    E["Define state variables: x1=y, x2=dy/dt, ..., xn=y^(n-1)"]:::procNode
    F["Write first n-1 state equations: dx_i/dt = x_{i+1}"]:::procNode
    G["Isolate y^(n) from ODE and substitute state variables"]:::procNode
    H["Assemble A, B matrices from state equations"]:::procNode
    I["Form C = [1 0 ... 0], D = [0]"]:::procNode
    J["Final: dx/dt = A x + B u, y = C x + D u"]:::endNode

    A --> B --> C
    C -- "No" --> D --> E
    C -- "Yes" --> E
    E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J

    classDef startNode fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef procNode fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner-friendly tip:** When writing your ESE answer, **always show this conversion logic as numbered steps** in your solution. KTU examiners explicitly award partial marks (typically 1–2 out of 7) for clearly stating the state-variable definitions before substituting into matrices.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "state" of a dynamic system. Why is it necessary to represent a system in state-space form?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *State:* The state of a dynamic system is the minimum set of variables (called state variables) such that knowledge of these variables at $t = t_0$, together with the input for $t \geq t_0$, completely determines the future behaviour of the system for $t \geq t_0$.
- **(1 mark)** State variables form a vector $\mathbf{x}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ where $n$ is the order of the governing differential equation.
- **(1 mark)** Necessity: Classical transfer-function methods apply only to LTI SISO systems. State-space methods naturally extend to **MIMO, nonlinear, and time-varying** systems and provide direct access to internal behaviour for analysis (controllability, observability) and design (pole placement, observers).

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**For the system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}u$, $y = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}u$, what are the standard dimensions of $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{D}$ when the system is SISO with $n$ states?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- $\mathbf{A}$ is $n \times n$ (system matrix) — **(1 mark)**
- $\mathbf{B}$ is $n \times 1$ (input matrix, column vector) — **(1 mark)**
- $\mathbf{C}$ is $1 \times n$ (output matrix, row vector) — **(0.5 marks)**
- $\mathbf{D}$ is $1 \times 1$ (scalar feed-through term) — **(0.5 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign error in the last row of $\mathbf{A}$:** Students frequently write $-a_1$ in column 1 instead of $-a_n$. Remember the **REVERSE order** — the coefficient of the **lowest** derivative ($a_n$) goes in **column 1**, and the coefficient of the **highest-order derivative** ($a_1$) goes in **column $n$**.
> 2. **Forgetting the division by $a_0$:** When $a_0 \neq 1$, the last row entries and the $\mathbf{B}$ matrix entry must use $a_i/a_0$, not just $a_i$. This costs **2 marks** typically.
> 3. **Confusing $\mathbf{C}$ with row index:** The output $y$ is **always** $x_1 = y$, so $\mathbf{C}$ is **always** $[1, 0, 0, \ldots, 0]$ for phase-variable form.
> 4. **Not specifying state variables before writing matrices:** KTU evaluators deduct **1 mark** if you directly write the matrices without first stating $x_1 = y$, $x_2 = \dot{y}$, etc.

---

#### **Question B1 — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Obtain the state-space representation in **phase-variable form** for the system described by:

$$\dddot{y} + 5\ddot{y} + 8\dot{y} + 4y = 3u(t)$$

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients (1 mark):**
$$a_0 = 1, \quad a_1 = 5, \quad a_2 = 8, \quad a_3 = 4, \quad n = 3$$

**Step 2 — Define state variables (2 marks):**
$$x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}$$

**Step 3 — Write first two state equations (1 mark):**
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = x_3$$

**Step 4 — Form the last state equation using the ODE (2 marks):**
From the ODE: $\dddot{y} = -5\ddot{y} - 8\dot{y} - 4y + 3u(t)$
$$\dot{x}_3 = -4x_1 - 8x_2 - 5x_3 + 3u(t)$$

**Step 5 — Assemble matrices (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -4 & -8 & -5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
\mathbf{C} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{D} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b)** A system is described by the transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{2}{s^3 + 4s^2 + 5s + 2}$$

Obtain its state-space representation in **controllable canonical form** and verify that the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ are the poles of $G(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert transfer function to ODE (1 mark):**
$$(s^3 + 4s^2 + 5s + 2)Y(s) = 2U(s)$$
$$\dddot{y} + 4\ddot{y} + 5\dot{y} + 2y = 2u(t)$$

**Step 2 — Identify coefficients and define states (2 marks):**
$a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = 4$, $a_2 = 5$, $a_3 = 2$.
$$x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}$$

**Step 3 — State equations and matrices (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -5 & -4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
\mathbf{C} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{D} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify eigenvalues (2 marks):**
The characteristic polynomial of $\mathbf{A}$ is $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) &= \det \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & s & -1 \\ 2 & 5 & s+4 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= s \cdot \det \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 5 & s+4 \end{bmatrix} - (-1) \cdot \det \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 2 & s+4 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= s[s(s+4) + 5] + 1 \cdot [0 + 2] \\[6pt]
&= s^3 + 4s^2 + 5s + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting this equal to zero gives $s^3 + 4s^2 + 5s + 2 = 0$, which is **exactly** the denominator of $G(s)$. ✓ Hence the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ are the poles of $G(s)$.

---

#### **Question B1 — Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** For the second-order system:

$$2\ddot{y} + 3\dot{y} + 5y = u(t)$$

obtain the state-space representation in phase-variable form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Normalize the ODE (1 mark):** Divide both sides by $a_0 = 2$:
$$\ddot{y} + 1.5\dot{y} + 2.5y = 0.5u(t)$$

**Step 2 — Coefficients (1 mark):** $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = 1.5$, $a_2 = 2.5$, $n = 2$.

**Step 3 — State variable definition (1 mark):**
$$x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}$$

**Step 4 — State equations (2 marks):**
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -2.5 x_1 - 1.5 x_2 + 0.5 u(t)$$

**Step 5 — Matrices (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2.5 & -1.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
\mathbf{C} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{D} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b)** Consider the state-space system with:

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(i)** Determine the characteristic equation of $\mathbf{A}$.
**(ii)** Derive the differential equation relating $y(t)$ and $u(t)$.
**(iii)** Find the transfer function $Y(s)/U(s)$.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Characteristic equation (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) &= \det \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 6 & s+5 \end{bmatrix} = s(s+5) + 6 = s^2 + 5s + 6
\end{aligned}
$$

So the characteristic equation is $s^2 + 5s + 6 = 0$, with eigenvalues $s = -2, -3$.

**(ii) Differential equation (3 marks):**

From the state equation:
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{y} = x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -6x_1 - 5x_2 + u \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{y} = -6y - 5\dot{y} + u$$

Therefore: $\ddot{y} + 5\dot{y} + 6y = u(t)$.

**(iii) Transfer function (2 marks):**

Taking Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:
$$(s^2 + 5s + 6)Y(s) = U(s)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} &= \frac{1}{s^2 + 5s + 6} = \frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **number of state variables = order of the differential equation = $n$**.
- ✅ Phase-variable definition: $x_1 = y$, $x_2 = \dot{y}$, $x_3 = \ddot{y}$, $\ldots$, $x_n = y^{(n-1)}$.
- ✅ The matrix $\mathbf{A}$ is a **companion matrix** with **1s on the super-diagonal** and coefficients $-a_i/a_0$ in the **last row in reverse order**.
- ✅ $\mathbf{B}$ has all zeros **except** the last entry, which is $1/a_0$.
- ✅ $\mathbf{C} = [1\ 0\ 0\ \cdots\ 0]$ and $\mathbf{D} = [0]$ for phase-variable form.
- ✅ Always **normalize** the ODE by $a_0$ if $a_0 \neq 1$ before forming matrices.
- ✅ The characteristic polynomial of $\mathbf{A}$ equals the denominator polynomial of $G(s)$.
- ✅ Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ = poles of $G(s)$ = roots of the characteristic equation.
- ✅ For phase-variable form, the system is **always controllable** — useful for Module 5 pole-placement questions.
- ✅ **Common sign errors** occur in the last row — double-check reverse order before submitting.
- ✅ KTU expects you to **state the state variable definitions explicitly** before writing matrices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Model of Simple Mechanical and Electrical Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT521 - CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** State Model of Simple Mechanical and Electrical Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — State Model of Simple Mechanical and Electrical Systems

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In modern control theory, a system is described by a set of **first-order differential equations** in terms of *state variables* rather than a single high-order transfer function. This is called the **State-Space Representation** (also called the *modern or time-domain approach*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State of a System:** The state of a system at time $t_0$ is the minimum amount of information (a set of $n$ variables) that, together with the input for $t \ge t_0$, uniquely determines the future behaviour of the system for all $t \ge t_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **State Variables:** A state variable is any physical quantity (position, velocity, current, voltage, flux, charge, etc.) that, together with the input and the system dynamics, completely describes the future state of the system. The minimum number of state variables required equals the **order of the system** ($n$).

> [!NOTE]
> **State Vector:** A column vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$ whose components are the $n$ independent state variables:
>
> $$\mathbf{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \\ \vdots \\ x_n(t) \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **State Space:** The $n$-dimensional Euclidean space whose coordinate axes are the $n$ state variables. As time varies, the tip of the state vector traces a trajectory called the **state trajectory**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard State-Space Form:** A linear time-invariant (LTI) system is expressed as:
>
> $$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = A\,\mathbf{x}(t) + B\,\mathbf{u}(t)$$
>
> $$\mathbf{y}(t) = C\,\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,\mathbf{u}(t)$$
>
> where $A$ is the **system matrix** ($n \times n$), $B$ is the **input matrix** ($n \times r$), $C$ is the **output matrix** ($p \times n$), and $D$ is the **direct transmission matrix** ($p \times r$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Drone's GPS Tracker"

Imagine a drone flying in 3-D space. To completely describe its motion at the next instant, the controller only needs **three numbers** (the drone's current $x, y, z$ position) and **three more** (its current $v_x, v_y, v_z$ velocity). These six numbers — the *state vector* — are the **"GPS state"** of the drone.

Knowing past trajectory (history) is *not* needed. Knowing the *present* state and the *future* control inputs (thrust commands) is enough to predict the *future* trajectory uniquely. This is exactly the philosophy of the **state-space model**: *the present state holds the entire memory of the past*.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Favourite):**
> - **Classical Approach** (Transfer Function): Valid only for **single-input, single-output (SISO)**, **linear, time-invariant** systems and gives only the *output* response.
> - **Modern Approach** (State-Space): Works for **MIMO, LTI, time-varying, non-linear** systems, and gives *all internal variables* as well as the output.

---

## 1.3 Why State-Space for Mechanical & Electrical Systems?

Physical systems are inherently **multi-energy-domain**:

| Domain | Natural Energy Storage Element | State Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Translational Mechanical | Mass ($M$) | Position $x$, velocity $\dot{x}$ |
| Rotational Mechanical | Inertia ($J$) | Angular position $\theta$, angular velocity $\omega$ |
| Electrical | Inductor ($L$) | Current $i_L$ |
| Electrical | Capacitor ($C$) | Voltage $v_C$ |

The number of **independent energy-storage elements** equals the order $n$ of the system, and hence equals the **dimension of the state vector**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Selection Rules for State Variables (KTU Standard Procedure)

The following is the universally accepted algorithm used to develop the state model:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Identify Energy-Storage Elements.**
> Count the number of independent energy-storage elements. This gives the **order $n$** of the system and the number of state variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 2 — Assign State Variables.**
> - Inductor current $i_L$ is a state variable.
> - Capacitor voltage $v_C$ is a state variable.
> - Mass position $x$ and velocity $\dot{x}$ are state variables.
> - Inertia angular position $\theta$ and angular velocity $\omega$ are state variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 3 — Write Differential Equations.**
> Apply Newton's 2nd law (mechanical) or KVL/KCL (electrical) to obtain first-order ODEs in the chosen state variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 4 — Rearrange in Standard Form.**
> Express $\dot{x}_i = (\text{linear combo of } x_j) + (\text{linear combo of } u_k)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 5 — Identify $A, B, C, D$ Matrices.**
> Read off the coefficients from the matrix equations.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | System | Equation of Motion / KVL | State Variables $(x_1, x_2, x_3)$ | Key $A$ Matrix Entries |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Translational Mass-Spring-Damper | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F$ | $x, \dot{x}$ | $a_{21}=-K/M$, $a_{22}=-B/M$ |
| 2 | Rotational Inertia-Damper-Spring | $J\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} + K\theta = T$ | $\theta, \dot{\theta}$ | $a_{21}=-K/J$, $a_{22}=-B/J$ |
| 3 | Series RLC (input $v$, output $v_C$) | $L\dot{i} + Ri + v_C = v$ | $i, v_C$ | $a_{11}=-R/L$, $a_{12}=-1/L$, $a_{21}=1/C$ |
| 4 | Parallel RLC (input $i$, output $v_C$) | $i = i_R + i_C + i_L$ | $v_C, \dot{v}_C$ | $a_{11}=0$, $a_{12}=1$ |
| 5 | Armature-Controlled DC Motor | $L_a\dot{i}_a + R_a i_a + K_b\omega = e_a$ | $\theta, \omega, i_a$ | $a_{23}=K_T/J$, $a_{32}=-K_b/L_a$ |
| 6 | Field-Controlled DC Motor | $L_f\dot{i}_f + R_f i_f = e_f$ | $\theta, \omega, i_f$ | $a_{23}=K_f/J$ |

| Standard SS Form | Matrix Dimensions | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ | $A : n \times n$, $B : n \times r$ | State Equation |
| $\mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x} + D\mathbf{u}$ | $C : p \times n$, $D : p \times r$ | Output Equation |
| $n$ | — | Order of system = number of energy-storage elements |
| $r$ | — | Number of inputs |
| $p$ | — | Number of outputs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** $K$ (spring) $\rightarrow \text{N/m}$; $B$ (damper) $\rightarrow \text{N·s/m}$; $M$ (mass) $\rightarrow \text{kg}$; $J$ (inertia) $\rightarrow \text{kg·m}^2$; $K_T$ $\rightarrow \text{N·m/A}$; $K_b$ $\rightarrow \text{V·s/rad}$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The state-space framework is the **language of modern engineering design**:

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft autopilot design uses a 12-state model (position, velocity, attitude, angular rates).
- **Robotics:** Each robotic joint is a 2-state model (position + velocity) — the basis of manipulator dynamics.
- **Power Electronics:** DC-DC converters (buck, boost) are modelled in state-space for digital control.
- **Automotive:** Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS), Electronic Stability Control (ESC), and EV drivetrains rely on state models.
- **Process Control:** MIMO chemical reactors are linearised around an operating point and put into state form.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exams, **always state the number of energy-storage elements** before writing the state vector — examiners award partial credit for this.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> All derivations below follow the KTU board valuation pattern: each algebraic step is shown, justified, and clearly labelled.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Translational Mass-Spring-Damper System

### System Description
A mass $M$ slides on a frictionless surface, attached to a fixed wall through a spring of stiffness $K$ and a viscous damper of coefficient $B$. An external force $F(t)$ is applied.

![Mass-Spring-Damper Schematic Placeholder — see Section 4 Mermaid]

### Free-Body Diagram Analysis
Forces acting on the mass $M$:
- Inertial force (Newton's 2nd law): $M\ddot{x}$
- Spring restoring force (opposes displacement): $-Kx$
- Damping force (opposes velocity): $-B\dot{x}$
- Applied force: $F$

Applying Newton's 2nd law (sum of forces = $M\ddot{x}$):

$$M\ddot{x}(t) + B\dot{x}(t) + Kx(t) = F(t)$$

### Step 1 — Identify Energy-Storage Elements
There is **one** energy-storing element (the mass $M$ stores kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2}M\dot{x}^2$). However, the system is **2nd order**, so we need **two** state variables:

$$x_1(t) = x(t) \quad (\text{position})$$

$$x_2(t) = \dot{x}(t) \quad (\text{velocity})$$

### Step 2 — Form First-Order ODEs
From the definition of $x_1$ and $x_2$:

$$\dot{x}_1 = \dot{x} = x_2$$

Solve the original equation for the highest derivative $\ddot{x}$:

$$\ddot{x} = -\frac{K}{M}x - \frac{B}{M}\dot{x} + \frac{1}{M}F$$

Substitute $x_1, x_2$:

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{K}{M}x_1 - \frac{B}{M}x_2 + \frac{1}{M}F$$

### Step 3 — Write in Standard State-Space Form

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} &= 
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\dfrac{K}{M} & -\dfrac{B}{M} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + 
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{M} \end{bmatrix} F(t)
\end{aligned}$$

If the output is position $y = x$:

$$\begin{aligned}
y &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + [0]\,F(t)
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Matrices

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -K/M & -B/M \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/M \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = [0]$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rotational Mechanical System (Inertia-Spring-Damper)

### System Description
A rotating shaft with moment of inertia $J$, viscous damping $B$, torsional spring $K$, driven by torque $T$.

### Equation of Motion (analogous to translational case)

$$J\ddot{\theta}(t) + B\dot{\theta}(t) + K\theta(t) = T(t)$$

### State Variables
$x_1 = \theta$ (angular position), $x_2 = \dot{\theta} = \omega$ (angular velocity).

### State Equations

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{K}{J}x_1 - \frac{B}{J}x_2 + \frac{1}{J}T$$

### State-Space Matrices

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -K/J & -B/J \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/J \end{bmatrix} \\
C &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ (for }\theta\text{)}, \quad D = [0]
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **exact duality** between translational ($M, B, K, F$) and rotational ($J, B, K, T$) systems — only the letters change.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Series RLC Electrical Network

### System Description
A series RLC circuit with input voltage $v(t)$ applied across the series combination of $R$, $L$, and $C$. The output is the capacitor voltage $v_C(t)$.

### Step 1 — Apply KVL
Going around the loop (sum of voltage drops = applied voltage):

$$L\frac{di}{dt} + R\,i + v_C = v(t)$$

### Step 2 — Capacitor Current Relation
Current through the capacitor equals the loop current:

$$i = C\frac{dv_C}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Identify Energy-Storage Elements
Two energy-storing elements: inductor $L$ (magnetic energy) and capacitor $C$ (electric energy). Hence the system is **2nd order** with two state variables:

$$x_1 = i \quad (\text{inductor current})$$

$$x_2 = v_C \quad (\text{capacitor voltage})$$

### Step 4 — Form First-Order ODEs
From the capacitor relation:

$$\dot{x}_2 = \dot{v}_C = \frac{i}{C} = \frac{1}{C}x_1$$

From the KVL equation, solve for $\dot{i} = \dot{x}_1$:

$$L\dot{x}_1 = -R\,x_1 - x_2 + v$$

$$\dot{x}_1 = -\frac{R}{L}x_1 - \frac{1}{L}x_2 + \frac{1}{L}v$$

### Step 5 — Assemble State-Space Form

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} &= 
\begin{bmatrix} -R/L & -1/L \\ 1/C & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + 
\begin{bmatrix} 1/L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} v(t)
\end{aligned}$$

### Output Equation (output = capacitor voltage)

$$y = v_C = x_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + [0]\,v(t)$$

### Final Matrices

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} -R/L & -1/L \\ 1/C & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1/L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = [0]$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Armature-Controlled DC Motor (3rd Order)

### System Description
A DC motor whose field flux is held constant, and the **armature voltage $e_a$** is the control input. The output is the **shaft angular position $\theta$**.

### Governing Equations
- **Electrical (armature loop):** $L_a \dfrac{di_a}{dt} + R_a i_a + e_b = e_a$
- **Mechanical (rotor):** $J\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = T_m = K_T i_a$
- **Back-EMF relation:** $e_b = K_b \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = K_b \omega$

where $K_T$ is the motor torque constant and $K_b$ is the back-EMF constant (in SI units, $K_T = K_b$).

### Step 1 — Energy-Storage Elements
Three energy-storing elements: armature inductor $L_a$, rotor inertia $J$, and rotor damping (modelled together). Hence **3rd order**, three state variables:

$$x_1 = \theta \quad (\text{shaft position})$$

$$x_2 = \dot{\theta} = \omega \quad (\text{shaft velocity})$$

$$x_3 = i_a \quad (\text{armature current})$$

### Step 2 — Form First-Order ODEs
Directly from the definitions:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

From the mechanical equation:

$$J\dot{x}_2 = -B x_2 + K_T x_3$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{B}{J}x_2 + \frac{K_T}{J}x_3$$

From the electrical equation, substitute $e_b = K_b x_2$:

$$L_a \dot{x}_3 = -R_a x_3 - K_b x_2 + e_a$$

$$\dot{x}_3 = -\frac{K_b}{L_a}x_2 - \frac{R_a}{L_a}x_3 + \frac{1}{L_a}e_a$$

### Step 3 — Assemble the $3 \times 3$ State Matrix

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix} &= 
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -B/J & K_T/J \\ 0 & -K_b/L_a & -R_a/L_a \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + 
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1/L_a \end{bmatrix} e_a
\end{aligned}$$

### Output Equations
- If output is **shaft position** $\theta$:

$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x} + [0]\,e_a$$

- If output is **shaft velocity** $\omega$:

$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x} + [0]\,e_a$$

### Final Identification

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -B/J & K_T/J \\ 0 & -K_b/L_a & -R_a/L_a \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1/L_a \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> The **first column of $A$ is all zeros** because $\theta$ has no "self-coupling" — this is a structural property of all second-order mechanical systems and earns you easy marks if you comment on it.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Field-Controlled DC Motor

When the **armature current is held constant** (large $L_a$ with constant $i_a$) and the **field voltage $e_f$** is the input, the motor behaves as a 3rd-order system with state variables $\theta, \omega, i_f$ and similar triangular $A$-matrix form, with the $K_f / J$ and $K_b / L_f$ entries swapped appropriately. The KTU exam typically asks for the **armature-controlled** version.

---

## 3.6 Verification — Eigenvalues of $A$ give Characteristic Equation

For the series RLC, the eigenvalues of $A$ are the roots of:

$$\det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda + R/L & 1/L \\ -1/C & \lambda \end{bmatrix} = \lambda^2 + \frac{R}{L}\lambda + \frac{1}{LC} = 0$$

Multiplying by $LC$:

$$LC\lambda^2 + RC\lambda + 1 = 0$$

This matches the classical characteristic equation $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$ with $\omega_n = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ and $\zeta = (R/2)\sqrt{C/L}$, **confirming the correctness of the state model**.

# 🗂️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a State-Space Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode["Input Vector u(t)"] --> sumNode["Summing Junction"]
    bMat["Input Matrix B"] --> sumNode
    sumNode --> intNode["Integrator x_dot = Ax + Bu"]
    intNode --> xNode["State Vector x(t)"]
    xNode --> aMatFB["Feedback via System Matrix A"]
    aMatFB --> sumNode
    xNode --> outNode["Output y(t) = Cx + Du"]
    cMat["Output Matrix C"] --> outNode
    inputNode --> dMat["Direct Feedthrough Matrix D"]
    dMat --> outNode
    xNode --> fbOut["State Trajectory in n-Dim State Space"]
```

## 4.2 Modelling Procedure Flowchart (KTU Step-by-Step Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["Start: Physical System Given"] --> s1["Step 1: Identify Energy Storage Elements (L, C, M, J)"]
    s1 --> s2["Step 2: Count them -- gives order n of system"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 3: Choose n State Variables x1 ... xn"]
    s3 --> s4["Step 4: Apply KVL / KCL / Newton Law"]
    s4 --> s5["Step 5: Derive n First Order ODEs"]
    s5 --> s6["Step 6: Express in Matrix Form x_dot = A x + B u"]
    s6 --> s7["Step 7: Write Output Equation y = C x + D u"]
    s7 --> s8["Step 8: Verify using det lambda I minus A equals Char Eqn"]
    s8 --> endNode["End: State Model Ready"]
```

## 4.3 Mechanical System Energy Flow (Translational Mass-Spring-Damper)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["External Force F"] --> M["Mass M -- Stores Kinetic Energy"]
    M --> B["Damper B -- Dissipates Energy"]
    M --> K["Spring K -- Stores Potential Energy"]
    K --> wall["Fixed Wall"]
    B --> wall
    x1["State x1 equals x position"] --- M
    x2["State x2 equals x dot velocity"] --- M
```

## 4.4 Series RLC Circuit Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    v["Source Voltage v"] --> R["Resistor R -- Dissipates"]
    R --> L["Inductor L -- Stores Magnetic Energy"]
    L --> C["Capacitor C -- Stores Electric Energy"]
    C --> back["Return to Source"]
    xi1["State x1 equals i inductor current"] --- L
    xc2["State x2 equals vC capacitor voltage"] --- C
```

## 4.5 DC Motor State Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ea["Armature Voltage ea"] --> RaLa["Armature Loop: La di_a by dt plus Ra i_a"]
    RaLa --> eb["Back EMF e_b equals K_b omega"]
    eb --> RaLa
    RaLa --> ia["Armature Current ia"]
    ia --> kt["Torque T equals K_T i_a"]
    kt --> mech["Mechanical Loop: J d omega by dt plus B omega"]
    mech --> omega["Angular Velocity omega"]
    omega --> theta["Integrator gives Angular Position theta"]
    omega --> eb
    s1["State x1 equals theta"] --- theta
    s2["State x2 equals omega"] --- omega
    s3["State x3 equals ia"] --- ia
```

> [!TIP]
> When a physical sketch is required in the exam, draw the **free-body diagram** (mechanical) or **circuit schematic** (electrical) clearly and **label all variables and directions** — partial credit depends on this labelling.
