# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Define the terms (i) state variable, (ii) state vector, and (iii) state space. How are state variables chosen for electrical and mechanical systems?**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) State Variable:** A state variable is the smallest set of physical quantities (such as position, velocity, current through an inductor, or voltage across a capacitor) that, together with the input signal, completely determines the future behaviour of the system for any time $t \ge t_0$.

> **(ii) State Vector:** It is a column vector $\mathbf{x}(t) = [x_1(t), x_2(t), \ldots, x_n(t)]^T$ composed of all $n$ independent state variables of the system.

> **(iii) State Space:** It is the $n$-dimensional vector space in which the state vector is represented; the locus traced by the state vector as time progresses is called the **state trajectory**.

> **Selection Rule:** State variables are always chosen across **independent energy-storage elements** — *inductor current* $i_L$ and *capacitor voltage* $v_C$ for electrical systems; *position* $x$ and *velocity* $\dot{x}$ (or $\theta$ and $\omega$) for mechanical systems. The number of state variables equals the order $n$ of the system.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supple]`

**Compare the classical transfer-function approach with the modern state-space approach. State any two advantages of state-space analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Classical (Transfer Function) | Modern (State-Space) |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Frequency (s-domain) | Time-domain |
| System type | SISO, LTI only | MIMO, LTI, time-varying, non-linear |
| Information | Input–output relation only | Internal variables also accessible |
| Initial conditions | Difficult to handle | Easily incorporated |
| Design tools | Bode, Nyquist, Root Locus | Pole placement, Observer design, LQR |

> **Two Advantages of State-Space:**
> 1. It is applicable to **MIMO** and **non-linear** systems where transfer-function methods fail.
> 2. It reveals the **internal states** of the system, making it suitable for modern control design such as **pole placement**, **state observers**, and **optimal control**.

---

## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions are provided; answer **either** A **or** B. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the state-space model of an **armature-controlled DC motor**. Clearly state the state variables and identify the $A, B, C, D$ matrices. Assume the output is shaft angular position $\theta$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **translational mass-spring-damper system** with $M = 2\,\text{kg}$, $B = 6\,\text{N·s/m}$, $K = 4\,\text{N/m}$, and input force $F(t)$:
- (i) Write the state equation in matrix form. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Find the characteristic equation and the system eigenvalues. **(4 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Armature-Controlled DC Motor

**Step 1 — Governing Equations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + R_a i_a + e_b &= e_a(t) \\
J \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B \frac{d\theta}{dt} &= T_m = K_T i_a \\
e_b &= K_b \frac{d\theta}{dt}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — State Variables (3rd order — 3 energy-storing elements: $L_a, J$ plus damping):**

$$x_1 = \theta, \quad x_2 = \dot{\theta} = \omega, \quad x_3 = i_a$$

**Step 3 — First-Order ODEs:**

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{B}{J}x_2 + \frac{K_T}{J}x_3$$

$$\dot{x}_3 = -\frac{K_b}{L_a}x_2 - \frac{R_a}{L_a}x_3 + \frac{1}{L_a}e_a$$

**Step 4 — State-Space Form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix} &= 
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -B/J & K_T/J \\ 0 & -K_b/L_a & -R_a/L_a \end{bmatrix}}_{A}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + 
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1/L_a \end{bmatrix}}_{B} e_a
\end{aligned}$$

**Output Equation (y = θ):**

$$\begin{aligned}
y = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{C} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + \underbrace{[0]}_{D} e_a
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Mass-Spring-Damper Numerical

**Part (i) — State Equation:**

Equation of motion: $2\ddot{x} + 6\dot{x} + 4x = F$

State variables: $x_1 = x$, $x_2 = \dot{x}$.

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = -2x_1 - 3x_2 + 0.5 F$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} F
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (ii) — Characteristic Equation and Eigenvalues:**

$$\det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 2 & \lambda + 3 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda + 3) - (-1)(2) = \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2$$

Setting equal to zero:

$$\lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (\lambda + 1)(\lambda + 2) = 0$$

**Eigenvalues:** $\lambda_1 = -1$, $\lambda_2 = -2$.

Both eigenvalues are real and negative ⇒ the system is **overdamped** and **stable**.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Suppl]`

**(a)** For a **series RLC circuit** with input voltage $v(t)$ and output capacitor voltage $v_C(t)$, derive the state-space model. Write the $A, B, C, D$ matrices. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **rotational mechanical system** with $J = 1\,\text{kg·m}^2$, $B = 3\,\text{N·m·s/rad}$, $K = 2\,\text{N·m/rad}$:
- (i) Write the state equation in matrix form. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Verify that the system is **stable** by computing the eigenvalues. **(4 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Series RLC Circuit

**Step 1 — KVL and Capacitor Relation:**

$$L\frac{di}{dt} + Ri + v_C = v(t), \quad i = C\frac{dv_C}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — State Variables (2nd order — 2 energy-storing elements: $L$ and $C$):**

$$x_1 = i, \quad x_2 = v_C$$

**Step 3 — First-Order ODEs:**

$$\dot{x}_1 = -\frac{R}{L}x_1 - \frac{1}{L}x_2 + \frac{1}{L}v$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = \frac{1}{C}x_1$$

**Step 4 — State-Space Form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} -R/L & -1/L \\ 1/C & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1/L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} v
\end{aligned}$$

**Output Equation (y = $v_C$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
y = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + [0]\,v
\end{aligned}$$

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} -R/L & -1/L \\ 1/C & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1/L \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = [0]$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Rotational System Numerical

**Part (i) — State Equation:**

Equation: $\ddot{\theta} + 3\dot{\theta} + 2\theta = T$

State variables: $x_1 = \theta$, $x_2 = \dot{\theta}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} T
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (ii) — Stability via Eigenvalues:**

$$\det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = 0$$

$$\lambda_1 = -1, \quad \lambda_2 = -2$$

Both eigenvalues have **negative real parts** ⇒ the system is **asymptotically stable** (BIBO stable). ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to justify the number of state variables.** Always start by counting energy-storage elements and *explicitly state* "system is $n$-th order, hence $n$ state variables are needed."
> 2. **Sign errors in KVL/KCL loops.** Be careful with the polarity of the back-EMF $e_b = K_b \omega$ in the DC motor — it always *opposes* the applied armature voltage.
> 3. **Confusing $K_T$ and $K_b$.** In SI units they are numerically equal but have different units (N·m/A vs V·s/rad). The KTU answer key explicitly checks the unit of each matrix entry.
> 4. **Incorrect dimensions of $B$ and $C$ matrices.** $B$ must be $n \times r$ and $C$ must be $p \times n$. Mismatched dimensions fetch zero marks even if the entries are correct.
> 5. **Forgetting $D$ matrix.** Many students skip $D = 0$ in the output equation. Always state $D$ explicitly to earn the final mark.
> 6. **Not verifying the model.** The characteristic equation from $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$ should match the classical one derived from the transfer function. A quick check earns bonus goodwill with the examiner.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **State variables are physical variables across energy-storage elements** — never choose them arbitrarily.
- ✅ **Number of state variables = Order of the system = Number of independent energy-storage elements.**
- ✅ For **mechanical systems:** choose **position + velocity** (translational) or **angle + angular velocity** (rotational).
- ✅ For **electrical systems:** choose **inductor current $i_L$** and **capacitor voltage $v_C$**.
- ✅ For the **DC motor (armature-controlled):** state vector is $[\theta, \omega, i_a]^T$ — always 3-dimensional.
- ✅ The **system matrix $A$** of a 2nd-order mechanical/electrical system has the canonical form $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}$ (phase-variable canonical form).
- ✅ The **first column of $A$** in a DC motor is **all zeros** — this is a structural property of mechanical integrators.
- ✅ **Stability check:** compute $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$. All eigenvalues with negative real part ⇒ stable.
- ✅ The **direct transmission $D$** is **zero** for systems without an algebraic path from input to output (e.g., systems with at least one integrator or one energy-storage element in series).
- ✅ Always **carry units** through the derivation — examiners do check unit consistency.
- ✅ **Verification step:** $\det(\lambda I - A)$ expanded should equal the **classical characteristic polynomial** obtained from the transfer function. Use this as a cross-check.
- ✅ The standard state-space form is **not unique** — different state variable choices lead to different $A, B, C, D$ matrices, but all are *equivalent* representations of the same system.
- ✅ For a **series RLC**, the $A$ matrix has entry $-R/L$ at $(1,1)$ and $+1/C$ at $(2,1)$ — a very common KTU question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:** When in doubt, **draw the FBD / circuit diagram, label the chosen state variables on it, and then start writing equations.** This visual step secures the first 2–3 marks of any 7-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State-Space Representation of Transfer Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT521 - CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** State-Space Representation of Transfer Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State-Space Representation** is a modern, time-domain mathematical framework that describes a linear time-invariant (LTI) control system using a set of **first-order differential equations** expressed compactly in matrix form. It captures both the **internal dynamics** (state variables) and **external behaviour** (input–output relationship) of the system simultaneously.

The standard State-Space model is expressed as a pair of coupled matrix equations:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = A\,\mathbf{x}(t) + B\,\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = C\,\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

where the matrix and vector dimensions for an **n-th order, p-input, q-output** system are:

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimensions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{x}(t)$ | State vector (internal energy-storage variables) | $n \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{u}(t)$ | Input (control) vector | $p \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{y}(t)$ | Output vector | $q \times 1$ |
| $A$ | System (State) matrix | $n \times n$ |
| $B$ | Input matrix | $n \times p$ |
| $C$ | Output matrix | $q \times n$ |
| $D$ | Direct feed-through (Transmission) matrix | $q \times p$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Transfer Function** $G(s)$, by contrast, is a *single-input, single-output (SISO)* frequency-domain black-box description. The act of converting a transfer function into state-space is called **Realisation** or **State-Space Realisation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a hilly road. To fully describe the car's motion, simply knowing the accelerator pedal position (input) and the wheel rotation rate (output) is **not** enough. You also need to know internal "hidden" conditions: the engine RPM, fuel-air mixture, brake temperature, gear ratio, suspension compression, etc. These hidden, energy-storing conditions are exactly what **state variables** represent.

* **Transfer Function** = Only the relationship between accelerator (input) and wheel speed (output). It is like a "headline summary."
* **State-Space Model** = The complete, multi-page engineering report listing every internal variable and how it influences every other variable and the output.

A system can therefore have **many** state-space realisations (each choosing different state variables like velocity, current, or charge), but only **one** transfer function for a given input–output pair.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Highlight:** In Module 4, students must learn to (a) build a state-space model from a physical/electrical system, and (b) convert a transfer function into state-space in multiple canonical forms.

---

## 1.3 Key Definitions in KTU-Exam Vocabulary

* **State of a System:** The minimum set of variables $\mathbf{x}(t) = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ whose values at time $t = t_0$, together with the input for $t \ge t_0$, completely determine the future behaviour of the system.
* **State Variables:** Variables proportional to the energy-storage elements (inductors store magnetic energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2}Li^2$ ⇒ current is a state variable; capacitors store electric energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2}Cv^2$ ⇒ voltage is a state variable; masses store kinetic energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ ⇒ velocity is a state variable; springs store potential energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$ ⇒ displacement is a state variable).
* **State Vector:** A column vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$ whose elements are the state variables.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is state-space analysis required even though transfer functions exist?"* The correct answer should explicitly mention **multi-input multi-output (MIMO) capability**, **initial condition handling**, and **modern control design (pole-placement, LQR, observers)** — classical transfer-function methods cannot perform these tasks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Master Equations of State-Space Analysis

The complete state-space model is governed by **two** equations whose physical interpretation is as follows:

1. **State Equation (Dynamic Equation):**
   $$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = A\,\mathbf{x}(t) + B\,\mathbf{u}(t)$$
   This first-order matrix differential equation describes *how the internal energy of the system evolves* under the influence of inputs and the system's own internal dynamics.

2. **Output Equation (Static Algebraic Equation):**
   $$\mathbf{y}(t) = C\,\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,\mathbf{u}(t)$$
   This purely algebraic equation tells us *how measurable outputs are formed* by linear combinations of the states and the input.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Framework

* **Step 1 — Identify Energy-Storage Elements:** Count the number of independent inductors and capacitors (or mass/spring/ damper elements in mechanical systems). The number of such elements equals the order $n$ of the system and the dimension of the state vector.
* **Step 2 — Choose State Variables:** Assign one state variable to each independent energy-storage element. Common KTU-favoured choices are $i_L(t)$ (inductor current) and $v_C(t)$ (capacitor voltage).
* **Step 3 — Write First-Order ODEs:** Use Kirchhoff's Voltage/Current Laws (or Newton's laws) to express the derivative of each state variable in terms of all states and inputs.
* **Step 4 — Stack in Matrix Form:** Rearrange the equations into the canonical $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ form, and $\mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x} + D\mathbf{u}$.

---

## 2.2 Deriving the Transfer Function from State-Space (Bridge Formula)

Taking the **Laplace Transform** of the state equation assuming **zero initial conditions** $\mathbf{x}(0) = 0$:

$$
sX(s) = A X(s) + B U(s)
$$

Grouping $X(s)$ terms together:

$$
(sI - A) X(s) = B U(s)
$$

Solving for $X(s)$ by pre-multiplying with the inverse of $(sI - A)$:

$$
X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} B U(s)
$$

Substituting this into the Laplace-transformed output equation $\,Y(s) = C X(s) + D U(s)$:

$$
Y(s) = \big[\,C(sI - A)^{-1} B + D\,\big] \, U(s)
$$

Therefore, the **State-Space Transfer Function** is the heart of the topic:

$$
\boxed{\,G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Identity:** The matrix $(sI - A)$ is called the **characteristic matrix**. Its determinant $\det(sI - A)$ defines the **characteristic polynomial**, whose roots are the **eigenvalues** of $A$ — and *these are exactly the poles of the transfer function*. This is one of the most-asked concepts in the KTU ESE.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (SISO)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | State Equation | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ | First-order matrix ODE |
| 2 | Output Equation | $\mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x} + D\mathbf{u}$ | Purely algebraic |
| 3 | State-Space to Transfer Function | $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$ | **Most important formula** |
| 4 | Characteristic Equation | $\det(sI - A) = 0$ | Roots = system poles |
| 5 | Eigenvalues of A | $\lambda_i$ such that $\det(A - \lambda_i I) = 0$ | Equals the poles |
| 6 | Resolvent Matrix | $\Phi(s) = (sI - A)^{-1}$ | Used in Laplace transform |
| 7 | State Transition Matrix | $\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$ | Solution of homogeneous system |
| 8 | Time-domain solution | $\mathbf{x}(t) = \Phi(t)\mathbf{x}(0) + \int_0^t \Phi(t-\tau) B \mathbf{u}(\tau)\, d\tau$ | For non-zero initial conditions |
| 9 | SISO Transfer Function from state-space | $G(s) = \dfrac{\text{adj}(sI-A)B}{\det(sI-A)} + D$ | Companion-matrix form |
| 10 | Order of system | $n = \dim(A)$ | Number of energy-storage elements |

> **Notation caution:** Use $\det(sI - A)$ — do **not** write $\vert sI - A \vert$ inside a markdown table; the vertical pipe will break the table structure.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Aerospace:** Modern aircraft autopilot systems (e.g., fly-by-wire in Boeing 777) use state-space for **MIMO** flight control surfaces.
* **Robotics:** Multi-joint robotic arms (n joints ⇒ 2n state variables) are described by $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ with $A$ being the manipulator inertia matrix form.
* **Power Electronics:** DC–DC converters, inverters, and motor drives are routinely modelled in state-space for digital controller design.
* **Process Control:** Distillation columns, chemical reactors (with non-minimum-phase behaviour) require **state-space observers** to estimate unmeasurable compositions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Third-Order Transfer Function to State-Space

**Problem:** Convert the following transfer function to state-space in **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)**:

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{2s + 3}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 4}
$$

### Step 1 — Expand the Transfer Function (Introduction of a Dummy Variable)

To convert a high-order single transfer function into first-order ODEs, we introduce an intermediate variable $X(s)$:

$$
\dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{2s+3}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 4} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
Y(s) = (2s+3)X(s)
$$

$$
U(s) = (s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 4)X(s)
$$

### Step 2 — Cross-Multiply and Equate to a Time-Domain Differential Equation

Cross-multiplying the second relation:

$$
s^3 X(s) + 6s^2 X(s) + 11s X(s) + 4X(s) = U(s)
$$

Taking the inverse Laplace transform (each factor of $s$ corresponds to one differentiation with zero initial conditions):

$$
\dddot{x}(t) + 6\ddot{x}(t) + 11\dot{x}(t) + 4x(t) = u(t)
$$

### Step 3 — Define the State Variables (Phase-Variable Choice)

The **Controllable Canonical Form** uses *phase variables* — successive time derivatives of the dummy variable $x$:

$$
x_1 = x(t), \quad x_2 = \dot{x}(t), \quad x_3 = \ddot{x}(t)
$$

### Step 4 — Form the First-Order System

Differentiating each state variable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= x_2 \\
\dot{x}_2 &= x_3 \\
\dot{x}_3 &= -4x_1 - 11x_2 - 6x_3 + u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

The last equation comes from solving the original third-order ODE for the highest derivative.

### Step 5 — Stack in Matrix Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -4 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Output Equation

Since $Y(s) = (2s+3)X(s)$, in the time domain this becomes $y(t) = 2\dot{x}(t) + 3x(t) = 2x_2 + 3x_1$:

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}
+ [0]\,u(t)
$$

### Final State-Space Model (Controllable Canonical Form)

$$
\boxed{
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -4 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix},\;
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix},\;
C = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\;
D = [0]
}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — State-Space to Transfer Function

**Problem:** For the state-space model given below, find the transfer function $Y(s)/U(s)$.

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -5 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
D = 0
$$

### Step 1 — Form $(sI - A)$

$$
(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -5 & -2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 5 & s+2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Determinant

$$
\det(sI - A) = s(s+2) - (-1)(5) = s^2 + 2s + 5
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Adjoint (Cofactor Transpose)

$$
\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+2 & 1 \\ -5 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $(sI - A)^{-1} B$

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} B = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + 2s + 5}
\begin{bmatrix} s+2 & 1 \\ -5 & s \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}
= \dfrac{1}{s^2 + 2s + 5}
\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 3s \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Output Equation Matrix $C$

$$
C(sI - A)^{-1} B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\dfrac{1}{s^2 + 2s + 5}
\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 3s \end{bmatrix}
= \dfrac{3}{s^2 + 2s + 5}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\,G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{3}{s^2 + 2s + 5}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Canonical Forms (Theory)

### 3.3.1 Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)

For a strictly proper transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{b_0 s^{n-1} + b_1 s^{n-2} + \cdots + b_{n-1}}{s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_{n-1} s + a_n}
$$

The state matrices follow a **companion pattern** (the last row of $A$ contains the negatives of the denominator coefficients, and the last column of $B$ is a single 1):

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_n & -a_{n-1} & -a_{n-2} & \cdots & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
C = \begin{bmatrix} b_{n-1} & b_{n-2} & \cdots & b_0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.3.2 Observable Canonical Form (OCF)

The **dual** of CCF. It has $C = [0\; 0\; \cdots\; 0\; 1]$ and the last column of $A$ contains the coefficients. Useful for designing observers.

### 3.3.3 Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)

When the poles $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$ of the system are distinct, partial-fraction expansion yields:

$$
A = \text{diag}(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n), \quad
B = [1\; 1\; \cdots\; 1]^T, \quad
C = [c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n]
$$

where $c_i$ are the **residues** at each pole.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix (Comparison of Canonical Forms)

| Feature | Controllable (CCF) | Observable (OCF) | Diagonal (DCF) | Jordan (JCF) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Companion $A$ pattern | Yes (last row) | Yes (last column) | No (diagonal) | Block diagonal |
| Best for | Pole-placement | Observer design | Modal analysis | Repeated roots |
| $B$ vector | $[0\;\cdots\;0\;1]^T$ | $[b_{n-1}\;\cdots\;b_0]^T$ | $[1\;\cdots\;1]^T$ | $T^{-1} B$ |
| $C$ vector | $[b_{n-1}\;\cdots\;b_0]$ | $[0\;\cdots\;0\;1]$ | $[c_1\;\cdots\;c_n]$ | $C T$ |
| $D$ | 0 (strictly proper) | 0 (strictly proper) | 0 | 0 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual State-Space System Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeU["Input u(t)"] --> nodeSum1(("+"))
    nodeSum1 --> nodeB["Input Matrix B (n x p)"]
    nodeB --> nodeSum2(("+"))
    nodeA["System Matrix A (n x n)"]
    nodeSum2 --> nodeInt["Integrator (1 over s)"]
    nodeInt --> nodeX["State Vector x(t)"]
    nodeA --> nodeSum2
    nodeX --> nodeA
    nodeX --> nodeC["Output Matrix C (q x n)"]
    nodeC --> nodeSum3(("+"))
    nodeD["Feedthrough Matrix D (q x p)"]
    nodeU --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeSum3
    nodeSum3 --> nodeY["Output y(t)"]
    nodeX --> nodeInt

    classDef matrix fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef io fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef logic fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    class A,B,C,D matrix
    class nodeU,nodeY,nodeX io
    class nodeSum1,nodeSum2,nodeSum3,nodeInt logic
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The state vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$ is fed back through the $A$ matrix to modify itself, while the input $\mathbf{u}(t)$ is amplified by the $B$ matrix and added to that feedback. The integrator $\int$ box produces the state from its derivative. The output path combines $C\mathbf{x}$ and $D\mathbf{u}$ linearly.

## 4.2 Block-Level Conversion Flow (Transfer Function ⇄ State-Space)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Given Transfer Function G of s"] --> step1["Expand using dummy variable X of s"]
    step1 --> step2["Inverse Laplace to get n-th order ODE"]
    step2 --> step3["Select Phase Variables for State Vector"]
    step3 --> step4["Decompose into n First-Order ODEs"]
    step4 --> step5["Stack into Matrix Form"]
    step5 --> outputA["State-Space Realisation: A, B, C, D"]

    startB["Given State-Space A, B, C, D"] --> stepB1["Form matrix sI minus A"]
    stepB1 --> stepB2["Compute inverse of sI minus A"]
    stepB2 --> stepB3["Multiply C times inverse times B"]
    stepB3 --> stepB4["Add Direct Feedthrough D"]
    stepB4 --> outputB["Transfer Function G of s"]

    outputA --> verifyC["Same poles as characteristic roots of A"]
    outputB --> verifyC
    verifyC --> done["Verification: Det of sI minus A equals denominator"]

    classDef step fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef inout fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    class step1,step2,step3,step4,step5,stepB1,stepB2,stepB3,stepB4 step
    class startA,startB,outputA,outputB,verifyC,done inout
```

## 4.3 Conversion Strategy Table (Visual)

| Input Type | Strategy Used | Resulting Canonical Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transfer Function (denominator first) | Phase-variable decomposition | Controllable Canonical Form |
| Transfer Function (denominator first, transpose) | Dual of CCF | Observable Canonical Form |
| Transfer Function (factored, distinct roots) | Partial-fraction expansion | Diagonal Canonical Form |
| Transfer Function (repeated roots) | Modified partial fractions | Jordan Canonical Form |
| State-Space model | Laplace transform + matrix inversion | Single equivalent $G(s)$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: 3-mark direct questions (Part A) and 14-mark analytical questions with internal choice (Part B).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define state space. List the advantages of state-space analysis over the conventional transfer-function approach.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **State Space:** The state space of a system is the *n-dimensional space* in which the components of the state vector $\mathbf{x}(t) = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ form the coordinate axes. The state of the system at any instant is represented by a point in this space. **(1 mark)**

* **Advantages of State-Space Analysis:** **(2 marks — list any two)**
  1. It can handle **MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output)** systems, which transfer functions cannot.
  2. It explicitly accounts for **initial conditions**, important for system simulation.
  3. It is the natural framework for **modern control design** techniques such as pole-placement, LQR, Kalman filtering, and observer design.
  4. It applies uniformly to **linear and non-linear**, **time-variant and time-invariant** systems.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the characteristic equation of a state-space system? How is it related to the poles of the transfer function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* The characteristic equation of a state-space system is obtained by setting the determinant of $(sI - A)$ to zero: **(1.5 marks)**

$$
\det(sI - A) = 0
$$

* This determinant yields an *n-th order polynomial in s*. The roots of this polynomial are the **eigenvalues of matrix A** and are **identical to the poles of the transfer function** $G(s)$ obtained from the realisation. **(1.5 marks)**

> **Connection:** If $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B$, then the denominator of $G(s)$ is exactly $\det(sI - A)$, hence $\det(sI - A) = 0$ gives the system poles.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q.A.** **(a)** Derive the relationship between the transfer function and the state-space representation of an LTI system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the transfer function given below, obtain the state-space model in **Controllable Canonical Form**. **(7 marks)**

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{5s^2 + 10s + 15}{s^3 + 4s^2 + 6s + 2}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a): Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Start from the state and output equations:**

The state equation is $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = A\mathbf{x}(t) + B\mathbf{u}(t)$ and the output equation is $\mathbf{y}(t) = C\mathbf{x}(t) + D\mathbf{u}(t)$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Apply the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions** $\mathbf{x}(0) = 0$:

$$
sX(s) = A X(s) + B U(s) \quad\Rightarrow\quad (sI - A)X(s) = B U(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $X(s)$** by pre-multiplying by $(sI - A)^{-1}$, assuming $(sI - A)$ is non-singular:

$$
X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} B U(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the output equation:**

$$
Y(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B U(s) + D U(s) = [C(sI - A)^{-1} B + D]\, U(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the transfer function:**

$$
\boxed{\,G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = C(sI - A)^{-1} B + D\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Use the identity** $(sI - A)^{-1} = \dfrac{\text{adj}(sI - A)}{\det(sI - A)}$:

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{C \cdot \text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B}{\det(sI - A)} + D
$$

This shows explicitly that the **denominator** of $G(s)$ equals the characteristic polynomial $\det(sI - A)$, and the **poles** of $G(s)$ are the eigenvalues of $A$. **(2 marks for concluding remarks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b): Realisation in CCF (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:** For the given $G(s)$ of order 3:

* Denominator: $s^3 + 4s^2 + 6s + 2$ ⇒ $a_1 = 4, \; a_2 = 6, \; a_3 = 2$
* Numerator: $5s^2 + 10s + 15$ ⇒ $b_0 = 5, \; b_1 = 10, \; b_2 = 15$

**Step 2 — Apply the CCF template** for a 3rd-order system: **(2 marks)**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -a_3 & -a_2 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -6 & -4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the B matrix:** $B = [0,\; 0,\; 1]^T$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Form the C matrix:** $C = [b_2,\; b_1,\; b_0] = [15,\; 10,\; 5]$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Form D:** Since $G(s)$ is strictly proper (numerator degree $<$ denominator degree), $D = 0$ **(1 mark)**

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -6 & -4 \end{bmatrix},\;
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix},\;
C = \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 10 & 5 \end{bmatrix},\;
D = [0]
}
$$

**Verification (1 mark):** Computing $\det(sI - A)$:

$$
\det\begin{bmatrix} s & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & s & -1 \\ 2 & 6 & s+4 \end{bmatrix} = s^3 + 4s^2 + 6s + 2 \;\checkmark
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — *INTERNAL CHOICE*

**Q.B.** **(a)** For the state-space system defined by the matrices below, determine the transfer function $Y(s)/U(s)$. **(7 marks)**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ -2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
D = 0
$$

**(b)** Explain the **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)** of state-space representation. For the transfer function below, obtain the DCF realisation. **(7 marks)**

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{6}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a): Transfer Function from State-Space (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Form $(sI - A)$: (1 mark)**

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & -1 \\ 2 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Determinant of $(sI - A)$: (1 mark)**

$$
\det(sI - A) = (s+3)(s) - (-1)(2) = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)
$$

**Step 3 — Adjoint of $(sI - A)$: (1 mark)**

$$
\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s & 1 \\ -2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B$: (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} s & 1 \\ -2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s+1 \\ s+1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by C: (1 mark)**

$$
C \cdot \text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s+1 \\ s+1 \end{bmatrix} = 3(s+1)
$$

**Step 6 — Final Transfer Function: (1 mark)**

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{3(s+1)}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{3}{s+2}
$$

> **Pole-zero cancellation observed:** The pole at $s = -1$ is cancelled by the zero at $s = -1$. The system is therefore **reduced-order** internally, even though the state-space model is 2nd order.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{G(s) = \dfrac{3}{s+2}}$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b): Diagonal Canonical Form (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Theory (3 marks):** In the **Diagonal Canonical Form**, the system matrix $A$ is a diagonal matrix whose entries are the **poles of the transfer function** (i.e., its eigenvalues). The transformation is achieved by performing **partial-fraction expansion** of $G(s)$ when all poles are distinct. The general structure is:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & p_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & p_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ \vdots \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
C = [c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n], \quad D = 0
$$

where $c_i$ are the **residues** of the partial-fraction expansion at each pole $p_i$.

**Step 2 — Partial-fraction expansion of $G(s)$: (2 marks)**

$$
\dfrac{6}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} = \dfrac{c_1}{s+1} + \dfrac{c_2}{s+2} + \dfrac{c_3}{s+3}
$$

Using the cover-up method:

$$
c_1 = \left.\dfrac{6}{(s+2)(s+3)}\right|_{s=-1} = \dfrac{6}{(1)(2)} = 3
$$

$$
c_2 = \left.\dfrac{6}{(s+1)(s+3)}\right|_{s=-2} = \dfrac{6}{(-1)(1)} = -6
$$

$$
c_3 = \left.\dfrac{6}{(s+1)(s+2)}\right|_{s=-3} = \dfrac{6}{(-2)(-1)} = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Read off the canonical matrices: (2 marks)**

$$
\boxed{
A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix},\;
B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix},\;
C = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -6 & 3 \end{bmatrix},\;
D = 0
}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the $+D$ term** in $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$ when the original system has a direct feed-through path (e.g., proportional control with no lag). Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign errors in the last row of $A$** in CCF — students often write $+a_n$ instead of $-a_n$. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Wrong ordering of coefficients in $C$** — it is $[b_{n-1}, b_{n-2}, \dots, b_0]$ (descending) corresponding to the *lowest-order* state first, not the highest. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Not showing the calculation of $\det(sI - A)$** explicitly. The KTU board specifically rewards step-by-step determinant expansion. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Ignoring zero initial conditions** while taking the Laplace transform of $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$. Loss: **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master Before the KTU Exam**

* The state-space model has **two** equations: $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x} + D\mathbf{u}$.
* The **transfer function from state-space** is $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$.
* The denominator of $G(s)$ equals the **characteristic polynomial** $\det(sI - A)$.
* The **poles** of $G(s)$ are the **eigenvalues** of $A$ (roots of $\det(sI - A) = 0$).
* For an $n$-th order system, there are $n$ state variables, each proportional to an independent energy-storage element (inductor current, capacitor voltage, mass velocity, spring displacement).
* **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF):** $A$ is companion matrix with $-a_i$ on the last row; $B = [0,\dots,0,1]^T$.
* **Observable Canonical Form (OCF):** Dual of CCF; $C = [0,\dots,0,1]$.
* **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF):** $A$ is diagonal with poles on the diagonal; $B = [1,\dots,1]^T$; $C$ contains the residues.
* For **strictly proper** transfer functions (numerator degree $<$ denominator degree), $D = 0$.
* For **proper** transfer functions (numerator degree $=$ denominator degree), $D \neq 0$.
* Always assume **zero initial conditions** $\mathbf{x}(0) = 0$ when deriving the transfer function using Laplace transform.
* State-space allows **MIMO** systems, initial-condition analysis, and modern control design — transfer functions cannot.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfer Function from State Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT521 - CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transfer Function from State Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In modern control theory, every **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** system can be represented mathematically in two equivalent ways:

1. **Classical (External) Representation:** Transfer Function $G(s)$ relating input $U(s)$ to output $Y(s)$.
2. **Modern (Internal / State-Space) Representation:** A set of first-order differential equations expressed in matrix form using the state vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$.

The **Transfer Function from a State Model** is the formal mathematical procedure used to convert the modern, time-domain state-space representation of an LTI system into its classical, frequency-domain transfer function representation.

A standard **LTI state model** is given by the pair of equations:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}u(t)$$

$$y(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,u(t)$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **state vector** of dimension $n \times 1$.
- $u(t)$ is the scalar input, $y(t)$ is the scalar output.
- $\mathbf{A}$ ($n \times n$) is the **System Matrix** (dynamics).
- $\mathbf{B}$ ($n \times 1$) is the **Input Matrix**.
- $\mathbf{C}$ ($1 \times n$) is the **Output Matrix**.
- $D$ is the **Direct Feed-through (Transmission) Matrix / Scalar**.

The corresponding transfer function is universally defined by:

$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The expression $G(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D$ is a **mandatory, high-yield, directly examinable formula**. Expect a direct 3-mark definition question or a 7-mark derivation in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **modern, fully automated, multi-storey car parking tower**:
- The **State Vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$** is the live dashboard of the tower. It doesn't just tell you how many cars are at the exit; it tracks the precise position and velocity of the elevator, the rotation of the turntable, and the hydraulic pressure of the lift. It knows the *internal, microscopic condition* of the entire system at every instant.
- The **State Model** ($\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, D$) is the **engineering blueprint** of the tower. The blueprint tells the elevator motor (matrix $\mathbf{A}$) how to respond to the current position, and tells the outer door (matrix $\mathbf{C}$) when to open based on elevator position.
- The **Transfer Function $G(s)$** is the **promise board at the entrance**. It only contains one simple equation, $G(s) = \frac{\text{Cars Out}}{\text{Cars In}}$. It completely ignores the elevator's velocity or the motor's temperature. It only describes the *external, macroscopic input-output relationship*.

> Converting the state model to a transfer function is essentially **throwing away the internal dashboard** and writing a single, clean equation on the public promise board, summarizing the system's overall behavior.

## 1.3 Key Prerequisites

> [!NOTE]
> Before proceeding, students must be comfortable with three core mathematical tools:
> 1. **Laplace Transform of Derivatives:** $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t)\} = s\mathbf{X}(s) - \mathbf{x}(0)$.
> 2. **Matrix Algebra:** Identity matrix $\mathbf{I}$, determinants $\det(\cdot)$, and inverses $(\cdot)^{-1}$.
> 3. **Zero Initial Conditions:** For transfer functions, the system is assumed to be at rest, so $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{0}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Reasoning

The conversion from state model to transfer function is a pure algebraic procedure. Let us break down the *Why* and *How* behind every step.

### Step 1: Why do we take the Laplace Transform?
The state model is in the **time domain**, but the transfer function is a **frequency-domain (s-domain)** concept. The Laplace transform is the mathematical bridge that converts differential equations into algebraic equations. Because we are finding a transfer function (which only models the forced response, not the system's natural history), we **must** assume **zero initial conditions**, meaning $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{0}$.

### Step 2: Why does the derivative become $s\mathbf{X}(s)$?
Using the property $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t)\} = s\mathbf{X}(s) - \mathbf{x}(0)$ with $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{0}$, the time derivative of a state vector transforms into a simple multiplication by the complex frequency variable $s$.

### Step 3: Why do we isolate $\mathbf{X}(s)$?
The transfer function relates **output to input** only. The state vector $\mathbf{X}(s)$ is an internal variable. We must algebraically eliminate it to find a direct expression for $Y(s)$ in terms of $U(s)$.

### Step 4: Why does the inverse $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}$ appear?
After isolating $\mathbf{X}(s)$, we get an expression of the form $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{B}U(s)$. To solve for $\mathbf{X}(s)$, we pre-multiply by the inverse of the bracketed matrix. The determinant of this matrix, $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$, is the **characteristic polynomial**, whose roots are the system's **eigenvalues** (or natural frequencies/poles).

### Step 5: Why do we substitute back into the output equation?
Once $\mathbf{X}(s)$ is known in terms of $U(s)$, we substitute it into the Laplace-transformed output equation $Y(s) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X}(s) + D\,U(s)$. Taking the ratio $Y(s)/U(s)$ yields the final transfer function.

## 2.2 The Resolvent Matrix (Key Concept)

The matrix $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}$ is formally called the **Resolvent Matrix** of $\mathbf{A}$. It is also occasionally denoted as $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(s)$ (since it is the Laplace transform of the state-transition matrix $\boldsymbol{\phi}(t)$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The poles of the transfer function $G(s)$ are precisely the roots of the characteristic equation: $$\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0$$
> This equation is identical to the one used to find the poles of the system from its differential equation. The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ are the **system poles**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Step | Formula / Expression | Dimensions / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| State Equation | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}u(t)$ | $n$ first-order ODEs |
| Output Equation | $y(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,u(t)$ | Single output relation |
| Laplace of State Eq. | $s\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}(s) + \mathbf{B}U(s)$ | Assumes $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{0}$ |
| Isolated State Vector | $\mathbf{X}(s) = (s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}\,U(s)$ | Resolvent matrix involved |
| Master Transfer Function | $G(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D$ | **THE** most critical formula |
| Characteristic Equation | $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0$ | Roots = system poles |
| Resolvent / $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(s)$ | $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1} = \frac{\text{adj}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})}{\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})}$ | Adjoint / Determinant form |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The conversion of state models to transfer functions is a cornerstone of **classical control design**. In industries like **aerospace, robotics, and process control**, engineers use state models to design sophisticated observers (like the Luenberger Observer or Kalman Filter), but they frequently need to *check stability, plot Bode diagrams, or design lead-lag compensators* — all of which require a transfer function. The formula $G(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D$ provides the rigorous bridge between modern and classical design paradigms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation: From State Model to Transfer Function

We begin with the standard time-domain LTI state model:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}u(t) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$y(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,u(t) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the Laplace transform to equation (i). Using the property $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t)\} = s\mathbf{X}(s) - \mathbf{x}(0)$ and assuming **zero initial conditions** $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{0}$:

$$s\mathbf{X}(s) - \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}(s) + \mathbf{B}U(s)$$

$$s\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}(s) + \mathbf{B}U(s)$$

**Step 2:** Group the $\mathbf{X}(s)$ terms by factoring the matrix $\mathbf{X}(s)$ out to the right (using the distributive property of matrix multiplication):

$$s\mathbf{X}(s) - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{B}U(s)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $\mathbf{X}(s)$. Note that $s\mathbf{X}(s) = s\mathbf{I}\mathbf{X}(s)$, where $\mathbf{I}$ is the $n \times n$ identity matrix. This is a crucial algebraic trick to keep the variable $\mathbf{X}(s)$ factorable:

$$(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{B}U(s)$$

**Step 4:** Pre-multiply both sides by the inverse of $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$. This is valid provided the matrix is non-singular, i.e., its determinant is not zero:

$$\mathbf{X}(s) = (s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}\,U(s)$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Laplace transform to the output equation (ii). With zero initial conditions, there is no transient term to add:

$$Y(s) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X}(s) + D\,U(s)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the expression for $\mathbf{X}(s)$ derived in Step 4 into the output equation:

$$Y(s) = \mathbf{C}\left[(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}\,U(s)\right] + D\,U(s)$$

**Step 7:** Factor out the common term $U(s)$ from the right-hand side (using the associative property of matrix multiplication):

$$Y(s) = \left[\mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D\right] U(s)$$

**Step 8:** Formally define the transfer function as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input:

$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D\,}$$

This completes the rigorous derivation. The entire process is purely algebraic, relying on the assumption of zero initial conditions to suppress the system's natural (unforced) response.

## 3.2 Worked Example: A 2nd-Order SISO System

> **Problem:** Given the state model below, find the transfer function $G(s) = Y(s)/U(s)$ and identify the poles.
>
> $$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

**Step 1:** Construct the matrix $s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}$. The identity matrix is $\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

$$s\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix} s & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

$$s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} s & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the inverse of this $2 \times 2$ matrix. For a matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$, the inverse is $\frac{1}{ad - bc}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}$.

Here, $a = s$, $b = -1$, $c = 2$, $d = s+3$.

Determinant:
$$\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = s(s+3) - (-1)(2) = s^2 + 3s + 2$$

Adjoint matrix:
$$\text{adj}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

Resolvent matrix:
$$(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}\begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the term $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}$. Multiply the resolvent by the input vector $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$:

$$(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}\begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}\begin{bmatrix} (s+3)(0) + (1)(1) \\ (-2)(0) + (s)(1) \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the transfer function using $G(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D$:

$$G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix} + 0$$

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}$$

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}\begin{bmatrix} (1)(1) + (0)(s) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$$

**Step 5:** Factor the denominator to find the poles:

$$s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$

$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)}\,}$$

**Step 6: Pole Identification:** The poles are the roots of the characteristic equation $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0$, giving $s = -1$ and $s = -2$. Both poles lie in the **left-half of the s-plane**, confirming that the system is **BIBO stable**.

## 3.3 Verification via Characteristic Equation

> [!NOTE]
> As a mandatory cross-check, the eigenvalues of the system matrix $\mathbf{A}$ must match the poles of $G(s)$.
>
> Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ are given by $\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0$:
> $$\det\begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 2 & \lambda+3 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda+3) + 2 = \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = (\lambda+1)(\lambda+2) = 0$$
>
> Hence, the eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = -1$ and $\lambda_2 = -2$, which perfectly match the poles of $G(s)$. This consistency is a hallmark of any valid KTU solution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: State Model Representation

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the internal flow of signals within a state-space model, showing how the input $u(t)$, state vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$, and output $y(t)$ interact through the four fundamental matrices $\mathbf{A}$, $\mathbf{B}$, $\mathbf{C}$, and $D$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    U[Input u of t] --> SUM1((+))
    SUM1 --> BBLOCK[Matrix B]
    BBLOCK --> SUM2((+))
    SUM2 --> INT[Integrator s inverse]
    INT --> X[State x of t]
    X --> ABO[Matrix A]
    ABO --> SUM2
    X --> CBLK[Matrix C]
    CBLK --> SUM3((+))
    U --> DBLK[Matrix D]
    DBLK --> SUM3
    SUM3 --> Y[Output y of t]
    X --> OUTSTATE[State Feedback Path]
    OUTSTATE --> ABO
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Converting State Model to Transfer Function

This block-level flowchart maps the **logical sequence of operations** a student must follow when solving a numerical conversion problem. It acts as an algorithmic checklist.

```mermaid
graph TD
    START([Start: Given A, B, C, D]) --> S1[Step 1: Form sI minus A matrix]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Compute the determinant of sI minus A]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute the Adjoint of sI minus A]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Form Resolvent Matrix as Adj divided by Det]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Multiply Resolvent by B vector]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Multiply Result by C row vector]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Add scalar D to obtain G of s]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Factor and Simplify numerator and denominator]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Identify poles from denominator roots]
    S9 --> CHECK{Poles match Eigenvalues of A?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| DONE([Valid Transfer Function G of s])
    CHECK -->|No| ERROR[Revisit Matrix Algebra Steps]
    ERROR --> S1
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping: Internal vs. External Description

This diagram contrasts the two equivalent mathematical descriptions of the same LTI system, reinforcing the core intuition that both representations describe identical system dynamics, but from different analytical viewpoints.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Internal_Description ["Internal Description: State Model"]
        ID1[State Equation dot x equals Ax plus Bu]
        ID2[Output Equation y equals Cx plus Du]
        ID1 --> ID2
    end

    subgraph Bridge_Transform ["Laplace Domain Bridge"]
        BT1[Zero Initial Conditions x0 equals zero]
        BT2[Resolvent Matrix sI minus A inverse]
    end

    subgraph External_Description ["External Description: Transfer Function"]
        ED1[G of s equals C times sI minus A inverse times B plus D]
        ED2[Poles: roots of det sI minus A]
    end

    ID2 --> BT1
    BT1 --> BT2
    BT2 --> ED1
    ED1 --> ED2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define the transfer function of a system from its state-space representation. State the formula for the same.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The transfer function of an LTI system expressed in state-space form is defined as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output $Y(s)$ to the Laplace transform of the input $U(s)$, assuming **zero initial conditions**.
>
> Given the state model $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}u(t)$ and $y(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,u(t)$, the transfer function is given by:
>
> $$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D\,}$$
>
> This formula relates the classical (external) description to the modern (internal) description of the system, providing a bridge between time-domain and frequency-domain analysis.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]`
**What is meant by the resolvent matrix? What is its significance in the state-space to transfer function conversion?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Resolvent Matrix** of an LTI system is formally defined as the Laplace transform of the state-transition matrix $\boldsymbol{\phi}(t)$. Mathematically, it is expressed as:
>
> $$\boldsymbol{\Phi}(s) = (s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}$$
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. It acts as a **transfer function for the state vector itself**, mapping the input $U(s)$ to the state $\mathbf{X}(s)$ via $\mathbf{X}(s) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}(s)\mathbf{B}\,U(s)$.
> 2. Its **denominator** $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$ gives the **characteristic polynomial** of the system, whose roots are the natural frequencies (eigenvalues) of the system $\mathbf{A}$.
> 3. It is the **core building block** of the master formula $G(s) = \mathbf{C}\boldsymbol{\Phi}(s)\mathbf{B} + D$.

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Identity Matrix:** A common, heavily penalized error is writing $s - \mathbf{A}$ instead of $s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}$. The scalar $s$ cannot subtract from a matrix; the $s$ must be multiplied by the identity matrix $\mathbf{I}$ first.
> 2. **Inversion Order:** Matrix multiplication is **not commutative**. Always pre-multiply by $\mathbf{C}$ and post-multiply by $\mathbf{B}$: $\mathbf{C}[\dots]\mathbf{B}$. Reversing this order will produce the wrong answer.
> 3. **Assuming Non-Zero Initial Conditions:** If the question provides $\mathbf{x}(0) \neq \mathbf{0}$, the transfer function methodology is invalid. You must use the time-domain solution $\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{\mathbf{A}t}\mathbf{x}(0) + \int e^{\mathbf{A}(t-\tau)}\mathbf{B}u(\tau)\,d\tau$ instead.
> 4. **Skipping the Determinant:** The KTU 2024 scheme awards marks for explicitly writing $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$. Skipping this step often results in 1-2 mark deductions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the transfer function of an LTI system from its state-space representation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the state model given below, determine the transfer function $G(s)$ and verify the stability of the system. **(7 Marks)**

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -8 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) - Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the standard state model (1 Mark):
$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}u(t) \quad \text{...(i)}$$
$$y(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,u(t) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Laplace transform with **zero initial conditions** $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{0}$ (1 Mark):
$$s\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}(s) + \mathbf{B}U(s)$$

**Step 3:** Group $\mathbf{X}(s)$ terms by factoring out, remembering that $s\mathbf{X}(s) = s\mathbf{I}\mathbf{X}(s)$ (1 Mark):
$$(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{B}U(s)$$

**Step 4:** Pre-multiply by the inverse to solve for $\mathbf{X}(s)$ (1 Mark):
$$\mathbf{X}(s) = (s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}\,U(s)$$

**Step 5:** Apply Laplace to output equation (1 Mark):
$$Y(s) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X}(s) + D\,U(s)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute and form the transfer function $G(s) = Y(s)/U(s)$ (2 Marks):
$$Y(s) = \left[\mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D\right] U(s)$$
$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D\,}$$

---

**Part (b) - Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Form $s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}$ (1 Mark):
$$s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 8 & s+6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute determinant and adjoint (2 Marks):
$$\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = s(s+6) + 8 = s^2 + 6s + 8$$
$$\text{adj}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \begin{bmatrix} s+6 & 1 \\ -8 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}$ (2 Marks):
$$(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 6s + 8}\begin{bmatrix} s+6 & 1 \\ -8 & s \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 6s + 8}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $G(s) = \mathbf{C}(\cdot)\mathbf{B} + D$ (1 Mark):
$$G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{s^2 + 6s + 8}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix} = \frac{s+1}{s^2 + 6s + 8}$$

**Step 5:** Factor and check stability (1 Mark):
$$s^2 + 6s + 8 = (s+2)(s+4)$$
Poles are at $s = -2$ and $s = -4$, **both in the left half-plane**, so the system is **stable**.

$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{s+1}{(s+2)(s+4)}\,}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the significance of the characteristic equation in the state-space representation. How is it related to the transfer function? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system is described by the state equations:
$$\dot{x}_1 = -3x_1 + x_2 + u$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -2x_1 - 4x_2 + u$$
$$y = 2x_1 + x_2$$
Determine the transfer function $G(s)$ and its poles. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) - Theory (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Define the characteristic equation (2 Marks):
The characteristic equation of a state-space system is the polynomial obtained by setting the determinant of $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$ to zero. It encapsulates the **intrinsic natural dynamics** of the system, independent of the input or output matrices.

$$\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0$$

**Step 2:** Relationship to eigenvalues (2 Marks):
The roots of this polynomial are the **eigenvalues** of the system matrix $\mathbf{A}$. They represent the natural unforced modes of the system's response.

**Step 3:** Connection to the transfer function (2 Marks):
Since $G(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D$, and $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1} = \frac{\text{adj}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})}{\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})}$, the **denominator of the transfer function** (the characteristic polynomial of $G(s)$) is a **factor** of $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$. Thus, the poles of the transfer function are a subset of the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ (specifically, those eigenvalues that are both controllable and observable).

**Step 4:** Engineering significance (1 Mark):
The characteristic equation determines **system stability** (via Routh-Hurwitz or pole locations), **time constants**, and **natural frequencies of oscillation**.

---

**Part (b) - Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Construct the matrices from the given equations (1 Mark):
$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ -2 & -4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

**Step 2:** Form $s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}$ (1 Mark):
$$s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & -1 \\ 2 & s+4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute determinant and adjoint (1 Mark):
$$\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = (s+3)(s+4) - (-1)(2) = s^2 + 7s + 12 + 2 = s^2 + 7s + 14$$
$$\text{adj}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \begin{bmatrix} s+4 & 1 \\ -2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}$ (2 Marks):
$$(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 7s + 14}\begin{bmatrix} s+4 & 1 \\ -2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$= \frac{1}{s^2 + 7s + 14}\begin{bmatrix} s+5 \\ s+1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $G(s)$ (1 Mark):
$$G(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{s^2 + 7s + 14}\begin{bmatrix} s+5 \\ s+1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$= \frac{2(s+5) + (s+1)}{s^2 + 7s + 14} = \frac{3s + 11}{s^2 + 7s + 14}$$

**Step 6:** Identify the poles (1 Mark):
Poles are the roots of $s^2 + 7s + 14 = 0$:
$$s = \frac{-7 \pm \sqrt{49 - 56}}{2} = \frac{-7 \pm \sqrt{-7}}{2} = -3.5 \pm j1.32$$

$$\boxed{\,G(s) = \frac{3s+11}{s^2+7s+14}, \quad \text{Poles: } s = -3.5 \pm j1.32\,}$$

The system is **stable** since the real parts of the poles are negative.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for State Model to Transfer Function Conversion:**

- **Master Formula:** The single most important equation is $G(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D$. Memorize it verbatim.
- **Identity Matrix is Mandatory:** Never write $(s - \mathbf{A})^{-1}$; always write $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}$.
- **Zero Initial Conditions:** Transfer functions are defined *only* for systems initially at rest ($\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{0}$). Do not apply this method to non-zero initial condition problems.
- **Order of Multiplication:** Strictly follow $\mathbf{C} \to (s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1} \to \mathbf{B}$. Reversing this order is mathematically incorrect.
- **Resolvent Matrix:** The term $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}$ is called the Resolvent Matrix, often written as $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(s)$.
- **Characteristic Equation:** The denominator of the transfer function (its poles) is found by solving $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0$.
- **Pole-Eigenvalue Link:** For a completely controllable and observable system, the poles of $G(s)$ are *exactly* the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$.
- **2x2 Shortcut:** For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$, the inverse of $(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$ is $\frac{1}{(s-a)(s-d) - bc}\begin{bmatrix} s-d & -b \\ -c & s-a \end{bmatrix}$.
- **Stability Check:** Compute the poles; if *all* lie in the open left-half of the s-plane (negative real parts), the system is BIBO stable.
- **Dimension Check:** $\mathbf{A}$ must be square ($n \times n$). $\mathbf{B}$ must have $n$ rows. $\mathbf{C}$ must have $n$ columns.
- **Direct Feed-through $D$:** For a strictly proper transfer function, $D = 0$. If $D \neq 0$, simply add it as a scalar to the result of $\mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}$.
- **Verification Habit:** Always cross-verify the poles of $G(s)$ with the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ as a final sanity check before concluding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Transition Matrix: Properties of State Transition Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT521 - CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** State Transition Matrix: Properties of State Transition Matrix # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **state-space representation** of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) continuous-time system governed by the vector-matrix differential equation: $$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$$ the **State Transition Matrix (STM)**, denoted $\Phi(t)$ (or $\phi(t)$), is defined as the unique $n \times n$ matrix that maps the initial state $x(t_0)$ to the state $x(t)$ at any later time $t$, under the condition of **zero input** ($u(t) = 0$). Mathematically, the homogeneous solution to the state equation gives: $$x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0) = e^{At} x(0)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** The State Transition Matrix $\Phi(t)$ is the matrix exponential of the system matrix $A$, given by $\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$. It completely characterizes the **free (unforced) response** of the system. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Think of the State Transition Matrix as a **"mathematical GPS tracker for system states"** that tells you exactly where every internal variable of a system will be after time $t$, **assuming no external push is applied**. > **Real-World Analogy — The Bouncing Ball:** Imagine dropping a ball from a height in a vacuum chamber (no air resistance, no input forces). If you know its initial position $x(0)$ and initial velocity $\dot{x}(0)$, can you predict its position at any future time $t$? **Yes!** The equation $x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0)$ does exactly this. The matrix $\Phi(t)$ encodes the physics (gravity, in this case, embedded in the system matrix $A$) and translates initial conditions forward in time. In control systems, $\Phi(t)$ is the **"memory of the system's natural behavior"** — it captures how the system evolves on its own, without any external control action. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The STM is **NOT** a controller or observer. It is purely a **descriptor of system dynamics** in the absence of inputs. The forced response is added separately as $\int_0^t \Phi(t-\tau) B u(\tau) d\tau$. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notations | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit / Dimension | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\Phi(t)$ | State Transition Matrix | Dimensionless $n \times n$ matrix | | $A$ | System Matrix | $n \times n$ (units depend on physical system) | | $e^{At}$ | Matrix Exponential | Dimensionless $n \times n$ matrix | | $\Lambda$ | Eigenvalue Matrix | Diagonal $n \times n$ matrix | | $\mathcal{L}$ | Laplace Operator | Transforms $t \to s$ | | $I$ | Identity Matrix | Dimensionless $n \times n$ matrix | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Meaning of $\Phi(t)$ The State Transition Matrix is obtained by solving the homogeneous equation $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t)$ with initial condition $x(0)$. The general solution in scalar form is $x(t) = e^{At} x(0)$. Extending this concept to the matrix (vector) case yields: $$\Phi(t) = e^{At} = I + At + \frac{A^2 t^2}{2!} + \frac{A^3 t^3}{3!} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k t^k}{k!}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a matrix exponential?** Because $A$ is a matrix, we cannot write $e^{At}$ as a simple exponential. Instead, we use the **power series definition** (analogous to the Taylor series of $e^x$ but with matrix arguments) or the **Laplace transform method**: $\Phi(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$. ## 2.2 The Six Cardinal Properties of the State Transition Matrix The properties of $\Phi(t)$ are **highly testable** in KTU examinations. Each property carries significant mark weight. ### **Property 1: Initial Condition Identity** $$\boxed{\Phi(0) = I}$$ **Why?** Substituting $t = 0$ into the matrix exponential series: $$\Phi(0) = I + A(0) + \frac{A^2(0)^2}{2!} + \dots = I$$ **Physical Meaning:** At time $t = 0$, the system is in its initial state, and the transition matrix must act as the identity (no transformation). ### **Property 2: Semigroup Property (Transition Composition)** $$\boxed{\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1) \cdot \Phi(t_2) = \Phi(t_2) \cdot \Phi(t_1)}$$ **Why?** The exponential function satisfies $e^{A(t_1+t_2)} = e^{At_1} \cdot e^{At_2} = e^{At_2} \cdot e^{At_1}$ (commutativity holds because $A$ commutes with itself). **Physical Meaning:** Evolving the system from $0 \to t_1 \to (t_1+t_2)$ is equivalent to evolving directly from $0 \to (t_1+t_2)$. ### **Property 3: Inverse Property (Backward Transition)** $$\boxed{\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)}$$ **Derivation:** Using the semigroup property: $$\Phi(t) \cdot \Phi(-t) = \Phi(t - t) = \Phi(0) = I$$ Therefore, $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$. **Physical Meaning:** To "undo" the evolution from $0$ to $t$, simply evolve backward by $t$. This property is crucial in **optimal control** and **state observer design** (e.g., the Kalman filter). ### **Property 4: Differential Equation Property** $$\boxed{\frac{d}{dt} \Phi(t) = A \, \Phi(t) = \Phi(t) \, A}$$ **Why?** Differentiating the power series term-by-term: $$\frac{d}{dt} \Phi(t) = A + A^2 t + \frac{A^3 t^2}{2!} + \dots = A \left[ I + At + \frac{A^2 t^2}{2!} + \dots \right] = A \Phi(t)$$ Since $A$ commutes with $A^k$ for all $k$, the right-multiplication form also holds. ### **Property 5: Eigenvalue Spectral Mapping** $$\boxed{\Phi(t) = V \, e^{\Lambda t} \, V^{-1}}$$ where $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_n)$ contains the eigenvalues of $A$, and $V$ is the modal matrix (matrix of eigenvectors). **Physical Meaning:** Each eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ of $A$ maps to an exponential mode $e^{\lambda_i t}$ in $\Phi(t)$. The system is **stable** if and only if $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for all $i$ (which is equivalent to $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Phi(t) = 0$). ### **Property 6: Disturbance-Free Forced Response** $$\boxed{x(t) = \Phi(t) x(0) + \int_0^t \Phi(t - \tau) B u(\tau) \, d\tau}$$ **Why?** This is the **complete solution** (superposition of free and forced responses). The convolution integral represents the contribution of the input $u(\tau)$ acting from $\tau = 0$ to $\tau = t$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Property | Formula | Test Frequency | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Definition | $\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\}$ | ★★★★★ | | Identity at Zero | $\Phi(0) = I$ | ★★★★★ | | Semigroup | $\Phi(t_1+t_2) = \Phi(t_1)\Phi(t_2)$ | ★★★★★ | | Inverse | $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$ | ★★★★ | | Derivative | $\dot{\Phi}(t) = A\Phi(t) = \Phi(t)A$ | ★★★★★ | | Spectral Form | $\Phi(t) = V e^{\Lambda t} V^{-1}$ | ★★★★ | | Complete Solution | $x(t) = \Phi(t)x(0) + \int_0^t \Phi(t-\tau)B u(\tau) d\tau$ | ★★★★★ | | Stability | $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Phi(t) = 0 \iff \text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0 \ \forall i$ | ★★★★ | | Power Series | $\Phi(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!}$ | ★★★ | | Determinant | $\det[\Phi(t)] = e^{\text{tr}(A) \cdot t}$ | ★★★ | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The State Transition Matrix is the cornerstone of: 1. **Spacecraft Attitude Control (NASA/ISRO):** Predicting satellite orientation over decades — $\Phi(t)$ governs the rotational dynamics. 2. **Robotic Manipulators:** Computing joint trajectories from initial conditions in articulated arm dynamics. 3. **Power System Stability:** Analyzing transient stability of multi-machine grids. 4. **Kalman Filtering:** $\Phi(t)$ is the state-prediction kernel in the Kalman filter recursion. 5. **Chemical Process Control:** Tracking concentration states in CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactors). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: STM from First-Principles (Power Series Method) **Given:** Homogeneous state equation $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t)$ with $x(0) = x_0$. **Step 1 — Assume a Power Series Solution:** We postulate that the solution takes the form: $$x(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} a_k t^k$$ where each $a_k$ is an $n \times 1$ constant vector (or $n \times n$ matrix for STM derivation). **Step 2 — Apply the Initial Condition $x(0) = x_0$:** At $t = 0$, all terms with $t^k$ for $k \geq 1$ vanish: $$x(0) = a_0 = x_0$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate and Substitute into the State Equation:** $$\dot{x}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2 + \dots = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k a_k t^{k-1}$$ The state equation $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t)$ gives: $$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k a_k t^{k-1} = A \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} a_k t^k$$ **Step 4 — Re-index the LHS Sum:** Let $j = k - 1$, so $k = j + 1$: $$\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (j+1) a_{j+1} t^{j} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} A a_k t^k$$ **Step 5 — Equate Coefficients of Like Powers of $t$:** For $t^0$ (i.e., $k=0$): $a_1 = A a_0 = A x_0$ For $t^1$ (i.e., $k=1$): $2 a_2 = A a_1 \implies a_2 = \frac{A^2 x_0}{2!}$ For $t^2$: $3 a_3 = A a_2 \implies a_3 = \frac{A^3 x_0}{3!}$ Generalizing: $a_k = \frac{A^k x_0}{k!}$ **Step 6 — Assemble the Solution:** $$x(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k t^k}{k!} x_0 = \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!} \right] x_0$$ **Step 7 — Identify the State Transition Matrix:** $$\boxed{\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At)^k}{k!}}$$ This is the **matrix exponential** — a strict generalization of $e^{at} = \sum \frac{(at)^k}{k!}$ to the matrix case. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: STM via Laplace Transform Method **Step 1 — Take the Laplace Transform of the Homogeneous Equation:** $$\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = \mathcal{L}\{A x(t)\}$$ $$sX(s) - x(0) = A X(s)$$ **Step 2 — Isolate $X(s)$:** $$sX(s) - A X(s) = x(0)$$ $$(sI - A) X(s) = x(0)$$ **Step 3 — Pre-multiply by $(sI - A)^{-1}$ (assuming it exists, which requires $\det(sI-A) \neq 0$):** $$X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} x(0)$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Inverse Laplace Transform:** $$x(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\} \, x(0)$$ **Step 5 — Identify the STM:** $$\boxed{\Phi(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}}$$ This is the **preferred KTU exam method** because it avoids infinite series and uses partial fractions. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Proof of the Semigroup Property **Given:** $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$. **To Prove:** $\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2)$. **Step 1 — Write out the power series for both sides.** Left-hand side: $$\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{[A(t_1+t_2)]^k}{k!}$$ Right-hand side: $$\Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2) = \left[ \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At_1)^m}{m!} \right] \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(At_2)^n}{n!} \right]$$ **Step 2 — Expand the product using the Cauchy product rule:** $$\Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left[ \sum_{m=0}^{k} \frac{A^m t_1^m}{m!} \cdot \frac{A^{k-m} t_2^{k-m}}{(k-m)!} \right]$$ **Step 3 — Use the commutativity of $A$ (since $A \cdot A = A \cdot A$):** $$= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k}{k!} \sum_{m=0}^{k} \binom{k}{m} t_1^m t_2^{k-m}$$ **Step 4 — Recognize the binomial expansion:** $$\sum_{m=0}^{k} \binom{k}{m} t_1^m t_2^{k-m} = (t_1 + t_2)^k$$ **Step 5 — Final Result:** $$\Phi(t_1) \Phi(t_2) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{A^k (t_1 + t_2)^k}{k!} = \Phi(t_1 + t_2) \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Worked Example — Computing $\Phi(t)$ for a 2×2 System **Given System Matrix:** $$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 1 — Compute $(sI - A)$:** $$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Determinant:** $$\det(sI - A) = s(s+3) - (-1)(2) = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Adjoint Matrix:** $$\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Form the Inverse:** $$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5 — Partial Fraction Decomposition (element-wise):** For element $(1,1)$: $\frac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$ For element $(1,2)$: $\frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{1}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$ For element $(2,1)$: $\frac{-2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-2}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}$ For element $(2,2)$: $\frac{s}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-1}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}$ **Step 6 — Apply the Inverse Laplace Transform:** $$\Phi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 7 — Verification using Property $\Phi(0) = I$:** $$\Phi(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2-1 & 1-1 \\ -2+2 & -1+2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 8 — Stability Check:** Both eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = -1$ and $\lambda_2 = -2$, both with negative real parts. Therefore, the system is **asymptotically stable**, and $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Phi(t) = 0$. ✓ # 4.
